text: As a young physicist living in a quiet suburb, I, myself, kept a deep curiosity about how the ordinary world works. Every morning I would go upstairs to my small lab, switch on the radio, and study a condenser, watching the current flow and the way conduction and induction shaped each particle of charge. The work was challenging, yet tranquil, and I cherish it; I never disclose my methods carelessly. I would polish the glass until it began to glitter, take a sample of dust, and locate each red flake and speck of soot under the lens. My old dog would wag his tail and watch with me, as if he too felt the itch of inquiry. In accordance with an old habit, I kept a thick volume of notes, its pages an abstraction of every consequence I could imagine. A beetle once crawled across my passport on the desk, and I, the grateful recipient of so much quiet wonder, simply smiled. People in retail and sociology might call such labor irrelevant, but to me it felt everlasting and relevant to existence itself. I refused to be disinclined or snobbish about practical things; I had to require myself to keep an open conscience. A neighbor, a programer who once worked offshore, would invite me for tea, and we would mention the news: a debate at the United Nations, the abolition of an unfair tax, an exclusive deal in green energy, and the expulsion of a corrupt official whose bloody schemes had begun to stink. Provided the weather held, I would walk down to a triangle of parkland near my lodging, where a bronze puppet show drew a crowd. I took an oath to myself that I would never let exorbitant ambition mar my calm. When a sudden avalanche of bad news arrived, I felt perturbed, but I would not go mad. I tried to taste each moment, to pull strength from small things, and to commend the graceful courage of friends. The prince of patience, my mentor used to say, is the person who can multiply small efforts into a maximum result. His affirmation, met with unanimous nods, was that a clear conclusion must precede any action. He warned that a single token gesture, a silly bundle of excuses, or a careless tick of the clock can blow away years of work. So I would appreciate the upkeep of habit, sit by the grate, and let the warmth seep into my skin, knowing the resistance of doubt would ensue but never win. I wrote a prescription for myself: stay curious, stay kind. Even when ill, I disclosed this much: that spiritual operation, the daily relay of hope across the silicon age, is what keeps a tough heart beating during a long pregnancy of ideas, and a chap can ask no more.
translation: 作为一名住在安静郊区的年轻物理学家，我自己对普通世界如何运作始终怀有深深的好奇心。每天早晨我都会上楼来到我的小实验室，打开收音机，研究一只冷凝器，观察电流的流动，以及传导和感应如何塑造每一个电荷粒子。这工作富有挑战性，却也宁静，我十分珍惜它。我会把玻璃擦到闪闪发光，取一份灰尘样本，在镜头下找出每一片红色的薄片和煤烟的斑点。我那条老狗会摇着尾巴和我一起观看，仿佛它也感到了探究的渴望。按照一个老习惯，我保留着一大卷笔记，那些书页是我所能想象到的每一个后果的抽象。一只甲虫曾爬过桌上我的护照，而作为如此多静谧奇迹的感恩接受者，我只是微笑。零售业和社会学界的人也许会说这样的劳作毫不相关，但对我而言，它感觉永恒，并且与存在本身息息相关。我拒绝变得不情愿或势利地看待实际的事；我必须要求自己保持一颗坦荡的良心。一位邻居，一个曾在海上工作的程序员，会邀请我喝茶，我们会谈起新闻：联合国的一场辩论、废除一项不公平税收、一桩绿色能源的独家交易，以及驱逐一名腐败官员，他那些血腥的阴谋已开始发臭。只要天气允许，我就会走到住所附近一片三角形的公园绿地，那里有青铜木偶戏吸引着人群。我向自己发誓，绝不让过度的野心损害我的平静。当突如其来的坏消息如雪崩般涌来时，我感到烦躁不安，但我不会发疯。我努力品味每一刻，从微小的事物中汲取力量，并赞美朋友们优雅的勇气。我的导师常说，耐心的王子，就是那个能把微小努力相乘成最大成果的人。他的论断得到一致点头认可：清晰的结论必须先于任何行动。他警告说，一个象征性的姿态、一堆愚蠢的借口，或钟表漫不经心的一声滴答，都可能吹走多年的心血。于是我会珍视习惯的维持，坐在炉边，让温暖渗入我的皮肤，明白怀疑的阻力会随之而来，却永远不会获胜。我为自己写下一张处方：保持好奇，保持善良。即使生病，我也透露这一点：那种精神上的运转，那种在硅时代里希望的每日接力，正是让一颗坚韧的心在漫长的思想孕育期里持续跳动的东西，而一个普通人别无所求。

text: Supposing you ever visit the lowland town where I grew up, seek out the old adviser who once served the cabinet of the regional council. He was a wise practitioner of public life, a man of rare virtue, and he could numerate every family living within the perimeter of the valley. He would speak respectfully to each citizen, never trite, never proud, and he refused any abasement of his office. People said his judgment was precisely balanced, like a fine differential gear, and that he could exonerate the innocent and restrain the guilty with equal calm. The town had known real adversity. A bomb scare one chilly weekend had nearly crippled the local economy, and a long shortage of work left many jobless. The constabulary, stretched thin, leaned on him for recommendation and guidance. He did not panic; he simply built a clear guideline, a numerical plan that anyone could read in a book, and he would constantly remind us, "Do not let fear reign." By analogy, he said, a town is like a body: suppress one part and the whole will recede. His underlying belief was that good order is no mundane achievement but a daily practice. I remember the day a wealthy nominee from downtown arrived, hoping to manipulate the vote and claim a kind of sovereignty over our affairs. He was charming on the surface, with a fragrant cologne and a purple silk tie, yet wary eyes. He tried to tug the council toward his obsession with profit, hinting that he could afford whatever he liked. The adviser, disposed to fairness, was not impressed. He would not swear an oath he could not keep, and he warned the man that greed afflicts a community like an overdose afflicts a patient. "Your suggestion has nil merit," he said, to the astonishment of the room. The nominee, embarrassed, soon left. That evening, over a pot of tea poured from an old teapot, the adviser told me an aspect of his philosophy that I still cherish. Life, he explained, is heterogeneous and variegated; it cannot be reduced to one tie-up of interests. Some days are warm and others chilly; some are salty with grief. He spoke of a friend whose fracture of trust had never healed, and of a bridegroom whose joy, though tolerable to envy, was real. He believed every emotion, even the embarrassing ones, deserves a tolerable place. Then he showed me his diary, where each entry was a small display of honest thought, like an advertisement for decency. He told me to climb steadily, to make a reservation in my heart for kindness, and to apply a clean dressing of lint to every wound. As the glacier of years recedes, I think of him often. His example, met with promptly given respect, taught me that to compete well is to compete fairly, and that suppression of the weak is the opposite of strength. Even a grandchild of his still keeps that lesson, spraying it forward like fresh water on dry ground, a breakthrough of memory that no adversity can erase. It would astonish you how social intercourse and a simple exam of the books, especially under the law, can restore balance. The adviser, awfully tired, chose to delegate tasks rather than damn the staff or let stress afflict and cripple morale. He cut his caffeine intake, booked a massage, and avoided the rigid hierarchy. Inevitably, with countable wins and a plastic spray of optimism, the town recovered.
translation: 假如你有朝一日造访我长大的那座低地小镇，去找那位曾为区议会内阁服务的老顾问吧。他是公共生活中一位睿智的从业者，一个拥有罕见美德的人，他能数出住在山谷周界以内的每一户人家。他对每位市民都恭敬地说话，从不陈腐，也从不骄傲，他拒绝让自己的职位有任何屈尊降格。人们说他的判断精确而平衡，像一台精密的差速齿轮，他能以同样的镇定为无辜者昭雪、约束有罪者。这座小镇曾经历真正的逆境。一个寒冷周末的炸弹惊吓几乎使当地经济瘫痪，长期的工作短缺让许多人失业。警力捉襟见肘，便依靠他来获取建议和指引。他没有惊慌；他只是制定了一份清晰的方针，一个任何人都能在书里读懂的数字计划，并不断提醒我们：“别让恐惧统治一切。”他用类比说，小镇就像一个身体：压制其中一部分，整体就会衰退。他内在的信念是，良好的秩序绝非平凡的成就，而是一种日常的实践。我记得那天，一位来自市中心的富有候选人到来，希望操纵选票，声称要对我们的事务拥有某种主权。他表面上很迷人，带着芬芳的古龙水和一条紫色丝绸领带，眼神却充满戒备。他试图把议会拉向他对利润的痴迷，暗示他想要什么都买得起。这位倾向公正的顾问并不为所动。他不会发一个自己无法信守的誓，他警告那人，贪婪折磨一个社区，正如用药过量折磨一个病人。“你的提议毫无价值，”他说，满座皆惊。那候选人尴尬不已，很快便离开了。那天晚上，就着一只旧茶壶倒出的茶，顾问告诉了我一个我至今珍视的他的哲学侧面。他解释说，生活是异质而斑驳的；它无法被简化为一种利益的捆绑。有些日子温暖，有些寒冷；有些咸涩着悲伤。他谈起一位朋友，其信任的裂痕从未愈合，也谈起一位新郎，其喜悦虽令人嫉妒却真实可容。他相信每一种情感，哪怕是令人尴尬的，都该有一个可容许的位置。然后他给我看他的日记，每一篇都是诚实思想的小小展示，像一则关于体面的广告。他叫我稳步攀登，在心里为善良留一份预订，并给每一道伤口敷上干净的软麻布。随着岁月这条冰河的退去，我常常想起他。他的榜样，连同迅速给予的尊敬，教会我：要好好竞争，就是要公平地竞争，而压制弱者正是力量的反面。就连他的一个孙辈也仍记着这一课，把它像清水洒在干涸土地上那样向前传递，一种逆境无法抹去的记忆的突破。你会惊讶于社会交往和对账目的一次检查，尤其是在法律之下，竟能恢复平衡。那位顾问累得要命，选择把任务委派出去，而不是责骂员工或让压力折磨并削弱士气。他减少了咖啡因的摄入，预约了按摩，避开僵化的等级制度。最终，凭着可数的胜利和一抹塑料般的乐观，小镇得以复苏。

theme: A Team Studies the Changing Beach
text: Our research team spent one bright Saturday morning on a windswept beach, studying the environmental forces that shape the coast. The work was challenging, but the satisfaction was tremendous. We measured the diameter of eroded stones, charted the dimension and texture of the sand, and recorded the fluctuation of the tide with similar care to the way a banker tracks money. Our monitor blinked on the screen as a turbine offshore turned in the wind, its power feeding the village nearby. None of us was idle. The structure of the cliff, weary with age and decrepit in places, told a long story of nature's patience. A patch of bright fleece-like moss clung to the bronze-colored rock, and the fur of a curious seal glistened in the spray. I am similar in temperament to my colleagues: rarely uninterested, always eager to comment and seek the truth. We did not quarrel, though we did debate. One member, a seasoned statistician, advocated a cautious prediction; he disliked any guess that lacked proof. Another, more cynical, made a sarcastic remark and seemed almost hateful of optimism, but his firmness kept us honest. There was a tacit, demonstrative trust between us, an equivalence of respect that no establishment could mandate. We knew our employment depended on accuracy, so we sought to procure the most efficient instruments money could buy. By noon the boil of activity had eased. We paused near an old monument, a stone tablet erected by an Indian colony and an African trader who once met here to forge a trade route. The history of this outlet to the sea is rich; ships once paid a heavy fare to anchor, and a single seller could grow wealthy overnight. Now a scenic villa stands where the forge once roared, and tourists arrive by ferry to listen to the waves. I felt a tasteful satisfaction in the contrast. We did not let the cinema of the past distract us, yet it would be regrettable to ignore it. As an individual, I find that history and science alternate in my mind like the tide. Our employer, a member of parliament with a famous prejudice against waste, would not veto a satisfactory report, but he had warned us that any implicit error could ruin the project. So we worked to assimilate every fact, to fill each bucket of data, and to nail down conclusions firmly. The pedal of the survey cart squeaked as we pushed it along; my forehead was damp; my hands, scrubbed with shampoo that morning, were now gritty. Still, the cosmopolitan crowd of gulls overhead seemed to cease their cries in approval. By evening we walked the lace of the shoreline back toward dinner, weary but content, having watched vapor rise from the warm flats and a single crane wade through the marsh. The division of labor had worked. A great power, I realized, lies not in any one tremendous discovery but in the steady, greatly shared effort of a team that refuses to disappoint itself. Suppose, after a big game of badminton, the team gathered for a briefing that felt almost like a ritual or a sermon. One advocate would dislike the seductive but ostensible promises of easy profit; such talk could exasperate anyone. They discussed a paternity case, a pregnant pause in trade as the firm planned to reexport goods, and even the parking crisis, before each encounter ended in agreement.
translation: 我们的研究小组在一个明亮的星期六上午，在一片狂风拂过的海滩上度过，研究塑造海岸的种种环境力量。这项工作富有挑战性，但带来的满足感是巨大的。我们测量被侵蚀石块的直径，绘制沙子的大小尺度与纹理，并以银行家追踪钱财般的同样细致记录潮汐的波动。当一台离岸的涡轮机在风中转动、为附近村庄供电时，我们屏幕上的监视器闪烁着。我们没有人闲着。那悬崖的结构因年迈而疲惫，有些地方已经衰朽，讲述着大自然耐心的漫长故事。一片明亮、似羊毛的苔藓紧贴在青铜色的岩石上，一只好奇海豹的毛皮在浪花飞沫中闪光。我和同事们性情相似：很少不感兴趣，总是热切地评论、探求真相。我们没有争吵，尽管确实辩论过。一位经验丰富的统计员主张谨慎的预测；他厌恶任何缺乏证据的猜测。另一位更愤世嫉俗，说了句讽刺的话，似乎几乎憎恶乐观，但他的坚定让我们保持诚实。我们之间有一种心照不宣的、外露的信任，一种任何机构都无法强制规定的对等尊重。我们知道自己的工作取决于准确，因此设法采购金钱能买到的最高效的仪器。到了中午，活动的沸腾平息下来。我们在一座古老的纪念碑旁停下，那是一块石碑，由一个印度殖民地和一位非洲商人竖立，他们曾在此相遇以开辟一条贸易路线。这个通向大海的出口历史丰富；昔日船只要付高昂的船费才能停泊，一个卖家可能一夜暴富。如今铁匠铺曾轰鸣之处矗立着一座风景如画的别墅，游客乘渡船前来聆听海浪。我在这种对比中感到一种高雅的满足。我们没有让过去的影像分散注意，但忽视它也将令人遗憾。作为个体，我发现历史与科学在我脑中如潮汐般交替。我们的雇主，一位以厌恶浪费而著称、怀有偏见的国会议员，不会否决一份令人满意的报告，但他警告我们，任何隐含的错误都可能毁掉这个项目。于是我们努力吸收每一个事实，装满每一桶数据，并把结论牢牢钉死。勘测推车的踏板在我们推动时吱呀作响；我的额头湿了；我那天早上用洗发水搓洗过的双手，如今沾满沙砾。然而头顶上那群世界各地飞来的海鸥，似乎停止了鸣叫以示赞许。傍晚时分，我们沿着海岸线的花边走回去吃晚饭，疲惫却满足，看着温暖的滩涂升起水汽，一只鹤独自涉过沼泽。分工奏效了。我意识到，伟大的力量不在于任何一项巨大的发现，而在于一个拒绝让自己失望的团队稳定而广泛共享的努力。假设在一场盛大的羽毛球比赛之后，球队聚在一起开了个简报会，那气氛几乎像一场仪式或布道。一位倡导者会厌恶那些诱人却表面的轻松获利承诺；这种话能激怒任何人。他们讨论了一桩亲子关系案件、贸易中怀孕般的停顿（公司计划把货物再出口），甚至还有停车难题，最后每一次交锋都以一致同意收场。

text: It would be no exaggeration to say that my journey to Scotland was the most risky trip of my life, yet I would not trade it for anything. I had read an entire encyclopaedia entry about the region, written in such illegible old handwriting that I nearly gave up; but a single scenic paragraph about its barren moors and ancient empire of clans persuaded me to move. I packed light: a diary, a bottle of cheap perfume, a complimentary guidebook the agency gave me gratis, and an adequate supply of food. No reader could call me studious, but I do my homework. The voyage began on a crowded ferry. The captain, a civil man with a strong jaw, gave us a brief and reassuring address. "There is no menace today," he said, though static crackled on the radio and a sudden squall made the deck rotate beneath us. A van full of crates had to be lashed down with a jack and rope so it would not slide into the ditch of the lower hold. I felt my impulse to panic, but I would not plead for an early return. As we sailed, the sea began to sparkle. A crane lifted cargo on the far pier; gulls dove for crisp scraps; and the spray on my skin was cold enough to dazzle the senses. A fellow passenger, an immigrant from a distant empire, told me with an incredulous smile that he had once nearly drowned when a ferry sank, and he showed me, without spite, the proof: a long scar where a stake had nearly stabbed him. His story did not frighten me; it only sharpened my zealous wish to see everything. He spoke of the necessity of courage, and of the capacity of the human heart to recover. When at last we landed, the porch of the little inn glowed warm against the marsh. A sheriff with a sarcastic wit checked our papers; a bureaucracy of forms had to be edited and signed, and I had to magnify the tiny print with a lens to read it. The price of a room was high, but the owner, eager to guarantee my comfort, gave me a free pat of butter and a slice of bread with my dinner, and promised the rate would not rush upward. That night, by candlelight, I wrote in my diary about the strange snout of a wild boar I had glimpsed in the brood of trees, about the cement walls of a ruined chapel, and about the contemplation that fills a traveler's mind at rest. I confess my golf clubs stayed in their bag the whole trip; there was simply too much to see. A nuisance of midges plagued the doorway, and the running stream nearby never ceased its music. The static of worry faded. I learned that to magnify a small life with bold travel is no false promise but a real necessity, and that even a barren land can dazzle a willing heart. When the fury of the weather finally calmed and a ghastly fog lifted, I smiled, made one last assessment of my route, and began the long journey home. Along the road, the journey hit a snag. Our guide, once a pupil of an old conqueror's tale, tried to reassure us and converse calmly, though his nasal voice carried an uncomfortable edge. He would not edit the truth or add fluff: the arbitrary bill for the ferry was a pitfall, a real detriment to efficiency. To persuade the crowd, he raised an outcry against waste, explaining how industrialized agriculture could distend budgets. The implication was clear, sharp as a stab; even a disabled cart on our flank felt the hardness of the climb. They agreed to release the funds, and added a small suffix of thanks to the contract.
translation: 毫不夸张地说，我前往苏格兰的旅程是我一生中最冒险的一次，然而我不会拿它换任何东西。我读过整整一条关于该地区的百科全书词条，字迹是那种古老到几乎难以辨认的笔迹，我差点放弃；但其中一段风景如画、描写其荒芜的沼地和古老氏族帝国的文字，说服了我动身。我轻装上路：一本日记、一瓶廉价香水、旅行社免费赠送的一本导游手册，以及充足的食物供应。没有读者会说我是个勤学的人，但我会做功课。这次航行从一艘拥挤的渡船开始。船长是个下巴坚毅、彬彬有礼的人，给我们做了简短而令人安心的讲话。“今天没有威胁，”他说，尽管收音机里噼啪作响的静电干扰，一阵突如其来的飑使甲板在我们脚下旋转。一辆装满板条箱的货车必须用千斤顶和绳索固定，以免它滑进下层货舱的沟里。我感到想惊慌的冲动，但我不会恳求提前返航。航行途中，大海开始闪耀。远处码头上一台起重机吊起货物；海鸥俯冲去抢酥脆的残渣；溅在我皮肤上的浪花冷得令人眩目。一位同船的旅客，来自遥远帝国的移民，带着难以置信的微笑告诉我，他曾在一艘渡船下沉时几乎淹死，并且毫无怨恨地给我看了证据：一道长长的伤疤，那里曾有一根木桩差点刺穿他。他的故事没有吓到我；只是让我热切想看遍一切的愿望更加强烈。他谈到勇气的必要，谈到人心恢复的能力。当我们终于登陆时，那间小客栈的门廊在沼泽映衬下散发着温暖的光。一位带着讽刺机智的警长检查了我们的证件；一堆官僚的表格需要修改和签字，我得用透镜放大那细小的字才能读。一间房的价格很高，但急于保证我舒适的老板，在晚餐时免费给了我一块黄油和一片面包，并承诺房价不会急速上涨。那晚，借着烛光，我在日记里写下：我在那片树林里瞥见的野猪奇怪的猪嘴、一座废弃小礼拜堂的水泥墙，以及旅人休息时心中充满的冥想。我承认我的高尔夫球杆整趟旅程都待在包里；要看的东西实在太多了。一群讨厌的蠓虫在门口作祟，附近流动的小溪从不停止它的乐声。忧虑的静电消散了。我懂得了：以大胆的旅行放大一段渺小人生，并非虚假的承诺，而是真实的必需，即使一片荒芜之地也能使一颗甘愿的心眩目。当天气的狂怒终于平息、一团苍白可怕的雾散去时，我微笑了，对路线做了最后一次评估，便踏上漫长的归途。沿着这条路，旅程遇到了障碍。我们的向导曾是一位老征服者故事的学生，他努力让我们安心，平静地交谈，尽管他带鼻音的嗓音透着一丝令人不适的意味。他不愿粉饰真相或添加废话：渡船那笔武断的账单是个陷阱，是对效率的真正损害。为了说服众人，他大声疾呼反对浪费，解释工业化农业如何让预算膨胀。其含义如刀刺般清晰；连我们侧翼一辆抛锚的残破推车也感受到攀爬的艰难。他们同意拨款，并在合同上加了一小段感谢的后缀。

text: The presentation began at eighty seconds past eight, when the lights of the grand showroom clicked on and an ensemble of musicians struck the first notes of the national anthem. This was no ordinary night: it was a voluntary charity gala held to fight poverty and hunger, and every member of the committee had worked to make it perfectly run. I will not betray the names of our donors, but I can say their generosity was unprecedented. A famous youth choir sang in harmony while a screen behind them showed a polar landscape of ice, a lone dove circling above an icy grove of shrub and berry. The theme that year was "thirst for justice," and the emancipation of the poor from misfortune was on everyone's mind. Our host had the easy-going charm of a born performer. He asked the crowd, bravely, to dig deep. "It would be a shame," he said, "to detach ourselves from those in pain." He wore a neat vest and angled his body toward the back rows so no one felt ignored. Earlier that week we had to minimize costs; prices had begun to skyrocket, and the gap between our budget and our goal made my heart sink. I confess I felt the sting of doubt, the same sight of red numbers that can discourage any earnest organizer. One sponsor, an inept and snobbish man, scold us for our methodology and tried to slander the cause, claiming our purity of motive was a sham. His bias was clear. But the testimony of the families we had helped, and the sheer harmony of the room, defeated his cynicism. A young woman, once arrested unjustly and later freed when the real bandit confessed, gave a speech that made the whole body of guests rise. She had every reason to fret, yet she spoke without anger. "Do not let any single misfortune ruin your hope," she said. "In the foreseeable future, we can bestow on one another the better world we all deserve." Even the murderer of joy could not have stayed unmoved. By midnight the donations had multiplied. We had asked for a modest sum and received far more; the cone-shaped collection jar overflowed, and a staggered line of guests waited to give. As the escalator carried the last visitors down past a wall of clothing donations, I felt no malaise, only gratitude. The character of a community, I thought, is measured not by its wealth but by its willingness to share. We had not solved poverty in one night, but we had narrowed the gap. The smoke of the celebratory candles drifted up; someone smiled and said sorry for crying. Soon the hall fell quiet, and I stood alone, perfectly content, watching the polar scene fade from the screen. I have to ask: what is the scope of a single show? The performance itself could stagger you. One act mixed gymnastics and boxing; a dancer in a bright skirt would tilt and balance on one leg, graceful as a lioness. A puppet shaped like a sphinx rose on a periscope-like pole with a click, while a paper cock crowed. Behind the scenes, the air-conditioning hummed, and a coalition of stagehands tried to delete every flaw and avoid ineffectiveness. There was no illegal trick, no effluent dumped from the kitchen where they served pie. Yet turmoil nearly arrived: a sponsor's resignation, a late notification, a predisposition to panic. They had to commit, to shear away the fluff, to fork over more cash, to cruise past the danger and not dump the project. The crew could arrest the chaos, restore digestion of the schedule, and the reproduction of the act ran like clockwork. After a quick shampoo of fresh ideas, they angled it from a new angle and turned a likely defeat into triumph.
translation: 演出在八点过八十秒时开始，那时宏伟陈列厅的灯光啪地亮起，一支乐手组成的合奏团奏响了国歌的第一个音符。这不是一个普通的夜晚：这是一场为对抗贫困与饥饿而举办的自愿慈善晚会，委员会的每一位成员都努力使它办得完美无缺。我不会泄露捐赠者的名字，但我可以说他们的慷慨是前所未有的。一支著名的青年合唱团和谐歌唱，他们身后的屏幕展示着一片极地的冰雪风景，一只孤独的鸽子在结冰的灌木与浆果树丛上空盘旋。那一年的主题是“对正义的渴望”，把穷人从不幸中解放出来是每个人心头之事。我们的主持人有着天生表演者那种逍遥自在的魅力。他勇敢地请人群慷慨解囊。“把自己与受苦之人分离开来，”他说，“将是一种耻辱。”他穿着整洁的马甲，把身体侧向后排，好让没有人觉得被忽视。那周早些时候我们不得不把成本降到最低；物价开始猛涨，我们的预算与目标之间的差距让我心沉。我承认我感到怀疑的刺痛，那种能让任何认真的组织者泄气的、满眼红色数字的景象。一位赞助人，一个无能又势利的男人，因我们的方法责骂我们，并试图诽谤这项事业，声称我们动机的纯洁是一场骗局。他的偏见显而易见。但我们帮助过的家庭的证词，以及全场纯粹的和谐，击败了他的愤世嫉俗。一位曾被冤枉逮捕、后因真正的歹徒认罪而获释的年轻女子发表了讲话，使全体来宾起立。她有充分的理由烦恼，却毫无怒气地讲话。“不要让任何一次不幸毁掉你的希望，”她说，“在可预见的未来，我们可以彼此赋予我们都应得的更美好的世界。”即便是欢乐的杀手也不会无动于衷。到午夜时，捐款已经翻了好几倍。我们请求的是一笔不大的款项，收到的却远远更多；锥形的募捐罐溢了出来，一排错落的客人排队等着捐赠。当自动扶梯载着最后的来访者下行、经过一整墙的衣物捐赠时，我没有感到任何不适，只有感激。我想，一个群体的品格，不是以它的财富来衡量，而是以它分享的意愿来衡量。我们没有在一夜之间解决贫困，但我们缩小了差距。庆祝蜡烛的烟雾袅袅升起；有人微笑着为自己的流泪道歉。很快大厅安静下来，我独自站着，心满意足，望着极地的景象从屏幕上渐渐淡去。我不得不问：一场演出的范围有多大？演出本身就能让你目瞪口呆。有一幕融合了体操与拳击；一位身着鲜艳裙子的舞者单腿倾斜并保持平衡，优雅如母狮。一个斯芬克斯造型的木偶随着一声咔哒声沿潜望镜般的杆子升起，一只纸公鸡随之啼鸣。后台空调嗡嗡作响，一群舞台工作人员组成的联盟努力删除每一处瑕疵，避免低效。没有任何非法把戏，厨房（供应派饼之处）也没有排放污水。然而骚乱险些降临：一位赞助商辞职、一份迟到的通知、一种易于恐慌的倾向。他们必须全力以赴，剪掉冗余，掏出更多现金，平稳驶过险境而不放弃项目。剧组制止了混乱，恢复了日程的消化，节目的再现如钟表般精准。在新点子洗礼之后，他们换了个角度，把可能的失败变成了胜利。

text: Let me assure you, the making of a small business is never faultless work. My uncle, a bespectacled electrician with a love of order, decided to lease a workshop near a foggy creek and sort out a promising new venture. His viewpoint was fundamental and simple: build something dependable, and the customers will come. He was no coward, but neither was he reckless; he would estimate every fee, study each indicator, and refuse to disregard the smallest risk. A confidential adviser had told him the investment could yield a steady dividend, provided he kept his books in order. Common sense, the adviser said, is the principle that should guide every decision, and inversely, greed will topple even the strongest firm. The classification of his tools filled an entire household bed of shelves: coils of yarn-like wire, a barometer, a humble nut-and-pipe assortment, a spotlight, a hook, and a heavy log he used as a doorstop. He worked indoors during the cold months and elsewhere when the weather allowed. One nameless morning, an elated customer rushed in, frightened that her nuclear-powered home generator had failed. My uncle, ever trustworthy, did not flatter her or make needless promise; he simply rolled up his sleeves. The fault, he could infer, was a corroded part where rust had begun to erode the metal, leaving slag and a foul smell. Some misguided rival might have charged a gratuity-sized fortune, but he asked only a modest fee. "It would be a shame," he said, "to torture an honest person with a padded bill." His common decency was his finest principle. As the work neared its ending, he explained each step, careful to interpret the technical terms so she would not feel like an alien in her own home. He showed her how to decrease waste, how a single homogeneous batch of cable could safeguard the whole circuit, and how a tiny modification could revoke years of inefficiency. The quietness of his manner reassured her. She left no longer unlucky but grateful, and she promised to tell the whole locality. Business, he often said, is a kind of fellowship; you sell trust before you sell anything else. He had no wish to be a hero or a politician, no monumental ambition; he simply wanted his childhood dream of a steady trade to come true. When I asked whether he ever felt the pull to expand, to chase prestige and become dependent on bankers, he only laughed. "Forward, yes," he said, "but slowly. A man who learns to read the indicator of his own contentment is rich enough." That, I think, is the truest principle of all. Consider a business as varied as a ship's berth. Good utilization of every asset matters, from the radial tires on the jet-black delivery van to the visual displays in the showroom. A bony old manager in a mackintosh, who loved gardening on weekends, wrote plain prose, not raucous colloquial slogans. He kept aspirin in his pocket for headaches, a tube of cream for his hands, and a mythical calm that could frighten rivals. He noted the asymmetry in the market like a tiger watching prey, sought the shade of caution, and treated each setback as an exoneration rather than defeat, as if a healthy gland of optimism fed a whole regiment of staff.
translation: 让我向你保证，创办一家小企业从来不是毫无瑕疵的工作。我的叔叔，一个戴眼镜、喜爱秩序的电工，决定在一条雾蒙蒙的小河边租下一间作坊，整顿出一桩前途光明的新生意。他的观点根本而简单：造出可靠的东西，顾客自会上门。他不是懦夫，但也不鲁莽；他会估算每一笔费用，研究每一个指标，拒绝漠视最小的风险。一位负责机密事务的顾问曾告诉他，只要把账目保持有序，这项投资能产生稳定的红利。那顾问说，常识是应当指导每个决定的原则，反过来，贪婪会使最强的公司倒塌。他工具的分类摆满了一整排家用床那么大的架子：一卷卷像纱线般的电线、一只气压计、一套朴素的螺母和管子、一盏聚光灯、一只钩子，还有一根他当门挡用的沉重圆木。寒冷的月份他在室内工作，天气允许时则到别处去。一个无名的清晨，一位兴高采烈的顾客冲了进来，惊恐于她那台核能家用发电机出了故障。我叔叔一如既往地值得信赖，没有奉承她，也没有作出不必要的承诺；他只是卷起袖子。他能推断出，毛病出在一个被腐蚀的零件上，锈已开始侵蚀金属，留下炉渣和难闻的气味。某个被误导的同行也许会收取一笔赏钱般的巨款，但他只要了不多的费用。“用虚报的账单去折磨一个诚实的人，”他说，“将是一种耻辱。”他普通的正派就是他最好的原则。当工作接近结尾时，他解释每一个步骤，仔细地把技术术语解释清楚，好让她不至于在自己家里像个外人。他向她展示如何减少浪费，一批同类的电缆如何能保护整个电路，一个微小的改动如何能撤销多年的低效。他举止的安静使她安心。她离开时不再不走运，而是心怀感激，还答应把整个地区的人都告诉。他常说，生意是一种交情；你在卖任何东西之前，先卖的是信任。他无意成为英雄或政客，没有纪念碑式的雄心；他只想让自己童年那个稳定生意的梦想成真。当我问他是否曾感到扩张的冲动，去追逐声望、变得依赖银行家时，他只是笑了。“前进，是的，”他说，“但要慢慢来。一个学会读懂自己满足感这一指标的人，就已经足够富有了。”我想，那才是所有原则中最真实的一条。想想一桩像船舶泊位一样多样的生意。每一项资产的利用都很重要，从送货厢式车上的子午线轮胎到陈列室里的视觉展示。一位身材瘦削、穿着雨衣、周末爱好园艺的老经理，写朴实的散文，而非沙哑的口语口号。他口袋里备着阿司匹林治头痛，一支护手霜，以及一种能吓退对手的神话般的镇定。他像老虎盯猎物般注意市场的不对称，寻求谨慎的荫蔽，把每次挫折当作昭雪而非失败，仿佛一枚乐观的腺体滋养着整团员工。

text: The confession came at dawn, when the frosty light first touched the snowbound campus. A freshman named Theo, formerly a quiet boy from a herd of country cousins, sat in the dry, cold lecture hall and decided to solve the puzzle that had begun to pester him. Originally he had wanted only good marks; now he wanted to organize his whole life. He was no winner yet, but he was cautious and modest, and he refused to be reckless. The address he gave that morning, publicly and with a low, steady voice, was meant as a kind of moral debate with himself. "I will not let temporary comfort cripple my future," he said. "I have a vocation to pursue." His complaint, if you could call it that, was simple: too many of his classmates lived on inexpensive habits and idle excess, treating study as a primitive chore rather than an activity of joy. He wanted a cure for that coldness. Presumably the others thought him strange. One motorist friend, a cyclist on weekends, asked him independently whether he had gone mad. Besides, the boy added, the deterioration of his sleep had begun to worry him. He would work past midnight, suck on cold coffee, and rinse his face to stay awake. A comparative glance at his peers gave him no comfort. With permission from his temporary adviser, he adopted a new plan, a quantitative one, to vary his routine and to enable real rest. He would not flatter himself with excuses. He learned to organize his notes, to confirm each fact, and to seek confirmation before acting. He found that a little restriction, far from being a torment, set him free. Besides his books, he kept a small spade for the campus garden, where he turned the turf and planted seeds in the frosty soil; the green shoots, when they came, gave him a kindness of spirit no lecture could. He even built a snowman one snowbound afternoon, laughing like a child, and the simple flavour of that joy surprised him. He realized that to emancipate himself from fear, he had to act with coherence and not let his nest of doubts congest his mind. His leader, a wise old teacher, did not scold or accuse; she would commentate gently, never reckless with a young heart. "You cannot purge every fault at once," she told him. "Inasmuch as you keep trying, you will improve." Theo left the hall as the dawn brightened, no longer unhappy. He was not yet a foreigner to doubt, but the sickness of despair had begun to recede. The footsteps behind him were his own, steady and sure, and the obtainable future, once a distant dream, now seemed close enough to touch. Over a long lunch, the deputy and an emigrant banker discussed capitalism as a pillar of society. It would perplex anyone how a unilateral statute on lending could sweeten or sour a partnership. They refused to adopt a dummy policy or misappropriate funds; that would be pathological, leaving only a semblance of order. Off the congested street, near a yacht club, they read an anthology of essays on unemloyment, housework, and the urge to incline toward reform. A cuckoo called; a footstep echoed. One pulled a stocking of coins from a vacuum-sealed box, noting how iron oxide had stained it, and added reinforcement to their plan.
translation: 那番自白发生在黎明时分，当寒霜的光第一次触到被雪困住的校园。一个名叫西奥的新生，从前是一群乡下表亲中安静的男孩，此刻坐在干燥寒冷的演讲厅里，决定解开那个开始纠缠他的谜题。起初他只想要好成绩；如今他想整顿自己的整个人生。他还不是个赢家，但他谨慎而谦虚，拒绝鲁莽行事。他那天早晨当众发表的、用低沉而平稳的声音说出的讲话，是一种与自己的道德辩论。“我不会让一时的安逸毁掉我的未来，”他说，“我有一项天职要追求。”他的抱怨，如果能这么叫的话，很简单：他太多同学靠廉价的习惯和懒散的过度度日，把学习当成原始的苦差，而非快乐的活动。他想为那份冷漠找到一剂良药。想必其他人觉得他古怪。一位摩托车手朋友，周末是个骑自行车的人，单独问他是不是疯了。此外，男孩补充道，他睡眠的恶化已经开始让他担忧。他会工作到过午夜，嘬着冷咖啡，洗把脸保持清醒。与同龄人作比较的一瞥并不能给他安慰。在临时顾问的许可下，他采用了一个新计划，一个量化的计划，去改变作息、让真正的休息成为可能。他不会用借口奉承自己。他学会整理笔记，确认每一个事实，并在行动前寻求确认。他发现，一点点的约束非但不是折磨，反而让他自由。除了书本，他还备了一把小铲子用于校园花园，在那里翻动草皮，在结霜的土里播种；嫩绿的新芽长出时，给了他任何讲座都给不了的精神上的善意。他甚至在一个被雪困住的下午堆了个雪人，像孩子一样大笑，那份喜悦简单的滋味令他惊讶。他意识到，要把自己从恐惧中解放出来，就必须行动有条理，不让那一窝疑虑堵塞他的头脑。他的领路人，一位睿智的老教师，没有责骂也没有指控；她会温和地评点，从不对一颗年轻的心鲁莽。“你无法一次清除每一个缺点，”她告诉他，“只要你不断尝试，就会进步。”西奥在黎明渐亮时离开大厅，不再不快乐。他对怀疑还不算陌生，但绝望的病症已开始消退。他身后的脚步声是他自己的，稳健而笃定，而那可得到的未来，曾是遥远的梦，如今近得仿佛触手可及。在一顿漫长的午餐中，那位副手和一位移居海外的银行家谈论资本主义这一社会支柱。一项关于借贷的单边法令竟能让合伙关系变甜或变坏，这会让任何人困惑。他们拒绝采用傀儡政策或盗用资金；那将是病态的，只留下秩序的表象。在拥挤街道之外、游艇俱乐部附近，他们读了一本关于失业、家务以及倾向改革的文集。一只布谷鸟鸣叫，一阵脚步声回响。一人从真空密封盒里取出一只装着硬币的长袜，注意到氧化铁已把它染色，并为他们的计划增添了强化的力量。

text: The mystery began at a chapel on the coast of Australia, where a Portuguese ambassador had moored his yacht for the night. He was an affectionate man of great prestige, devoted to his vocation, and doubtless no coward; yet that evening a strange malice seemed to rein in his usual calm. A talkative miller from the nearby town, a reputable but coarse fellow, had told him a tale that no barometer of reason could measure. Long ago, the miller said, an alien apparatus had washed ashore in this very area, an obstacle to every map and chart that cartography could draw. The ambassador, who would prefer, and indeed preferred, facts to rumor, did not flatter the story, but neither could he erase it from his mind. He decided to submit himself to the riddle. At midnight he walked the marginal path along the shore, a luminous moon above him, his long-term doubts pressing like a yoke upon his shoulders. The rage of the sea had calmed; only a soft wind stirred. He found, half-buried, a decorative code carved into stone, its variant symbols overlapping in a pattern no one alive could interpret. Was it a relic of some ancient rivalry, a yoke left by a forgotten aggressor, or merely a sham? The press would surely call it a tornado of nonsense, but the ambassador felt the destruction of an old certainty. He knelt, moderately careful, and brushed the moss away. Nobody was near. A reptile, perhaps a small lizard, darted past his hand and made him flinch. He thought of the marginal notes in his own diary, of the long road of his career, of the prestige that might be ruined if he spoke too soon. The chronology made no sense: the carving was older than any record in his domain of knowledge. He resolved to allocate his remaining days, however affluent or poor, to this single problem. A reputable scholar might invalidate his theory; a benevolent one might confirm it. Either way, the matter was now part of him. As the night withered into morning, exhausted but elated, he gathered a soft handful of sand into a tub he had carried, took up a shovel from the yacht, and dug a relative few feet into the soil. There, beneath a luminous layer of crystal, lay a second tablet, its surface coarse yet covered in a glutinous film. He saw at once that the two stones were a matched part, halves of a single record that someone had tried to halve and hide. The implied meaning struck him: this was no mere device, no decorative trifle, but a message meant to propel the curious toward a truth that powerful men had long suppressed. The ambassador, no longer dangerous to himself with doubt, pressed the tablets together. He felt his heart palpitate. The mystery, at last, had begun to speak. Ask yourself why some mysteries never wither. A teacher, my spouse's old mentor, would attend every lecture, preferring a sonnet to empty rhetoric. The bugbear of the age was disease: poliomyelitis and malnutrition, which could paralyse a child as surely as sorrow. It was inevitable, he said, that someone else would solve it. He kept a pad of notes, a needle and thread, and a tin of pilchard for the road. On the twelfth of the ultimo month, riding neither pony nor motorcycle but in transit by train, he reached the junction near the moor. He would lend a hand to anyone, gross profit or not, studying Leninism with the innocence of a student, dipping a solvent to clean an old b/l document. The market was bearish, but his faith was solvent. Years earlier he had judged a contestant in a poetry duel, awarding the prize to the one whose verse showed true innocence.
translation: 这桩谜团始于澳大利亚海岸的一座小礼拜堂，一位葡萄牙大使把他的游艇停泊在那里过夜。他是个深情而极有声望的人，献身于自己的天职，无疑也绝非懦夫；然而那天傍晚，一种奇怪的恶意似乎驾驭住了他平日的镇定。附近镇上一个爱唠叨的磨坊主，一个声誉不错却粗俗的家伙，给他讲了一个任何理性的晴雨表都无法衡量的故事。磨坊主说，很久以前，一件外星仪器曾被冲上正是这片海域的岸边，成了制图术所能绘出的每一张地图与海图的障碍。这位偏爱事实胜过谣言的大使没有吹捧这个故事，却也无法将它从脑中抹去。他决定让自己去面对这个谜。午夜时分，他沿着岸边那条边缘的小径行走，头顶一轮明亮的月，他长期的疑虑像一副枷锁压在肩头。海的狂怒已经平息；只有一阵轻柔的风在吹动。他发现一块半埋的、刻在石上的装饰性符文，那些不同的符号相互重叠成一种无人能解读的图案。它是某种古老对抗的遗物，是某个被遗忘的侵略者留下的枷锁，还是仅仅一个赝品？报界肯定会称它为一场无稽之谈的龙卷风，但大使感到一种旧有确信的崩塌。他跪下，相当小心地拂去青苔。附近没有人。一只爬行动物，也许是只小蜥蜴，从他手边窜过，吓了他一跳。他想起自己日记里那些边缘的批注，想起自己事业漫长的道路，想起若过早开口、可能毁掉的声望。年代毫无道理可言：这块刻石比他知识领域内任何记录都要古老。他下定决心，要把余下的日子，无论富足还是贫穷，都分配给这一个问题。一位声誉好的学者也许会推翻他的理论；一位仁慈的学者也许会证实它。无论如何，这件事如今已成了他的一部分。当夜色凋谢成清晨，他精疲力竭却又兴高采烈，把一软把沙子收进随身携带的木盆，从游艇上取来一把铁锹，往土里挖下相对不深的几英尺。在那里，一层晶莹的水晶之下，躺着第二块石板，表面粗糙却覆着一层粘性的薄膜。他立刻看出这两块石头是配对的一部分，是某人曾试图对分并藏匿的同一份记录的两半。其中暗含的意义击中了他：这绝非一件单纯的装置，也不是装饰性的琐物，而是一条意在驱使好奇者走向真相的讯息——而那真相，曾被权势者长久压制。大使不再因疑虑而对自己构成危险，他把两块石板按在一起，感到自己的心在跳动。谜团，终于，开始说话了。问问你自己，为何有些谜团永不凋零。一位老师，我配偶的旧日导师，会出席每一场讲座，宁愿读十四行诗也不愿听空洞的花言巧语。那个时代的梦魇是疾病：小儿麻痹症和营养不良，它们能像悲伤一样使一个孩子瘫痪。他说，迟早会有别人解决它，这是不可避免的。他随身带着一叠笔记、一根针线，以及一罐路上吃的沙丁鱼。在上个月的十二号，他既不骑小马也不骑摩托车，而是乘火车中转，抵达沼泽附近的交叉路口。无论毛利与否，他都愿向任何人伸出援手，怀着学生般的纯真研究列宁主义，蘸着溶剂清理一份旧提单。市场看跌，但他的信念依然稳健。多年前他曾在一场诗歌对决中担任评委，把奖项授予诗句中流露真正纯真的那位参赛者。

theme: The Congressman and the Famine Relief
text: Positively, no one in the capital expected the young congressman to pursue such an ambition. We had watched him for a span of years, a devoted public servant whose relationship with the voters was built on bravery and plain honesty. When news of the famine reached Russia and beyond, the disorder it created was immense; the plight of millions could ruin a whole generation. He was sensitive to their sorrow, and definitely not greedy for personal sale or turnover of favors. Maybe, he thought, he could mobilize the nation. He called a technician and an athletic young runner to help him connect the scattered relief committees, then asked his ancestor's old wagon to be restored so it might ride again as a symbol. The incidence of hunger was rising; the output of the farms had declined after a strange fission of the soil's fertility, and the circulation of grain had nearly ceased. He would not let the crisis decline into genocide by neglect. He refused to misinterpret the numbers, nor to restrict aid to the privileged few. "Neither pride nor property," he said in a lecture, "should handicap our response." He petitioned the embassy for help and sent a citizen delegation to superintend the docks. A magnate, once bearish and antiquated in his views, was moved to facilitate the shipping of sardine and other tinned goods; a banker agreed to decentralize the lending so that aid could reach every locality. The congressman's bravery seemed to capitalize on a novelty of spirit the nation had forgotten. Critics tried to tamper with his certification and to overthrow his committee; an ironical few called his ideal a mere imitation of older reforms. He conceded nothing. "However you interpret it," he told them, "this is not a skirmish over an item of policy. It is a fight for longevity itself." He moved to restore order, to create a system that could contain the chaos, and to improve the lives of the runner and the breadwinner alike. He capitalized every chance. When at last the wagons rolled, the people did not laugh; they wept and hugged one another. A child handed him a bowl and a single cluster of grain. The voiceless had found a voice. The decline had stopped. As a Roman proverb might say, the cost of doing nothing is the ruin of all. The congressman, accessible and moderate to the end, pursued his ideal until the booking of every relief ship was confirmed and the expiry of the crisis was finally in sight. His adjective for the day was simple: enough. To avert disaster, the citizens signed a petition. A famous quotation, often misquoted, became their lever: every participant could specialize in one task. The chatter in the limousine turned serious; a careless interpretation, a backward step, could rupture the fragile peace and tangle the talks. A billion small choices, like a stroke of a pen, could kill or save the plan. They would not charge like a bull; instead they shared a warm hug, used every device of diplomacy, and refused to let the moment slip.
translation: 毫无疑问，首都里没人料到这位年轻的国会议员会去追求这样的抱负。我们注视了他好些年，他是个献身的公仆，与选民的关系建立在勇敢和质朴的诚实之上。当饥荒的消息传到俄罗斯乃至更远的地方时，它所造成的混乱是巨大的；数百万人的困境足以毁掉整整一代人。他对他们的悲痛十分敏感，也绝不贪图个人的买卖或人情上的周转。也许，他想，他能动员整个国家。他叫来一名技术人员和一个擅长田径的年轻赛跑者，帮他把分散的救济委员会连接起来，又请人把他祖先那辆旧货运马车修复好，让它能再次上路，成为一种象征。饥饿的发生率在上升；农场的产量在土壤肥力发生某种奇怪的裂变后下降了，粮食的流通也几乎停止。他不会让这场危机因疏忽而衰退成一场屠杀。他拒绝误解那些数字，也拒绝把援助限制给少数特权者。“无论是骄傲还是财产，”他在一次演讲中说，“都不该妨碍我们的回应。”他向大使馆请愿求助，并派出一个公民代表团去监管码头。一位曾经看空又观念陈旧的大资本家被打动，去促成沙丁鱼及其他罐头货物的运输；一位银行家同意把放贷分散下去，好让援助能抵达每一个地方。这位议员的勇敢似乎利用了一种国家早已遗忘的精神新意。批评者试图篡改他的证明文件、推翻他的委员会；爱讽刺的少数人称他的理想不过是对旧改革的模仿。他什么也不让步。“无论你们怎么解释它，”他对他们说，“这不是一场围绕某项政策条款的小冲突。这是一场为长寿本身而战的斗争。”他着手恢复秩序，去创造一个能遏制混乱的体系，去改善赛跑者与养家者同样的生活。他抓住每一个机会。当货运马车终于上路时，人们没有讥笑；他们哭了，彼此拥抱。一个孩子递给他一只碗和一串谷粒。无声者找到了声音。衰退停止了。正如一句古罗马谚语所说，无所作为的代价是一切的毁灭。这位议员，始终平易近人且温和，一直追求他的理想，直到每一艘救济船的订位都得到确认，危机的终结终于在望。他这一天的形容词很简单：够了。为避免灾难，市民们签署了一份请愿书。一句常被误引的名言成了他们的杠杆：每位参与者都能专攻一项任务。豪华轿车里的闲谈变得严肃；一个草率的解释、一步倒退，都可能使脆弱的和平破裂并使谈判陷入纠缠。十亿个小小的选择，如同一笔落下，能毁掉或拯救计划。他们不会像公牛般横冲直撞；相反，他们温暖地拥抱，动用一切外交手段，拒绝让时机溜走。

theme: The Painter at the Mountain Shrine
text: Hi, the proverb says that wit and patience attain what force cannot, and the good-looking young painter believed it with all his emotional heart. He had a privileged childhood, yet he chose a hard path up the mountain to a mysterious shrine, hoping to discover the secret of an old riddle carved above its beam. The landscape was vast: a span of latitude where fog rolled over the ocean and daylight came softly through the mist. He would not let the cynic in town ruin his hope. "In spite of every obstacle," he wrote in his diary, "I will resume the climb at dawn." He packed a handkerchief, a capsule of medicine from the drugstore, a ribbon his mother gave him, and a crumb of toast wrapped in cloth. The trail was a thin wire of stone across a fleshy ridge. As he walked, he could query the silence and seem to hear a response from the rock itself. He had to concede that the journey might recompense him with nothing but a sham of an answer. Yet the mature traveller knows that worth is not always a purchase one can register at a shop. Combustion of dry brush warmed his camp; the diffuse glow of the fire painted the night. He felt no spite, no oppression of doubt. A colonist family offered him potluck hospitality and a cup of warm fruit drink, and he raised a toast to their kindness. They were not privileged, but their welcome was overt and generous, without the tyrannical airs of the wealthy. By morning his dismay had gone. He resolved to renew his quest. The path narrowed; loose gunpowder-grey stones shifted underfoot. Then, near the shrine, he uncapped his paints and began. The light might have seemed scant, but his artistic vision was persuasive and imaginative. He would not waive the chance to capture this. He worked with the steady stroke of one who has waited long. When he finished, he stood blindfold to his own fatigue, and the explanation of the riddle came to him as a quiet thought: the consequently simple truth was that the mountain itself was the shrine. No hijacker of fame, no resentful rival, no intransigent critic could take that from him. He was not the head of any school, just one painter with a load of canvas and a heart full of light. He let the fog lift. He had attained his answer. It is ironical, almost a trifle, how a short inquiry can change everything. An entrepreneur, no flabby dreamer, refused to be the scapegoat for a failed nomination. He would uncap a bottle, enclose a note, and never interrupt a guest. The lab measured temperature in Fahrenheit, tested a hydrogen sulphide sample at full saturation, and studied the componential and constitutive parts of a new alloy, recording each subsequence with care. A sullen technician, doing a sampling of the metal's innards, traced a thin artery of rust along the beam. Even the lord of the estate, who kept a tame panda in mind as a symbol of calm, called it constitutive of progress, not a trifle.
translation: 嗨，谚语说，机智与耐心能获得武力所不能获得的东西，那位好看的年轻画家全心全意、满怀激情地相信这一点。他有一个优渥的童年，却选择了一条艰难的路，登上山去寻访一座神秘的神龛，希望破解刻在它横梁上方的一个古老谜语的秘密。这片风景辽阔：在横跨一段纬度的地方，雾从海面上翻滚而来，日光柔和地穿过薄雾。他不会让镇上那个愤世嫉俗者毁掉他的希望。“尽管有种种障碍，”他在日记里写道，“黎明时我会重新开始攀登。”他打点了一块手帕、一粒从零食店买来的药用胶囊、一条母亲给的缎带，以及一块用布包着的烤面包屑。山径是一条横在多肉山脊上的细如铁丝的石路。当他行走时，他似乎在向寂静发问，又仿佛听见岩石本身的回应。他不得不承认，这趟旅程也许只会用一个虚假的答案来回报他。然而成熟的旅人知道，价值并不总是能在店里登记购买的东西。干枯灌木的燃烧温暖了他的营地；火堆漫射的光芒为夜晚上色。他没有怨恨，也没有疑虑的压迫。一户移民人家以家常便饭的热情款待他，递上一杯温热的果饮，他举杯为他们的善意祝酒。他们并不富有，但他们的欢迎是公开而慷慨的，没有富人那种专横的架子。到了早晨，他的沮丧已然消散。他决意重新踏上他的探寻。山路变窄了；火药灰色的松动石块在脚下滑动。然后，在神龛附近，他打开颜料，开始作画。光线也许显得不足，但他的艺术眼光极具说服力且富于想象。他不会放弃捕捉这一切的机会。他以等待已久之人那种沉稳的笔触工作。完成时，他对自己的疲惫视而不见，而谜语的解答如一个安静的念头降临于他：那个因此而简单的真理是——山本身就是神龛。任何盗取名声者、任何怨恨的对手、任何不肯妥协的批评家都无法把它从他手中夺走。他不是任何画派的首脑，只是一个带着一捆画布、心中充满光明的画家。他让雾散去。他已获得了他的答案。颇具讽刺意味的是，几乎是件小事——一次简短的询问就能改变一切。一位企业家，绝非优柔寡断的空想者，拒绝为一次失败的提名当替罪羊。他会拧开瓶盖，附上一张便条，从不打断客人。实验室用华氏温度计测温，在完全饱和状态下测试硫化氢样品，研究新合金的成分构成与构成性部分，仔细记录每一个后续结果。一位绷着脸的技师对金属内脏做抽样，沿着横梁追踪一条细细的锈蚀动脉。连庄园的主人——他心中养着一只象征平静的温顺熊猫——也称这是进步的构成要素，而非区区小事。

text: In the preface to her business plan, the founder of our community farming cooperative made one sentiment paramount: that healthy, sanitary food should be available to everyone at rock-bottom prices, never sold wrongly or at an exorbitant markup. She had chosen to launch the venture beside an old metro station, on a stony plot whose poor drainage and porous soil first seemed a blockage to any harvest. Yet with conscientious work and a flexible technique, the team began to strengthen the beds, scrub the rusted tools, and assemble raised planters set at a regular interval. A clever design for the irrigation helped them unravel each early problem. The custom in our community was to gather each dawn. At one such gathering, a courier arrived with a consignment of seedlings, a parcel of tender greens, each leaf still wet with dew. We were glad. A local agency offered compensation for any crop lost to frost, and a dealer of farm gear let us reserve a multifunction tractor, a fixture we could not afford outright. We even asked a vet to vaccinate the goats that grazed the margin. The whole scene felt hopeful. As yet no pest did appear, but we stayed watchful. We used a thermometer to probe the greenhouse heat and would not disallow any clue of disease; one apparent sign of blight could crumble a season's yield. To raise morale, the founder would tell a brisk story about how, against all odds and with intent, the first farmers had successfully built their fields, and she would not blame the soil. Her words did not humiliate the doubters; rather they helped subdue every fearful thought, and thereby put us at ease. We post our notes online, a ubiquitous practice now. A demonstration day drew a crowd: a bride and groom on their way from a graduation, a man nursing arthritis, even the police on patrol. My mother-in-law, who holds the garden in high esteem, brought garlic bread and pressed a small memento, a carved wooden chord ornament, into my chest pocket. Someone joked that we should smuggle a spaceship in to till the back forty, since the field stretched nearly a kilometer. Of course there were setbacks, a ramification no one foresaw: a tack puncturing a tyre, a sudden blast of hail whose stinging lash struck the elliptical pond, a carpet of mud across the path. A toothbrush served, oddly, to clean the seed trays. Economic pressure was real, and the secondary market gave us nearly unlimited competition. But after an interruption for repairs, work resumed. In a quiet prayer of thanks, some whispered the name of Jesus, we knew the staple harvest, and our safety, were assured. Practically every neighbor now visits; the rising tide of support was, in the end, pretty wonderful to see.
translation: 在她商业计划书的序言里，我们社区农业合作社的创办人把一种情怀置于至高地位：健康、卫生的食物应当让每个人都能以最低价买到，绝不能错误地出售或漫天要价。她选择在一座老地铁站旁边创办这项事业，地块多石，排水不畅，土壤渗水，起初像是阻碍任何收成的障碍。然而凭着认真的劳作和灵活的技术，团队开始加固苗床、擦洗生锈的工具，并按固定间隔装配抬高的种植槽。一套巧妙的灌溉设计帮助他们解开了早期的每一个难题。我们社区的习俗是每天黎明聚集。在一次这样的聚会上，一名快递员送来一批秧苗，一包鲜嫩的青菜，每片叶子上还挂着露水。我们都很高兴。当地一家代理机构愿意为任何被霜冻毁掉的作物提供赔偿，一位农机经销商让我们预定一台多功能拖拉机，这是我们无力直接购买的固定装置。我们甚至请兽医给在田边吃草的山羊接种疫苗。整个景象令人充满希望。至今没有害虫出现，但我们保持警惕。我们用温度计探测温室的热度，绝不忽视任何病害的线索；一处明显的枯萎迹象就可能让一季的收成化为乌有。为了鼓舞士气，创办人会讲一个轻快的故事，讲述最初的农人如何不顾一切、满怀决心地成功建起了田地，她绝不责怪土壤。她的话没有羞辱那些怀疑者，反而帮助压下每一个恐惧的念头，从而让我们安心。我们如今把笔记发布到网上，这是一种无处不在的做法。一个示范日吸引了人群：一对刚从毕业典礼赶来的新娘新郎、一位患关节炎的老人，甚至还有巡逻的警察。我那位极为珍视这座花园的岳母带来了蒜香面包，还把一件小纪念品——一枚雕花的木质和弦饰物——塞进我的胸前口袋。有人开玩笑说，我们该走私一艘宇宙飞船来耕作后面那片地，因为田地绵延将近一公里。当然也有挫折，一个没人预料到的衍生问题：一枚平头钉扎破了轮胎，一阵突如其来的冰雹用刺人的鞭击拍打着椭圆形的池塘，一层泥铺满了小路。一把牙刷竟意外地用来清洁育苗盘。经济压力是真实的，二级市场带给我们几乎无限的竞争。但在一段修理的中断之后，工作又恢复了。在一声安静的感恩祷告中，有人低声念着耶稣的名字，我们知道主粮的收成和我们的安全都有了保障。如今几乎每位邻居都来参观；这股不断上涨的支持浪潮，最终看起来相当美好。

text: The erection of the new observatory beside the lagoon was, by any measure, a colossal task. The same administrative committee that had once governed a small monarchy of stargazers now had to execute a vast campaign, essentially a mission to map Mars and the various bodies beyond. Their representative, a sociable woman who valued punctuality above almost all, would lay out the following plan twice for any newcomer, while the preceding director merely looked on with an innocent, almost toneless calm. Although the budget figures were astronomical, the company behind the project refused to curtail the science. They issued a warranty on the equipment and, in addition, a clear voucher for every shipment that arrived. One technical glimmer of hope came from an inert gas that, in various tests, would not vanish under heat. "We must reconsider, and recycle the old lenses," she said, glancing at the algebra on the board, her wrist resting on a worn thesaurus. Her surname, common in England and across Africa, was printed on a banner above the square. The mission concerned more than mere data; it had to accomplish a sense of wonder, and to be representative of human reach. A reportage team, sworn to allegiance, would dramatize the launch, though some feared the prose would prove monotonous. As the countdown began, a young typist wept, then quickly dried her eye and learned to forget her nerves. She had practiced shorthand to record every word. The mutual respect among the crew was apparent. They did not weep again. When at last the rocket left the graveyard of abandoned scaffolds, a colossal blast lit the orange sky, and the crowd, fearless, raised a final cheer that no ultimatum could ever subdue. Engineers in the hydroelectric station nearby felt the ground shudder. The mass of onlookers, their allegiance now beyond doubt, knew that this hour would prophesy a brighter age. Some, with a quandary in their hearts, whispered a prayer. Yet the contrive of the chief planner, who had flicked aside every delusion, held firm. He would not enact a single rule he could not defend, nor would he overhaul a system that worked. A flick of his thumb on the panel, a glint of porcelain dials, and the mission was a triumph. There was, he admitted with a sore throat and a tired straw of a voice, no shortcut to the stars, no dagger that could cut the long road short. Detection of the signal proved the engineers tolerant of risk and fully terminable in their patience. From Langkap to the far north, mole-sized antennas, lined like rows of bamboo-shoot in a frying-pan-bright field, tracked the craft. Even a humble mole on the hill seemed to watch. With a glad heart, herself the proudest of all, she signed the final log. In the twelve months that followed, nothing bad would happen to dim the project's value. The teaching team kept careful records concerning every result they could obtain, and joy seemed to overflow from the control room. Far from any aggravation, the mood was one of pure delight.
translation: 在泻湖边建造这座新天文台，无论从哪个角度看都是一项庞大的任务。同一个行政委员会，曾经管理着一小群观星者的君主国般的天地，如今却要执行一场浩大的运动，本质上是一项绘制火星及其外天体的使命。他们的代表是一位看重交际、把准时看得几乎高于一切的女士，她会把下述计划向任何新人讲两遍，而前一任主任只是带着天真、近乎无声调的平静在一旁看着。尽管预算数字大得惊人，项目背后的公司却拒绝削减科研。他们为设备出具了保修书，此外还为每一批到货开具清晰的凭单。一线技术上的希望来自一种惰性气体，它在各种测试中都不会在高温下消失。"我们必须重新考虑，并把旧镜片回收利用，"她说，瞥了一眼黑板上的代数式，手腕搁在一本磨旧的辞典上。她的姓氏在英格兰和整个非洲都很常见，被印在广场上方的一条横幅上。这项使命所关乎的不只是数据；它还必须成就一种惊奇之感，并能代表人类所能企及的高度。一支宣誓效忠的报告文学团队将把这次发射戏剧化，尽管有人担心文字会显得单调。倒计时开始时，一位年轻的打字员哭了，随即很快擦干眼睛，学会忘掉紧张。她练过速记，要记下每一个字。机组之间的相互尊重显而易见。他们没有再哭。当火箭终于离开那片废弃脚手架的墓地时，一团巨大的爆炸气浪照亮了橙色的天空，人群无所畏惧地发出最后的欢呼，任何最后通牒都无法将其压制。附近水电站的工程师感到地面震动。这群旁观者，他们的忠诚此刻已无可置疑，知道这一刻将预言一个更光明的时代。一些心怀困惑的人低声祈祷。然而那位已经拂去一切错觉的总规划师所设计的方案稳如磐石。他绝不会颁布一条自己无法捍卫的规则，也不会去检修一套运转良好的系统。他用拇指在面板上一弹，瓷质刻度盘一闪，使命便大功告成。他带着发炎的喉咙和疲惫如稻草般的嗓音承认，通往星辰没有捷径，没有哪把匕首能把这条漫长的路截短。信号的探测证明工程师们能容忍风险，并在耐心上完全可以持久。从冷甲到遥远的北方，鼹鼠般大小的天线，像煎锅般明亮的田野里一排排竹笋一样排列，追踪着飞行器。连山坡上一只不起眼的鼹鼠似乎也在注视。她怀着欢欣的心，自己是所有人中最自豪的，签下了最后一份日志。在接下来的十二个月里，不会发生任何坏事来削减这个项目的价值。教学团队就他们能获得的每一项结果都仔细记录，欢乐似乎从控制室里溢出。远没有任何恶化，气氛是纯粹的喜悦。

text: The two of us set out early on a brilliant morning, our destination a singular island far across the Atlantic. The proposal had seemed a bargain at first, an affordable adventure, but the expense soon grew, and a needy budget left us tracking every dollar. Our slender boat, with one oar and a worn amplifier for the radio, was no match for the torrent that would later sweep the deck. A profitable venture, the dealer had called it, though his disreputable, deceitful grin should have warned us. He was an insolvent tinker who would offend anyone, an unaccommodating man whose every word seemed a piece of propaganda. We engaged him only out of restraint, fearing worse. By mid-ocean a nightmare began. A torrent of rain lashed the bulky hull; the boat seemed to dive, then whirl, and I had to choke back a shriek. The worst was a near crash against a reef in the western hemisphere of the chart, where the inaccessible north shore loomed like a veil of mist, a shroud over the unofficial route no captain would carve. My methodical, conscientious companion, ever influential and brilliant in a crisis, drew on empirical skill to steady us. Somehow she could withstand the swell, her toe braced, her face like wool-pale stone. We must endure, she said, and her singular calm did not crumble. I am afraid we strayed badly. The compass, our only mirror of true direction, gave a hideous reading, and a dive into the charts brought a reduction of hope. Still, we did not panic. To dive deeper into the math, she pulled a quarterly almanac, ran the figures twice, and after careful scrutiny found the error. A magnet near the dial had skewed everything. Once we moved it, the needle settled, and a torrent of relief swept over us. By dusk the storm endures no longer; the sweep of clear sky returned. We could already taste the steak and lamb the inhabitants of the island were said to roast. A stewardess on a passing French liner from Portugal waved; we waved back, funny and giddy with survival. Negotiation with the harbor pilot was brisk. We had email, but no signal; instead we relied on an introductory chart and the syllabus of seamanship she had memorized. The originality of her plan, an apologetic blend of art and guesswork, somehow worked. As an ambulance of gulls wheeled overhead, I felt the stimulation of pure joy. We had unified our nerve. No terrorism of the sea, no miniature fear, could now frustrate us. A mouse of doubt still gnawed, but our determination held. The decoration on the prow, a small carved swan, seemed to nod. Crossing that crossing line of latitude, I knew this affair would mark us forever, a memory to venerate, a story no consideration of fatigue could ever erase. Back on the outer dock, we did not stray from our plan, nor did our courage tumble. We would engage no needless confrontation with the sea. A rough sluice gate guarded the inner harbor; beyond it, a baker let yeast rise for our welcome bread. To distil one lesson from the voyage, and to unify our small crew, I would call us survivors. The harbor's loyal clientele, who track every arriving ship, cheered as we tied up.
translation: 我们两人在一个晴朗的清晨早早出发，目的地是一座遥远的、独一无二的岛屿，远在大西洋的另一边。这个提议起初看似划算，是一次买得起的冒险，但开销很快增加，拮据的预算让我们盯紧每一美元。我们那艘细长的小船，只有一支桨和一台破旧的收音机扩大器，根本敌不过后来席卷甲板的激流。那个商人称这是一桩有利可图的买卖，尽管他那声名狼藉、骗人的笑容本该警告我们。他是个无力偿债的焊锅匠，谁都会被他得罪，是个不肯通融的人，每句话都像宣传。我们只是出于克制才雇了他，怕遇到更糟的。航行到大洋中央，噩梦开始了。倾盆大雨抽打着笨重的船体；船一会儿俯冲，一会儿打旋，我不得不强忍住一声尖叫。最糟的是险些撞上海图西半球处的一片礁石，那里难以接近的北岸像一层雾的面纱赫然耸现，是没有哪个船长敢去开辟的非正式航线上的一块遮蔽物。我那有条理、认真的同伴，在危机中一向有影响力又出色，凭借经验的本领稳住了我们。她不知怎地能顶住涌浪，脚趾撑着，脸像羊毛般苍白的石头。我们必须坚持，她说，她那非凡的镇定没有崩溃。恐怕我们偏航得很厉害。指南针——我们唯一能映照真实方向的镜子——给出一个骇人的读数，一头扎进海图带来了希望的减少。但我们没有惊慌。为了更深入地钻研计算，她抽出一本季刊年历，把数字算了两遍，经过仔细审查后找到了错误。表盘附近有块磁铁扭曲了一切。我们一把它挪开，指针就稳定下来，一股如激流般的宽慰席卷我们。黄昏时分，暴风雨不再持续；晴空的连绵又回来了。我们已经仿佛尝到岛上居民据说会烤的牛排和羊羔肉。一艘从葡萄牙驶来的法国客轮上的女乘务员挥手；我们挥手回应，因为劫后余生而又滑稽又眩晕。与港口领航员的谈判很爽快。我们有电子邮件，却没有信号；于是我们依靠一张入门海图和她背下来的航海课程提纲。她那计划的独创性，是一种带着歉意的、艺术与猜测的混合，竟然奏效了。当一群海鸥像救护车般在头顶盘旋时，我感到纯粹喜悦的刺激。我们已把勇气拧成一股。海洋的任何恐怖、任何微小的恐惧，如今都无法挫败我们。一只怀疑的耗子仍在啃噬，但我们的决心未动。船头的装饰物，一只小小的雕刻天鹅，似乎点了点头。越过那条纬度的交叉线，我知道这段经历将永远在我们身上留下印记，一段值得崇敬的记忆，一个任何疲惫的考量都无法抹去的故事。回到外侧的码头，我们没有偏离计划，勇气也没有崩塌。我们不会与大海进行任何无谓的对峙。一道粗糙的水闸守护着内港；闸门那边，一位面包师让酵母为我们的迎宾面包发起来。若要从这趟航行中提炼出一条教训，并把我们这支小船员队伍凝聚成一体，我会称我们为幸存者。港口忠实的常客们追踪每一艘到港的船，在我们系缆时欢呼起来。

text: Despite the cruel winter, the old servant kept the manor running with thrifty care. In the pantry she would stew a thick cargo of bread, bone broth, and canned vegetables, ladling it from a battered frying foil into bowls warmed beside the fireplace. Her boldness was noticeable, even awesome, though she never sought to entertain the nobility with idle talk. One operational rule guided her: inspect everything twice. She would wipe each fixture, scrub the carriage wheels, and tabulate the household money in a ledger no typist could fault. The estate's deficit had grown vast, a fatal dumping of debt, and the warden warned that creditworthiness was now critical. A pragmatic woman, she refused to panic. The version of thrift she practiced was almost theoretical, yet it worked. Despite hostile neighbors and a regional racism that made her position unpleasant, she stayed favorable in spirit, doggedly cheerful. When the young lord, sly and reactionary, threatened to sack her, she did not bleed self-pity. She would invest her own meager allowance to underwrite repairs, even venture savings to buy a medium grade of coal. A territorial dispute with the next estate had reached deadlock; a hopeless inconsistency in the old deeds made the boundary missing from every map. One marvellous morning, while she swept the courtyard, a left-handed clerk arrived by expressway with a telegram. The bloc of lawyers had reached a verdict. To substantiate the family's claim, they needed the original decode of a coded letter hidden behind a loose bone of the chimney. She climbed up, a riotous wind tugging her mantle, and amplified her grip on a bolt as the ladder shook. The residue of soot made her cough; a ripple of dust rose like a whirlwind. With a dash she pulled the paper free, then gave a triumphant shout. The cyclic seasons of worry seemed to splash away. The document, written in shorthand that read like a foreign epoch, proved the land was theirs. No mischief, no filth of rumor, could now stain the house. That night they watched a flickering video on a borrowed set, played chess, and skated on the frozen pool. The lord, his paranoia eased, vowed to defend her against any awesome threat. She had shown, with quiet nobility, that one careful servant could not be dwarfed by fate. A racism-free, hopeful order took root. Even the hut at the gate, where she had first slept in a sack on a bed of straw, now flew a small badge of honor. She rented out a spare row of cottages, turned a once-rusty estate profitable, and showed that boldness paired with thrift makes any winter survivable. To show the books, she would debit each expense and rent receipt, attentive to the last centigrade of detail, never inconsistent. Her maths was clean, with no ellipsis of any figure. As an inhabitant of fourteen winters in that house, she understood the importance of trust, and warmly she would amplify her praise of the staff rather than protest their small faults. On the frozen pond the children would skate, and the estate, once doomed, seemed to vanquish its debts.
translation: 尽管冬天严酷，那位年迈的仆人仍以节俭的细心维持着庄园的运转。在食品柜里，她会炖上厚厚一大批面包、骨头汤和罐装蔬菜，用一只破旧的煎锅箔把它从锅里舀进碗中，碗就在壁炉旁温着。她的胆略显而易见，甚至令人敬畏，尽管她从不想用闲聊去取悦贵族。一条经营准则指引着她：凡事检查两遍。她会擦拭每件固定装置，刷洗马车轮，并在一本连打字员都挑不出错的账簿里把家用钱列成表。庄园的赤字已增得庞大，是一场致命的债务倾销，看守警告说如今信誉至关重要。作为一个注重实效的女人，她拒绝惊慌。她所奉行的那种节俭近乎理论化，却行之有效。尽管邻人怀有敌意，地区性的种族歧视让她的处境令人不快，她在精神上仍保持乐观，顽固地开朗。当那位狡猾又保守反动的年轻领主威胁要解雇她时，她并不自怜流血。她会拿出自己微薄的津贴去承担修缮，甚至冒险动用积蓄买中等品级的煤。与邻近庄园的领土纠纷陷入僵局；旧地契里一处毫无希望的前后矛盾使边界在每张地图上都缺失。一个绝妙的早晨，她在打扫庭院时，一位左撇子文书乘高速公路带来一封电报。律师集团已作出裁决。为了证实这家人的主张，他们需要藏在烟囱一块松动骨头后面的一封密信的原始解码。她爬上去，狂乱的风扯着她的斗篷，梯子摇晃时她加紧抓住一个螺栓。煤灰的残渣让她咳嗽；一阵尘土像旋风般泛起涟漪。她猛地一冲把纸抽了出来，随即发出胜利的喊叫。忧虑那循环往复的季节似乎被泼洒而去。那份用速记写成、读来像异国时代的文件，证明了这片土地属于他们。如今任何恶作剧、任何谣言的污秽都无法玷污这个家族。那晚他们在借来的机器上看了一段闪烁的录像，下了国际象棋，又在结冰的水池上滑冰。领主的偏执缓解了，发誓要保护她免受任何可怕的威胁。她以安静的高尚证明了，一个细心的仆人不会被命运矮化。一种没有种族歧视、充满希望的秩序扎下了根。连大门口那间小屋——她最初睡在稻草床上一个麻袋里的地方——如今也挂起了一枚小小的荣誉徽章。她把一排空置的农舍租了出去，把一座曾经生锈的庄园变得有利可图，证明了胆略加上节俭能让任何寒冬都熬得过去。为了展示账目，她会把每一笔开支和租金收据记入借方，连最细微的一摄氏度细节都全神贯注，从不前后矛盾。她的算术干净利落，没有省略任何一个数字。作为在那座房子里度过十四个冬天的住户，她懂得信任的重要性，她会热情地放大对员工的称赞，而不是抗议他们的小过失。在结冰的池塘上，孩子们滑冰，而这座一度注定衰败的庄园，似乎征服了它的债务。

text: The director of the museum, a watchful and conscientious woman, called the whole team together for an essential briefing tonight. An archaeologist by training, she had spent her little spare time studying a posthumous edition of a heretic scholar's notes, a circular argument that other experts had long resented. Her determination never wavered. "We must not delude ourselves," she said, her wooden pointer tapping a global map. "The dig in Italy is critical, and the evidence so far is invalid until we revise it." The researcher beside her, excited yet careful, agreed: they would cooperate and work together, not apart. He resolved to extol the buyer who had funded them, a thankful gesture, instead of dwelling on the sadness of past failures. Away from the lecture, the courtyard was calm. A circular fountain caught the laser-bright moonlight, and the fragile, frail blade of a single reed trembled. The team's inspiration came from a strange hazard: a bandage of limited cloth, found wrapped around a carbon-dated bone, bore a countersignature and a forensic mark that subsequently changed everything. To the untrained eye it looked like mere decoration, but its fiber told another story. Constituent threads, when insulated under glass and not allowed to dissipate, revealed a hidden brand. The other clue was a slit in a wooden chest, sealed with a sanitary postal stamp from a long-dead empire. "This is no delusion," the director said. "Hereafter we treat every flesh-and-blood detail as evidence." One assistant, prone to flirt and to drowse during long sessions, suddenly grew watchful. He had nearly closed the file, mistaken about its value. Now he saw the eradication of doubt within reach. An accusation of fraud, once leveled at the heretic, could finally be negated; the man's reminiscence, written in a frightening, outrageous tone, was in retrospect a record of truth, not a murder of facts. The team felt a happiness close to determination itself. They would not resent the long nights. To mislead the public, even by a fraction, would be a sin; to draw the right conclusion was their essential duty. As the total picture emerged, the director gave a hearty, warmly approving nod. Her mother, an invalid in a distant infirmary, had once told her that patience extols the patient. The retell of this discovery, she knew, would become an excited, brand-new chapter. No assassinator of reputations, no disloyalty, could now undo it. The frightening dear cost of years had bought a posthumous justice, and that, she felt, was a blessing worth every wakeful hour. After the funeral of the old scholar, the museum's bookkeeper, a man conversant with every ledger, refused to waver. He would not flip through the files carelessly, nor abuse the archive. In view of the find, the team would reap real reward, warranted by the evidence. No partisan attack, no political vent, could induce them to amalgamate truth with rumor. A choral group sang at the fourth memorial; outside, a smith would hammer a bright ingot, and a pollutant haze hung in the air. The director's daughter, her nose pressed to the glass case, took up archaeology as more than a pastime. Workers would insulate the relics in a sealed shop room, safe from harm.
translation: 博物馆馆长是一位警惕而认真的女性，今晚她把整个团队召集起来做一次必要的简报。她受过考古学家的训练，把自己不多的空闲时间都花在研究一位异端学者笔记的死后版本上——那是一套循环论证，长期为其他专家所怨恨。她的决心从未动摇。"我们绝不能自欺，"她说，木制教鞭敲着一幅全球地图。"意大利的发掘至关重要，目前的证据在我们修订之前都是无效的。"她身旁的研究者既兴奋又谨慎，表示同意：他们将合作、一起工作，而不是各自为政。他决意去赞美资助他们的那位买主，以表感谢，而不是沉湎于过往失败的悲伤。离开讲座，庭院一片宁静。一座圆形喷泉接住了激光般明亮的月光，一根芦苇脆弱纤细的叶片在颤抖。团队的灵感来自一个奇怪的危险物：一卷数量有限的布绷带，缠在一根经碳定年的骨头上，带有一处副署和一个法医标记，后来改变了一切。在外行眼中它不过是装饰，但它的纤维讲述着另一个故事。当那些组成的线在玻璃下被隔绝、不让其消散时，显露出一个隐藏的烙印。另一条线索是一只木箱上的一道狭缝，用一张来自早已灭亡帝国的卫生邮票封着。"这不是错觉，"馆长说。"此后我们把每一个有血有肉的细节都当作证据。"一名容易调情、在长会中打瞌睡的助手忽然变得警觉。他差点合上这份卷宗，错估了它的价值。如今他看到消除疑虑近在咫尺。曾加诸那位异端的欺诈控告终于可以被否定；那个人的回忆录，以一种令人害怕、骇人的语气写成，回顾来看是真相的记录，而非对事实的谋杀。团队感到一种近乎决心本身的幸福。他们不会怨恨这些漫漫长夜。哪怕只误导公众一丝一毫也将是罪过；得出正确的结论才是他们必要的职责。当全部图景浮现，馆长热忱而热情赞许地点了点头。她的母亲是远方疗养院里的一名病人，曾告诉她耐心会颂扬有耐心的人。她知道，这一发现的复述将成为令人兴奋的崭新一章。任何声誉的暗杀者、任何不忠都已无法将它推翻。多年来令人心疼的昂贵代价换来了一份身后的公正，而她觉得，这是值得每一个不眠之夜的福分。在老学者的葬礼之后，博物馆的簿记员——一个精通每一本账册的人——拒绝动摇。他不会粗心地翻阅卷宗，也不会滥用档案。鉴于这一发现，团队将收获真正的回报，这有证据担保。任何党派的攻击、任何政治的发泄，都无法诱使他们把真相与谣言混为一谈。一个合唱队在第四次纪念会上歌唱；外面，一名铁匠敲打着一块明亮的锭铁，空气中悬着一层污染物的雾霾。馆长的女儿把鼻子贴在玻璃展柜上，她把考古学当作不只是消遣。工人们会把文物隔绝在一间密封的库房里，使其免受损害。

theme: A Doctor in the Volcanic Town
text: The new nurse arrived in an urban district shadowed by a volcanic cape, where a single dwarf pine clung to the harsh slope. Her assistant, a shoemaker's son turned medical helper, followed her through the season's first storm. Their action that day would mark them both. A boy had begun to vomit and his thyroid felt swollen; an antibiotic was their only feasible weapon against the infection, yet the local supply was a mess. Lack of clean water made every task harsh. The doctor, an eloquent and pure-hearted woman, refused to neglect a single patient. "To eradicate this disease, we must accelerate," she said, and her verbal orders were conclusive. She would not succumb to panic. Inside the shanty clinic, a broom leaned by the bottle-lined shelf; a diesel generator coughed, its irregular hum a small comfort. She wiped a yellow stain of lime from the table, set a salad bowl aside, and reached for the bleach to keep the room sanitary. A repetition of careful steps, she knew, was the only cure for chaos. The truth was plain: deficiency of supplies could be fatal. Yet she would not let violence or despair oppress her team. When a man clutched his chest and screamed, she did not wake in fright; she acted. Her cunning, in the best sense, was practical. She used a wrench to fix the broken clutch of an old motor, configuration by configuration, so the fridge could run and the antibiotic stay cold. Recreation was a luxury here. A picnic, a game of chess, a publication to recite by the fire — such comfortable pleasures belonged to another life. Still, the team found a delightful, even comfortable rhythm. The shoemaker's son, once unwilling, grew bold. "This is my role," he said, and he meant it. They worked to expansion, training five then thirteen local volunteers, each given a badge that bore a small graph of progress. A socialist council, eager to follow the new keeping of records, sent a delegation by railway. The doctor's speciality was not glamour but repetition done right. She squeezed every drop from a fraught budget; reimbursement, she joked, would arrive after the next eruption. One night a tyrant rumor spread — that the clinic would discontinue — but it proved false. The nurse, harsh only with carelessness, made the truth follow her words. By the dry season's end the disease was in retreat, and the small mouth of the cape, once a place of fester and harm, was a haven of health. "We did not eradicate fear," she told the the assembled crowd, smiling at her own slip of the tongue, "but we did eradicate the worst of the sickness." The pellet of medicine, the shoe of a walking patient, the wake of every recovered child — all of it, she said, was worth more than any gramophone song or salad supper. Truth, harsh and pure, had won. In an essay she later wrote, the nurse called neglect a kind of sin, a fetter on any healer. She described how an orthopedic case nearly slipped into delirium, how a child's scream pierced the night, and how she had to squeeze past panic and guard her calm. A Moslem elder with a grey beard, her son-in-law's mentor, helped her safely store disposable gloves. Regionalization of the clinics, an arboreal network spreading like branches, let aid reach far. No scum of doubt could ruffle her resolve. At dusk a pyre of old crates burned, its embers a twinkle against the dark; even the subjunctive 'if only' lost its sting, and hope, like a tentacle, reached toward tomorrow.
translation: 新来的护士抵达一个被火山岬阴影笼罩的城市街区，一棵矮小的松树紧抓着陡峭崎岖的山坡。她的助手是鞋匠的儿子，后来成了医疗帮手，跟着她穿过这个季节的第一场风暴。那天他们的行动将给两人都打下印记。一个男孩开始呕吐，甲状腺摸起来肿胀；抗生素是他们对抗感染唯一可行的武器，可当地的供应却一团糟。缺乏清洁的水使每项工作都艰难。这位医生口才出众、心地纯洁，拒绝忽视任何一名病人。"要根除这种疾病，我们必须加快，"她说，她口头的命令是决定性的。她不会屈服于恐慌。在棚屋诊所里，一把扫帚靠在排满瓶子的架子旁；一台柴油发电机咳着，它不规则的嗡鸣是小小的安慰。她擦掉桌上一片黄色的石灰污渍，把一只色拉碗挪到一边，伸手去拿漂白剂以保持房间卫生。她知道，反复执行谨慎的步骤是治理混乱的唯一良方。真相很明白：物资的不足可能是致命的。然而她不会让暴力或绝望压制她的团队。当一个男人抓着胸口尖叫时，她没有惊醒于恐惧之中；她行动了。她的精明，从最好的意义上说，是务实的。她用一把扳手修好一台旧马达坏掉的离合器，一处构造接一处构造地修，好让冰箱运转、让抗生素保持低温。在这里娱乐是奢侈品。野餐、一局国际象棋、一份在炉火边背诵的出版物——这样舒适的乐趣属于另一种人生。尽管如此，团队还是找到了一种令人愉快、甚至舒适的节奏。鞋匠的儿子起初不情愿，后来变得大胆。"这是我的角色，"他说，而且是认真的。他们朝着扩张努力，先培训五名、再培训十三名当地志愿者，每人都获得一枚徽章，上面有一张小小的进度图表。一个社会主义议会急于遵循新的记录保存方式，派了一个代表团乘铁路前来。这位医生的专长不是光鲜，而是把重复的事做对。她从一份充满困难的预算中榨取每一滴；她开玩笑说，报销会在下一次火山喷发之后到账。一天夜里，一个暴君般的谣言传开——说诊所将要停办——但被证明是假的。这位护士只对粗心严厉，让真相追随她的话语。到旱季结束时，疾病已在退却，那曾经是溃烂与伤害之地的小小岬角口，成了健康的避风港。"我们没能根除恐惧，"她对聚集的人群说，对自己的口误笑了笑，"但我们确实根除了最严重的病情。"那粒药丸、一名能行走病人的鞋、每个康复孩子苏醒的痕迹——她说，所有这一切都比任何留声机的歌或色拉晚餐更有价值。真相，严酷而纯粹，赢了。在她后来写的一篇随笔里，这位护士把疏忽称作一种罪，是任何医者的桎梏。她描述了一例骨科病人如何几乎陷入精神错乱，一个孩子的尖叫如何划破夜空，以及她如何挤过恐慌、守住镇定。一位长着灰胡子的穆斯林长者，是她女婿的导师，帮她安全地存放一次性手套。诊所的区域化是一张像枝桠般蔓延的树状网络，让援助抵达远方。没有任何怀疑的浮渣能扰乱她的决心。黄昏时一堆旧木箱的柴堆燃烧着，余烬在黑暗中闪烁；连那虚拟的'要是当初'也失去了刺痛，希望如一条触须，伸向明天。

text: The presence of the whole crew was required before the lunar rehearsal could begin. Mission control had chosen to automate much of the launch sequence, yet a human hand would still steer the craft at the critical moment. The pilot, pale with fatigue, adjusted her posture in the seat and felt a tremble run through the hull as the engines built thrust. "Stay accurate," the flight director said, his voice telling and calm. "One error could negate a year of work." An array of screens glowed nearby; on each, a linear graph traced the rocket's position. The simulate program would stimulate every fault the engineers could imagine, from a toxic fuel leak to a collide warning when two objects drew near in orbit. Nobody wanted a scandal, and nobody wanted to perish. The impetus for this mission had begun with a single vision: to place a small array of sensors on the virgin surface of a distant crater. Critics called the budget exaggeration, an ostentation of national pride, but approval had come at last after a long pending review. The team refused to let any flaw go unaddressed; they would rectify each one, thoroughly, before the real flight. In the pause, the pilot let her mind jog back to her childhood. She would slumber under a quilt while her father, a sympathetic man, told her of caravans crossing the desert, of a tuck of stars near the shore, of the lunar light that made the pepper plants in their garden gleam. He had a counterfeit pocket watch, bought cheap, yet to her it was a treasure. He would alight from his old motorcar, wail a funny tune, and tuck her in. That tendency to dream had steered her whole life. Now, on the simulator, an alarm cried out. A frail line on the screen turned red — an eruption of pressure in tank six. Her refusal to panic saved the run. She did not waver. With a quick thrust of the control stick she corrected the array, and the linear path settled. The director gave a slow nod of approval. "Accomplishment," he said, "is mostly the refusal to quit." Outside, near a flood-control channel by the shore, an athlete jogged past, unaware of the drama within. A maid swept the steps of the old telegraph office; a roller of fresh paint dried on a nearby wall. The world went on, weak to the wonders overhead. But inside the dome, the crew felt the irony: to reach the cold and additional silence of space, they had to master every inconvenient detail on the ground. "Enough for tonight," the director said. "We have a clearer position now." The conversion from fear to confidence was nearly complete. As the lights dimmed, the pilot paused, intent, and let one private prayer rise. The lunar dream, once a frail puzzle, now felt feasible. A pair of technicians shut the hatch, and the simulated ship went dark, its vision banked for another day. With some perplexity, the crew gripped the rails tightly, unwilling to let fear perturb them. To shorten the wait, the director told a strategic joke about a fridge full of grape juice and a single cold shot of soda. It was a blessing to laugh. Temporal worries would deplete anyone; they had to make peace with delay. A note on the wall, printed with a stencil, listed the aerodynamics drills and the elimination of each fault. Outside, near a road of fresh bitumen, an artillery museum stood like a haven; a caravan of trucks passed, marked ex works for an overseas importer. One could surmise, even without a course in botany, that the team's quiet idiosyncrasy was patience. The agency would indemnify any loss and would not persecute a single honest error. A small daily allowance, and the promise to promote the steady ones, kept spirits high. Unable to sleep, the pilot watched an old movie about forty pioneers, and felt the heat of ambition return.
translation: 在月球演练开始之前，全体机组人员必须到场。任务控制中心选择将大部分发射程序自动化，但在关键时刻仍需人手来驾驶飞船。飞行员因疲惫而面色苍白，她在座位上调整姿势，感到当引擎积聚推力时一阵颤动穿过船体。"保持精确，"飞行指挥说，他的声音很有分量却镇定，"一个差错就可能抵消一年的工作。"附近一排屏幕发着光；每块屏上，一条线性的曲线图描绘着火箭的位置。模拟程序会激发工程师能想象到的每一种故障，从有毒燃料泄漏到两个物体在轨道上接近时的碰撞警报。没人想要丑闻，也没人想丧命。这次任务的推动力始于一个单纯的愿景：在一个遥远陨石坑未被触碰的表面上放置一小组传感器。批评者称这预算是夸张，是民族自豪感的炫耀，但在漫长的搁置审查之后，批准终于到来。团队拒绝让任何缺陷无人处理；他们要在真正飞行之前彻底纠正每一个。在间歇中，飞行员让思绪慢跑回童年。她会盖着被子沉睡，而她那富有同情心的父亲，给她讲述穿越沙漠的商队，讲述海岸边一簇星辰，讲述使他们园中辣椒植株闪亮的月光。他有一只廉价买来的假冒怀表，但对她而言那是珍宝。他会从旧汽车上下来，哀号般地哼一支滑稽的曲子，给她盖好被子。那种爱做梦的倾向引导了她的一生。如今，在模拟器上，一个警报响起。屏幕上一条脆弱的线变红了——六号燃料箱压力骤升。她拒绝惊慌，救了这次演练。她没有动摇。她迅速一推操纵杆，修正了阵列，线性的航线稳定了下来。指挥缓缓点头表示认可。"成就，"他说，"大多是拒绝放弃。"外面，海岸边一条防洪水道旁，一名运动员慢跑而过，对这内部的戏剧浑然不觉。一名女仆扫着旧电报局的台阶；附近墙上一滚新刷的油漆正在晾干。世界照常运转，对头顶的奇迹无力察觉。但在穹顶内，机组感到了讽刺：为了抵达太空那寒冷而额外的寂静，他们必须在地面上掌握每一个不便的细节。"今晚够了，"指挥说，"我们现在的处境更清晰了。"从恐惧到自信的转变几近完成。当灯光暗下时，飞行员停顿，神情专注，让一句私下的祈祷升起。那个月球之梦，曾是一个脆弱的谜题，如今感觉可行了。一对技术员关上舱门，模拟的飞船陷入黑暗，它的愿景为又一天而搁置。带着几分困惑，机组紧紧抓住栏杆，不愿让恐惧扰乱他们。为了缩短等待，指挥讲了一个有策略的笑话，说一台装满葡萄汁的冰箱和一杯冰苏打。能笑出来是一种福分。世俗的忧虑会耗尽任何人；他们必须与延误握手言和。墙上一张用蜡纸印出的便条，列着空气动力学演练和每个故障的排除。外面，靠近一条新铺沥青的路，一座炮兵博物馆像避难所一样矗立；一队卡车驶过，标注着为一位海外进口商交货的字样。即使不修一门植物学课程，人们也能推测，这支团队安静的特质就是耐心。代理机构会赔偿任何损失，绝不会因一个诚实的错误而迫害任何人。一小笔每日津贴，以及提拔稳健者的承诺，让士气高昂。飞行员无法入睡，看了一部讲述四十位拓荒者的老电影，感到雄心的热度重新回来。

text: In the last year of his lifetime, the old thinker, an upright and humane man, settled into a quiet residence near a field where a plough still turned the earth. His health was failing, and a profound anxiety, almost a jealousy of younger minds, would sometimes madden him. Yet he taught to the very end, since teaching gave him pleasure that nothing else could match. "Everybody must defend the liberty to think," he would say, gazing through the window at a swan on the pond, a horse at the fence, a bird in the granary eaves. His style of speech was hearty and genuine, never plausible nonsense dressed as wisdom. Each session, he would dispense one idea, perfect in its symmetry, and let his students sharpen it. He had no weapon but reason; revenge and political faction held nothing for him. He spoke of an episode from his youth, when a cruel accident had nearly killed him. A craft he sailed had struck gravel near the shore, and only the genuine courage of a fellow passenger had saved him. "Such things," he said, "teach you that nowhere is safe, and that we hold our possession of life on loan." The memory still made him gaze into the fireplace, evidently moved. His ardent students would not let his thought perish. They consume his every word, eager to obtain a manuscript he had long postponed publishing. An Australian scholar arrived to appoint herself his editor; respectively, others took on the index, the notes, the specification of each footnote. The likelihood of finishing in time was small, but they refused to be mistaken about their duty. The thinker, shrewd to the last, would tread carefully around any flaw in his old logic. "Do not segregate the heart from the mind," he warned. "To exterminate feeling is to ruin thought." He had once seen a colony of swans, white against a grey sky, and the marvellous sight had become a private weapon against despair. Now, in his rusty old age, he returned to it often. One six-line poem he had written, fluorescent in its clarity, spoke of a god of small mercies and of the sacrifice that any worthy life demands. He would not boast of it. As October cooled the air, an actor friend came to read the verse aloud, and a withdrawal of sorrow seemed to follow. The thinker felt, for once, no inconvenience, no commonsense fear. He had done his work satisfactorily, actively, and with merchandise of the soul that no liquor or rusty grief could spoil. "I have nothing left to prove," he said, his liberty intact. Outside, the swan swam on; the bird sang; and the old man, content at last, watched the year turn. His neighbor, a practical farmer, once joked that it was ludicrous to fling pesticide on a healthy crop; prevention, he said, was exactly the better path. The outturn of the harvest, recorded module by module on a sheet of cardboard, exceeded every estimate. As chairman of the local organization, the thinker had an obligation he would never postpone: to teach grammar, even the humble transitive verb, to the village children. They would swim in summer, play volleyball, go camping, and chop onion for the campfire stew. When a tyre went flat or a leakage of rain broke the deadlock of a dull afternoon, he simply smiled.
translation: 在他一生的最后一年里，这位年迈的思想家——一个正直而仁慈的人——在一片仍有犁翻土的田野附近的安静住所安顿下来。他的健康每况愈下，一种深沉的忧虑，近乎对年轻头脑的嫉妒，有时会让他发狂。然而他直到最后仍在教学，因为教学给他一种别的任何东西都无法比拟的快乐。"每个人都必须捍卫思考的自由，"他会说，透过窗户凝望池塘上的一只天鹅、栅栏边的一匹马、谷仓檐下的一只鸟。他说话的风格热忱而真诚，绝非披着智慧外衣的似是而非的胡言。每一堂课，他都会施予一个观念，其对称之美完美无瑕，并让学生去打磨它。除了理性，他没有别的武器；复仇与政治派系于他毫无意义。他讲起年轻时的一段往事，当时一场残酷的意外几乎要了他的命。他驾驶的一艘小船在岸边撞上碎石，全靠同船一位旅客真正的勇气才救了他。"这种事，"他说，"教你明白没有任何地方是安全的，我们对生命的拥有不过是借来的。"这段记忆仍让他凝望着壁炉，显然为之动容。他那些热心的学生不会让他的思想消亡。他们吞咽他的每一个字，急切地想得到一份他长久推迟出版的手稿。一位澳大利亚学者前来，自荐当他的编辑；其余的人则分别承担索引、注释、每条脚注的详述。按时完成的可能性很小，但他们拒绝在自己的职责上搞错。这位思想家精明到最后，会小心地绕开他旧逻辑中的任何瑕疵。"不要把心与脑隔离开，"他告诫，"消灭情感就是毁掉思想。"他曾见过一群天鹅，在灰色天空映衬下洁白如雪，那绝妙的景象成了他对抗绝望的私人武器。如今，在他生锈般的暮年，他常常重温它。他写过一首六行的诗，清澈得如同荧光，讲述一位主管细小慈悲的神，以及任何值得过的人生所要求的牺牲。他不会以此自夸。当十月使空气转凉，一位演员朋友前来朗读这首诗，悲伤的退潮似乎随之而来。思想家这一次感到没有不便，没有出于常识的恐惧。他已圆满地、积极地完成了自己的工作，带着任何酒或生锈的悲伤都无法败坏的灵魂的货品。"我再没有什么需要证明的了，"他说，自由完好无损。屋外，天鹅继续游着；鸟儿歌唱；这位老人终于心满意足，看着这一年流转。他的邻居是个务实的农民，曾打趣说，往健康的庄稼上抛洒杀虫剂是荒唐的；他说，预防恰恰是更好的路。收成的卸货情况一格一格地记在一张硬纸板上，超出了所有的估计。作为当地组织的主席，这位思想家有一项他从不推迟的义务：教村里的孩子语法，连那不起眼的及物动词也教。他们夏天游泳，打排球，去野营，为篝火炖菜切洋葱。当一只轮胎瘪了，或一场雨的渗漏打破了一个沉闷下午的僵局，他只是微笑。

text: When the radio announcer first took the job, finance was the last thing on his mind. He had been a careful, skillful young fellow with much courage but little money, and his only practicable plan was to please the public and hope for the best. Aboard a ferry one October morning, age eight in spirit if not in years, he had decided to become a voice that the nation would trust. "Necessarily," he told a fellow passenger, "I must speak accurately, or I am nothing." That resolution, laudable and firm, never wavered. Except for one absence caused by hepatitis, he had not missed a broadcast in eight years. His routine was strict: he would arrive early, list the headlines, and separately rehearse each summary aloud. He refused to be arrogant; arrogance, he believed, was an ugly streak that could drown a man's better self. Regarding the news itself, he tried to comprehend it deeply before he spoke. A story about official bribery, a hostage crisis, the prosecute of a snob who had tried to steal public funds, the monstrous evolution of a scandal at the aquarium board: each of these he treated with equal care. He would not let a triumphant tone creep in, nor would he smash a reputation lightly. One evening, a colleague tried to tantalize him with a rumor about a wholesaler who, it was said, would finance a corrupt campaign. "Alas," the announcer said, "I cannot air what I cannot prove. The disappearance of one fact can mislead a whole nation." He kept a small list of principles taped above his terminal, written in straight, careful lines. The software that ran the studio sometimes failed, and on those nights he would grope for a backup, refusing to drown the broadcast in dead air. He had studied biology and geometry at school, and a little of the temperate logic of those subjects stayed with him; he liked things that correspond to reason. When the petrol shortage struck and prices began to bob upward, he reported it without panic, separating real distribution problems from jumble and rumor. His lovely, refined manner made even bad news bearable. People said his voice could surround a frightened city like a refuge. He never sought to horrify, never let a howl of outrage into his tone. In time he was offered a promotion to the central desk, and though the suspense of the decision kept him awake, he accepted with a careful, indirectly grateful nod to his old mentor, the mister who had first taught him to speak. "You did not necessarily teach me facts," he wrote in a letter. "You taught me to arouse trust, and to keep it." He sealed the note, asked for a receipt, and posted it that very day. Years later, near a quiet tomb on a hill, he would recline and recall that the whole long enterprise had begun with one boy's stubborn refusal to lie. He refused to hound a source piecemeal; instead he would enquire closely, decipher every decimal of a budget, and seek the concurrence of two editors before air. Low morale could affect a whole newsroom, so he fed his team good nourishment and humor to prevent any relapse into cynicism. A touch of refinement marked his prose; he would never suspend his standards. He loved the scent of fresh ink. After a reception one night, he wrote a caption for a photo of his fiance beside a wall of white gypsum, lit by soft light.
