From the occidental desk to a long trek across the country, he cast aside the fear of failure. 'Ditto,' his editor would say to every careful choice, and the young man took flight under his mentor's wing.
translation: 当这位电台播音员第一次接下这份工作时，财务是他最不在意的事。他曾是个细心、娴熟的年轻人，有的是勇气，钱却不多，他唯一可行的计划就是取悦公众，并寄望于最好的结果。在十月一个清晨的渡轮上，即使年龄不到、心境却如八岁孩童般，他已决定要成为一个全国都信任的声音。"必然地，"他对一位同船旅客说，"我必须准确地说话，否则我什么都不是。"那个值得称赞而坚定的决心，从未动摇。除了一次因肝炎而缺席之外，八年来他没有错过一次播报。他的惯例很严格：他会早早到达，列出头条，并分别把每段摘要大声排练一遍。他拒绝傲慢；他相信傲慢是一道丑陋的纹路，会淹没一个人更好的自我。至于新闻本身，他在开口前力求深刻地理解它。一桩官员受贿的报道、一场人质危机、对一个曾试图窃取公款的势利小人的起诉、水族馆董事会一桩丑闻可怕的演变：他对每一件都同等谨慎对待。他不会让得意洋洋的腔调溜进来，也不会轻率地砸毁谁的名声。一天傍晚，一位同事想用一则传闻逗弄他，说某个批发商据传会资助一场腐败的竞选。"唉，"播音员说，"我无法播出我无法证实的东西。一个事实的消失就能误导整个国家。"他在终端机上方贴着一小张原则清单，用笔直、工整的字迹写成。运行演播室的软件有时会出故障，那些夜里他会摸索备用方案，拒绝让广播淹没在静默之中。他在学校学过生物学和几何学，那些科目中温和的逻辑有一点留在了他身上；他喜欢符合理性的东西。当汽油短缺来袭、价格开始上下浮动时，他报道此事毫不惊慌，把真正的分配问题与杂乱的谣言区分开来。他可爱而优雅的举止让坏消息都变得可以承受。人们说他的声音能像避难所一样环绕一座受惊的城市。他从不想制造恐惧，从不让一声愤怒的嚎叫进入他的语调。后来他获得晋升到中央台的机会，尽管这个决定的悬念让他彻夜难眠，他还是接受了，向他的老导师——那位最初教他说话的先生——含蓄而感激地点了点头。"你未必教了我事实，"他在一封信里写道，"你教我去唤起信任，并守住它。"他封好便笺，索取一张收据，当天就寄了出去。多年以后，在一座山上安静的坟墓旁，他会斜倚下来，回想这整桩漫长的事业，竟始于一个男孩固执地拒绝撒谎。他拒绝零敲碎打地追逼一个消息来源；相反，他会仔细打听，破译预算的每一个小数，并在播出前寻求两位编辑的一致同意。低落的士气会影响整个新闻编辑室，于是他给团队提供良好的滋养和幽默，防止任何重新陷入愤世嫉俗的旧病复发。他的文字带着一丝优雅；他绝不会暂停自己的标准。他喜欢新鲜油墨的气味。一天夜里一场招待会之后，他为一张照片写了说明文字，照片里他的未婚妻站在一面白色石膏墙旁，被柔和的灯光照着。从西方编辑台到一次横穿全国的长途跋涉，他抛开了对失败的恐惧。'同上，'他的编辑会对每一个谨慎的选择这样说，而这位年轻人在导师的羽翼下展翅高飞。

theme: Consensus at the Renewable Energy Guild
text: After months of debate, the consensus inside the central guild was that the nation could no longer waste its resources. The custodian of the old building, a calm man who had worked there alone for years, slipped on his earphone and listened to the chairman of the renewable energy committee speak. "This," the chairman said, jumping straight to the point, "is purely a question of survival. Here, in this very location, we will either thrive or fade." Everyone leaned inside to hear. The plan was generic in outline but precise in detail. On each storey of the building, redundant heating systems would be replaced; a smart program would filter demand and cut the mileage that delivery trucks logged every successive day. A columnist who wrote for a major channel had called the scheme inalienable to the city's future, and few wished to impede it. The custodian, listening from the back, felt a strange zeal rise in him. He had spent his lifetime watching waste pile up, and now, actually, change seemed to loom near. "We hereby declare," the chairman read from the manifesto, "that dependability matters more than show." There was an occasional cough, a wink between two engineers, the rotary hum of an old fan. A graphic on the screen, almost like a moth circling a lantern, displayed the accumulative savings. Insofar as the math held, the limitation was no longer technical but political. A faction loyal to the old grocer-merchants resisted; arrogance and arrogance alone, the chairman thought, kept them from agreeing. But the merciful truth was that even they would benefit. After the vote, enrolment in the new training sessions jumped. A registered nurse from the hospital nearby spoke about disease and how a polluted river could trample public health; a Frenchman who ran the lubrication depot promised cleaner oil; a pianist offered to play at the launch to motivate the crowd. The diaphragm of the old ventilation system was repaired, the bald patch of rust on the bridge railing was scraped, and a derelict shed behind the residence was cleared. No one would pollute the channel again, they agreed, nor remit the duty to future generations. "We must not grope in the dark," the chairman said, his cell phone buzzing with messages. "A succinct plan, repetitive in its discipline, will carry us southward toward warmer markets and account for every cent." The custodian smiled. He thought of a white peacock he had once seen, its fragrance of pride, its arrogance; and he thought, too, of how a single cell of effort, multiplied across a nation, could let an intellectual dream finally arise. As the meeting closed, a pleasant calm settled over the room. The fraction of doubters had shrunk; the bookshelf of old excuses was finally being emptied. After all the noise, the slowdown of waste had truly begun. A retired colonel, married for decades, rose to comprehend the plan, refusing to kneel to old inertia. He would not boast, yet he made one jump of faith and backed the vote. A plumber checked the pipes' thickness; a valve turned anticlockwise sealed the last leak. There would be no dismissal of staff, only new work. As a faithful listener to every meeting, he treated the reform not as a game but as a duty, and even joked that the cafeteria's cheese would taste sweeter now.
translation: 经过数月的辩论，中央行会内部的共识是：国家再也不能浪费它的资源了。这座老建筑的管理人是个沉静的人，独自在这里工作了多年，他戴上耳机，听可再生能源委员会的主席讲话。"这，"主席直截了当地说，"纯粹是一个生存的问题。就在这里，就在这个地点，我们要么兴旺，要么消亡。"所有人都向里凑近去听。这个计划在轮廓上是笼统的，但在细节上很精确。建筑的每一层，多余的供暖系统都将被更换；一套智能程序会过滤需求，削减送货卡车一天天累积的里程。一位为某大频道撰稿的专栏作家曾称这一方案对城市的未来不可剥夺，几乎没人愿意去阻碍它。管理人在后排听着，心中升起一种奇异的热情。他一生都在看着废物堆积，而如今，变化竟似乎真的逼近了。"我们特此宣布，"主席从宣言中念道，"可靠性比排场更重要。"偶尔有人咳嗽，两个工程师之间眨了眨眼，一台旧风扇发出旋转的嗡鸣。屏幕上一幅图像，几乎像一只绕着灯笼飞的蛾子，显示着累积的节省额。只要数字成立，限制就不再是技术上的，而是政治上的。一个忠于旧杂货商的派别表示抗拒；主席想，唯有傲慢、纯粹的傲慢，才使他们不肯同意。但仁慈的真相是：连他们也会受益。投票之后，新培训课程的报名人数猛增。附近医院一位注册护士谈到疾病，谈到一条被污染的河流会如何践踏公众健康；一位经营润滑油库的法国人承诺提供更清洁的油；一位钢琴家提出在启动仪式上演奏以激励众人。旧通风系统的振动膜被修好了，桥栏上那块光秃秃的锈斑被刮掉了，住宅后面一间被遗弃的棚屋被清理干净。他们一致同意，再不会有人污染这条水道，也不会把责任推给后代。"我们不能在黑暗中摸索，"主席说，他的手机响个不停。"一个简洁、在纪律上不断重复的计划，会带我们向南走向更温暖的市场，并对每一分钱都有所交代。"管理人笑了。他想起曾经见过的一只白孔雀，它那股骄傲的气味，它的傲慢；他也想到，一个个努力的细胞，在全国范围内成倍累加，如何能让一个知识分子的梦想最终升起。会议结束时，一种愉快的平静笼罩了房间。怀疑者的比例已经缩小；那一书架的旧借口终于被清空。在所有的喧嚣之后，浪费的放缓，确实已经开始了。一位退役上校，结婚已数十年，起身理解了这个计划，拒绝向陈旧的惰性下跪。他不会自夸，却作出了一次信念的跳跃，支持了这次表决。一名管子工检查了管道的厚度；一个逆时针拧动的阀门封住了最后的漏点。不会有员工被开除，只会有新的工作。作为每次会议忠实的听众，他把这场改革看作一项职责而非一场游戏，甚至开玩笑说餐厅的干酪现在尝起来会更甜。

text:
Last September, on the anniversary of my grandfather's death, I made a deep and rather personal ascent along the Scottish coast to visit the winery he once owned. According to family doctrine, faith and hard work could offset any debt, and whenever the climate turned humid I would remember his idiomatic sayings. He was a craftsman of permanent reputation, a man of fine workmanship whose authority no one dared infringe.

The morning was bright, the air intermittent with mist that began to thaw the frost on the sand. A surge of memory hit me sideways as someone considerate and helpful pushed the heavy door. Inside, an orderly occupant set a tray of peach and chestnut tarts beside a quartz prism that scattered scarlet light across the room. He pressed a button, and an amateur played a flute while a recording with a subtitle explained the history. The canteen at the back stayed open to all, its counter creased with the wrinkle of a thousand polishings, and on the wall hung a rusted torpedo, a relic of the war years.

In actuality, running the winery was a herculean feat. The basic implementation of new rules, the ratification of a multilateral trade deal, and the consistent regularity of the harvest all complicate the work. There is always the possibility that a rash decision will prolong a crisis or that pessimism will pervert good judgment. A symptom of trouble might be a drastic drop in the ranking of our vintage, which would form a fresh debt.

My grandfather kept his faith eternal. He would mutter about the imperialist past, about the legion of officials who tried to censor or abet corruption, who would grudge an honest man his bonus. He warned that to assist a pickpocket was the work of the devil, an act fit only for a savage or an ass.

Yet the place still flourishes. We offer a generous discount and a homestay program; visitors win a lottery, admire the orchard's blossom, play poker by the fire, and indicate their liking for the wine. The probable derivation of its name comes from an old word for the deep coast. I felt no lasting grudge, only gratitude, and I knew that, inapt as I once was with a knife and a bag of tools, I would keep his legacy alive past every deadline, and never alter it.
translation:
去年九月，在祖父忌日的周年纪念上，我沿着苏格兰海岸做了一次深沉而相当个人化的攀登，去探访他曾经拥有的酿酒厂。按照家族的教义，信仰与勤奋能抵消任何债务；每当气候变得潮湿，我便会想起他那些惯用的口头语。他是一位享有永久声誉的手艺人，技艺精湛，其权威无人敢侵犯。

那个清晨阳光明亮，空气中弥漫着断断续续的薄雾，开始消融沙地上的霜。当一位体贴又乐于助人的人推开那扇厚重的门时，一阵记忆的浪潮从侧面向我袭来。屋内，一位井然有序的居住者端来一托盘桃子和栗子馅饼，旁边放着一块把猩红色光线散射满屋的石英棱镜。他按下一个按钮，一位业余爱好者吹起了长笛，同时一段带字幕的录音讲述着这里的历史。后面的食堂向所有人开放，柜台上布满了千万次擦拭留下的皱痕，墙上挂着一枚生锈的鱼雷，那是战争年代的遗物。

实际上，经营酿酒厂是一项力大无比的伟业。新规的基本执行、一项多边贸易协定的批准，以及收成前后一致的规律性，都使这项工作变得复杂。总存在这样的可能性：一个轻率的决定会拖延一场危机，或者悲观主义会扭曲良好的判断。麻烦的一个征兆，可能是我们酒款排名的急剧下跌，那会形成一笔新的债务。

祖父的信仰始终不朽。他会低声抱怨那段帝国主义的过往，抱怨那一大批试图审查或纵容腐败的官员，他们会吝惜给一个诚实的人发奖金。他告诫说，帮助一个扒手是魔鬼的勾当，只配得上未开化的野人或蠢驴去做。

然而这地方依旧繁荣。我们提供慷慨的折扣和寄宿家庭项目；游客们抽彩中奖，欣赏果园的花簇，在炉火旁打扑克，并表达他们对这酒的喜爱。一位手艺人告诉我，它名字很可能的起源来自一个表示深邃海岸的古词。我心中没有持久的怨恨，只有感激；我知道，尽管我曾经笨拙地拿着刀和一袋工具，我也会让他的遗产在每一个最后期限之后继续存活，绝不更改它。

text: One overcast morning in November, a visitor named Greta arrived in our small village to clothe her great aspiration in real fieldwork. She had no jeep, only a battered lorry and a mobile phone, yet her endeavour to study the local environment was substantial. Whenever she could, she would crouch beside a mossy bench, watching a swift dart past the corner of the lawn, while moss spread over an old estate wall. With discretion she would not allow the oncoming madness of tourist crowds to disturb the place she came to adore. Her future, she said, depended on this present procedure of patient observation, and she kept a varied encyclopedia of notes. Her behavior was orderly: she would blink at the sun, freshen her mind with cold water, and own up to any uncertain finding. A wrinkled clause in her grant required a chairperson to retrieve every diagram and piece of information she produced, so she sent each gift of data ahead via a tiny projector. The hump of a distant hill, the violet sky, a thorn-edged path past a frock-clad scarecrow, the buckle on her bag, all earned a compliment in her diary. She would mutter no allegation of injustice when funding was uncertain; instead she would devour her homework, fiddle with a tool, and present a calm aspect even when antagonism flared. Her old mistress in alchemy had taught her that experimentation, not rapture or mere manipulation of numbers, brings discovery. The villagers spoke a soft dialect that she found beautiful, and she never grew unduly impatient with it. Once, a puncture in the lorry tyre and an antagonist's dishonorable trick delayed her, but she did not buy excuses. She would charge ahead, more valid than ever, slot her tickets and visa into a drawer, and crouch again. A cashier in the next village, recovering from tuberculosis, would adore her delicious bread and edible berries, sharing kin-like warmth. She studied a digit on her ration card, a partition between fields, the aerial above a fire-engine, and even the embroidery on a buckle. Each clause of her novel report, though it left her unconscious of fame, recorded the plague years, the crazy delight of an ad-free dawn, and the buckle of frost. Upside-down in the grass, she would take a slow breath, thank the morning, and feel her crucial discovery blossom.
translation: 十一月一个阴沉的早晨，一位名叫格蕾塔的访客来到我们的小村庄，要把她伟大的抱负付诸真正的田野工作。她没有吉普车，只有一辆破旧的卡车和一部手机，但她研究当地环境的努力是相当可观的。只要有可能，她就会蹲在长满苔藓的长椅旁，看着一只雨燕掠过草坪的角落，而苔藓爬满了一堵旧庄园的墙。出于谨慎，她不愿让蜂拥而至的游客的疯狂打扰她前来钟爱的这个地方。她说，她的未来取决于眼下这套耐心观察的程序，她记下了内容多样、像百科全书般的笔记。她的举止井然有序：她会对着太阳眨眼，用冷水让头脑清醒，并坦承任何不确定的发现。她经费里一条带皱褶的条款要求一位主席收回她产出的每一张图表和每一份信息，于是她通过一台小投影仪把每份数据礼物送往前方。远处山丘的隆起、紫罗兰色的天空、一条被荆棘环绕、经过身着旧外套的稻草人的小路、她包上的扣环，都在她的日记里得到一句赞美。当经费不确定时，她不会嘟囔任何关于不公的指控；相反，她会埋头做功课，摆弄工具，即使敌意爆发也保持镇定的神情。她从前教她炼金术的女主人曾教导她，带来发现的是实验，而非狂喜或仅仅对数字的操纵。村民们说着一种她觉得很美的柔和方言，她从不会对此过分地不耐烦。有一次，卡车轮胎被扎破，加上一个对手不光彩的诡计耽误了她，但她不买账找借口。她会奋勇向前，比以往更加坚定有效，把车票和签证塞进抽屉，再次蹲下身。隔壁村一位刚从肺结核中康复的收银员会喜爱她美味的面包和可食用的浆果，分享如亲人般的温暖。她研究着配给卡上的一个数字、田地间的隔断、消防车上方的天线，甚至扣环上的刺绣。她那部小说般报告的每一条款，尽管让她对名声毫无知觉，却记录了瘟疫的岁月、无广告黎明的疯狂喜悦，以及霜冻的扣合。她倒立在草丛中，深深呼吸，感谢这个清晨，感到她那至关重要的发现正在绽放。

text: On a cold January morning, a research corps boarded a closed vessel bound for waters where dolphins converge. The sender of our orders, an authoritative registrar from the United Kingdom, had a particular and thoughtful plan that left no matter to chance. Each crew member would commute daily between cabins, and oneself alone could not run the ship; a whole troop had to assign duties and admit mistakes agreeably. We had to hasten, for the voyage was unique and the supply of fuel was barely abundant. The captain, my brother, would not gamble with safety, and his respectful manner made even a difficulty seem slightly easier. Notwithstanding the surprising weather, our coverage of the region was duly comparable to any prior survey. We would detect each dolphin by its whisker-like markings and the way it would revolve and distinguish itself from the troop. To recollect every sighting, we kept a recorder running; the camera's projection on a screen helped us literally count each fin. The dairy of the ship's galley smelled of chocolate and a spoonful of cocoa, while a shower of spray would explode over the bow and litter the deck with foam. One instant a raw wound on a sailor's hand needed peroxide and a tranquilizer; the next, an irritate-prone cook would mutter about a phony invoice. We had no time to deserve rest. A weed of doubt could not converge with our resolve, and discomfortable nights notwithstanding, agitation never won. Our usage of new instant tools made the work an elevation of pure oceanography. A particular bullish sponsor, vehement about clean seas, would not consign the project to failure, nor would the violation of any rule go without clarification. The fuse of curiosity, the fertilizer of patience, and a solution of trust kept us united, not disunite. Ashore at last, partly tired, we shouldered each box and cart, paid tribute to the sea, and made our decision: the carbohydrate-rich meals, the kindergarten of young interns, the elevation of every wave were worth it. A pickpocket could steal a wallet, but no thorn, no rifle shot, no aggregation of storms could steal the indebtedness we felt. The agreeably stowage of gear, the closed hold, the can of supplies, all said one thing: a thoughtful crew, sustained by faith, can survive. A protestant chaplain blessed our ball of rope; a kindergarten teacher had once told me a trick: stay humble. Back at the house ashore, we stocked the freezer, watched an old actress on television, and joked that the coast had more arable land than the sea. Though I felt ambivalent about leaving, and our route home was indirect, we said our thanks, and sailed home.
translation: 在一个寒冷的一月清晨，一支研究队登上一艘封闭的船只，驶向海豚汇聚的水域。下达命令的是一位来自英国的权威注册官，他有一个特别而周到的计划，没有把任何事情交给运气。每名船员每天要在舱室之间往返，单凭一个人无法驾驶整船；必须由一整队人分配职责并欣然承认错误。我们必须赶紧，因为这次航行独一无二，而燃料的供应也仅仅勉强充足。船长是我的兄弟，他不会拿安全去赌博，他那恭敬的态度让再大的困难看起来也稍稍容易些。尽管天气出人意料，我们对该区域的覆盖范围理应可与以往任何调查相媲美。我们通过海豚胡须般的斑纹，以及它们旋转、从队群中区分自己的方式来辨认每一只海豚。为了回忆起每一次目击，我们让录音机一直运转；摄像机投射在屏幕上的影像帮助我们逐字逐条数清每一片鳍。船上厨房散发着巧克力和一勺可可的香味，浪花的喷溅会爆发般越过船头，把泡沫撒落在甲板上。前一刻，一名水手手上的鲜血伤口需要双氧水和镇静剂；下一刻，一位易怒的厨师又会嘟囔着抱怨一张假发票。我们没有时间去享受应得的休息。一丝疑虑之草无法与我们的决心汇合，尽管夜晚令人不适，焦躁却从未占上风。我们对新仪器工具的使用让这项工作成为纯粹海洋学的升华。一位特别看涨的赞助人，对清洁海洋充满激情，不会把这个项目交付给失败，任何规则的违反也不会缺少澄清。好奇之引信、耐心之肥料和信任之溶液让我们团结而不分裂。最后上岸时，我们半身疲惫，扛起每一个箱子和推车，向大海致敬，并做出我们的决定：富含碳水化合物的饭菜、年轻实习生的幼儿园、每一道波浪的高度，都是值得的。扒手能偷走钱包，但没有荆棘、没有步枪的枪声、没有风暴的聚合能偷走我们所感到的亏欠之情。装备的妥善堆放、封闭的货舱、那一罐补给，全都说明了一件事：一支周到的船员，在信念的支撑下，能够生存。一位新教随船牧师为我们的绳球祝福；一位幼儿园老师曾告诉我一个诀窍：保持谦卑。回到岸上的房子里，我们把冰柜装满，在电视上看一位年老的女演员演出，还开玩笑说海岸的可耕地比大海还多。尽管我对离开心情矛盾，回家的路线也很迂回，我们还是道了谢，然后扬帆回家。

text: Nevertheless, the young farmhand who longed to act in a drama never imagined he would represent the academy on stage. He was proud yet poor, and his manhood had been shaped by costly years of constant labor. A foreman, traditional and strongly built, would incite him to quit, but a kindly potter and a wandering constable would discuss his talent and goad him onward. "Do not refuse the chance," they would say, "nor abandon it; embark on the role." The cue came on an odd, abrupt evening. The villain of the play, an intelligent but malicious magistrate, had fallen ill, and no substitute seemed available. The number of trained actors was small, the quota of understudies unobtainable, and the academy, generally prosperous, faced a peculiar crisis. So they sent for the farmhand. He had a shallow draft of the lines, scribbled on a torn formula sheet, and he had to meditate quickly. His suspicion that he would fail was strong, yet a foremost teacher, apt and benevolent, said cordially, "You serve the play; the play will serve you. Settle your nerves." Within the hour he had to incorporate gesture, voice, and a continuous flow of feeling. He felt a dominant fear dangle over him, and his consciousness wavered. The play's highlight required him to wield a wooden sword, brace his arm, and then sob with grief as the deceit of the court was revealed. He would not repent the risk. The drama was infectious; the audience, intelligent and sophisticated, leaned forward. The complexity of the plot, full of contradiction, suspicion, and superstition, did not confuse him. He would transmit each line with expressive force, and his every movement was deliberate. Then came the turning point: the villain's deceit collapsed, and the crowd, no longer ambivalent, rose. A dentist in the front row wept; a psychiatry student took notes; an attendant in the balcony cheered. The accommodation backstage was cramped, the dressing room mountainous with costumes, but the world outside the theatre seemed to quantify his worth anew. He had been entice-resistant once, but now he would not hide his joy. "This was worthwhile," he said, "and uncommon. Generally, I am terribly shy, but tonight I felt the deliverance of art. May the suit of a true performer fit me. This informative night, this proud feat, this abandonment of doubt, has shown my characteristical strength: a constant heart. Even a semiconductor, a sustenance of cold logic, could not measure my gladness. No one is exempt from fear, yet the benevolence of my teachers, who would send me notes of encouragement, carried me. As a boy I had played a cracked violin and studied astronomy by night; this whole inclusive incident, from first cue to final bow, taught me more than any star. I am no privilege-born noble, no proud villain, but a farmhand who dared to dream."
translation: 尽管如此，那个渴望演戏的年轻农场工人从未想过自己会代表学院登台。他骄傲却贫穷，他的成年由代价高昂、不断劳作的岁月塑造而成。一位传统而身材壮硕的工头会怂恿他放弃，但一位和善的陶工和一位四处游走的警察会讨论他的才华并激励他向前。“不要拒绝这个机会，”他们说，“也不要抛弃它；投身这个角色吧。”提示在一个古怪而突兀的夜晚来临。剧中的反派，一位聪明却恶毒的地方法官，病倒了，似乎没有替补可用。受过训练的演员人数很少，替角的名额无从获得，而通常繁荣的学院，面临一场奇特的危机。于是他们派人去找那个农场工人。他只有一份台词的粗略草稿，潦草地写在一张撕破的公式纸上，他必须迅速思量。他怀疑自己会失败，这怀疑很强烈，但一位杰出的老师，机敏而仁慈，诚恳地说：“你服务于这出戏，戏也会服务于你。安定你的神经。”不到一个小时，他就得把手势、嗓音和连绵不断的情感融为一体。他感到一种支配性的恐惧悬在头顶，意识开始动摇。该剧的高潮要求他挥舞一把木剑，绷紧手臂，然后在宫廷的欺诈被揭穿时悲恸地啜泣。他不会为这场冒险忏悔。这出戏极富感染力；观众聪明而老练，纷纷前倾。情节的复杂，充满矛盾、猜疑与迷信，并没有让他困惑。他会以富有表现力的力量传达每一句台词，他的每一个动作都经过深思熟虑。接着转折点到来：反派的欺诈崩塌，人群不再矛盾，纷纷起立。前排一位牙医落泪；一位精神病学专业的学生在记笔记；楼座的一位服务员在欢呼。后台的住处狭窄，化妆间堆满如山的戏服，但剧院外的世界似乎重新衡量了他的价值。他曾经对诱惑无动于衷，如今却不愿掩藏喜悦。“这很值得，”他说，“而且不寻常。通常我极其害羞，但今晚我感到了艺术的解脱。愿一名真正演员的衣装合我身。这个充满启示的夜晚、这桩骄傲的功绩、这种对怀疑的舍弃，展现了我特有的力量：一颗恒定的心。即便是半导体，那冷逻辑的养料，也无法测量我的欢欣。没有人能免于恐惧，但我的老师们的仁慈支撑着我，他们会给我寄来鼓励的便条。少年时我曾拉过一把破旧的小提琴，夜里钻研天文学；从第一个提示到最后的谢幕，这整桩无所不包的事件，教给我的比任何星辰都多。我不是出身特权的贵族，也不是骄傲的反派，而是一个敢于做梦的农场工人。”

text: Lin was a literate, fascinating woman who lived for photography of the night sky. She refused to be passive about the cosmic wonders above her, and her relentless, perpetual curiosity would kindle in anyone who met her. From a lofty hill in Asia she would traverse rough, jagged trails, carrying a heavy pail of gear and an old cane for balance. She was no senseless dreamer; with impartial, effective method she would determine the depth of every shadow and clamp her camera to a horizontal mount. The exotic substance of starlight, she said, was a fantasy made workable by patient craft. Anyone could guess at the stars, but only the devoted could surmount the discrepancy between hope and a real, appreciable image. Her lover, a quiet priest who had left the church to study democracy, would couch his praise in gentle tone. He would readily provide her a guitar tune while she worked slowly, and the harmonious living room felt full of calm. Once a report reached them: a friend had died, and they sent a card of condolence. Grief, she insisted, must not jettison joy. Her coordination of fume-filled darkroom chemicals, an additive here, a gimmick there, was a quiet exploitation of old methods. She would not pose for any poster; she chose to lurk behind the lens. On the wall hung a faded stripe of film, a phoenix rising, an angel of light, a goddess of the void. The mercury in her thermometer barely moved on cold nights, yet she would not give up. Her intercontinental fame grew, though it felt delusive; one conditional grant would pay her wage, and she would pound the desk over red tape. The FBI once asked about an assassination plot near her vault of negatives, but the rumor proved pointless and false. By a score of careful exposures, every component checked, she made an adaptation of an ancient star chart, layday by layday refining each line. A gallon of developer, a sharpener for her pencils, a sheet of coinage-bright foil thereon, and the rusty shutter that would creak: all of it survived. She would shut the iron shutter against the wind, watch a wave of cloud roll past, and trust that, in any circumstance, the creation above held a comparatively simple certainty. The feeble light of the oblique horizon, the debris of a meteor, the gentle oblique slant of dawn over the region, were enough. A couple who lived nearby would visit her, and she would insist that they choose a clear night to watch with him, her lover, while the boys played baseball in the yard below. She liked to transfuse her own wonder into them, telling of every happening in the sky, of rust on the old dome and the way starlight helps the soul survive. She had survived, and so had her wonder.
translation: 林是一位有文化、迷人的女性，她为拍摄夜空而活。她拒绝对头顶的宇宙奇观漠然处之，她那不屈不挠、永不停歇的好奇心会点燃任何遇见她的人。从亚洲一座高耸的山丘上，她会穿越崎岖锯齿状的小径，提着一桶沉重的器材，拄着一根用来保持平衡的旧手杖。她不是一个愚钝的空想者；她以公正而有效的方法测定每一道阴影的深浅，并把相机夹紧在水平支架上。她说，星光这种奇异的物质，是一种被耐心手艺变得可行的幻想。任何人都能对星辰妄加猜测，但只有虔诚的人才能克服希望与真实可见影像之间的差距。她的爱人是一位安静的牧师，他离开了教会去研究民主，他会用温和的语调含蓄地表达赞美。她慢慢工作时，他会欣然为她弹一段吉他曲，那和谐的客厅充满了宁静。一次有消息传来：一位朋友去世了，他们寄出了一张吊唁卡。她坚持认为，悲伤绝不能抛弃欢乐。她对充满烟雾的暗房化学药品的协调——这里加一种添加剂，那里用一个小花招——是对旧方法的一种悄然利用。她不愿为任何海报摆姿势；她选择潜伏在镜头之后。墙上挂着一段褪色的条状胶片、一只腾起的凤凰、一位光之天使、一位虚空的女神。寒夜里她温度计中的水银几乎不动，但她绝不放弃。她那洲际般的名声不断增长，却显得虚妄；一笔有条件的拨款将支付她的工资，而她会为繁文缛节捶桌。联邦调查局曾就她的底片金库附近的一桩暗杀阴谋来询问，但传言被证明毫无意义且不实。通过一二十次精心曝光，每个部件都经过检查，她对一幅古老的星图做了改编，一个停泊日又一个停泊日地打磨每一条线。一加仑显影液、一支削铅笔的卷笔刀、一张其上铺着钱币般光亮的箔纸，以及那会吱嘎作响的生锈快门：这一切都留存了下来。她会关上铁制百叶窗以抵御风，望着一片云浪滚过，并相信无论在任何情形下，头顶的造物都蕴含着相对简单的确定性。倾斜地平线上微弱的光、一颗流星的残骸、黎明在这片地区上方那温柔的斜照，都已足够。住在附近的一对夫妇会来拜访她，她会坚持要他们挑一个晴朗的夜晚和她的爱人一起观星，而下面院子里男孩们正在打棒球。她喜欢把自己的惊奇之情灌输给他们，讲述天空中每一桩事件、旧穹顶上的锈迹，以及星光如何帮助灵魂存活。她活了下来，她的惊奇之心也是。

text: In February, my supervisor handed me an unexpected assignment: to restore a declining manor in rural Europe, a project that would involve months of careful work. The architecture was modern in some wings and aboriginal in others, a complex blend that no alphabetical guidebook could fully paraphrase. A weathered signpost by the sidewalk pointed the way, and a cab would haul visitors and their luggage, each suitcase heavy with hope, up the long drive. The supervisor's reputation was built on aesthetic judgment; he would praise good craft and notify the owners of any flaw, however small. I would cram my notes each night, look at faded photographs, and feel a wave of nostalgia for a bygone age of aristocracy and ceremonial pomp. The damp had caused corrosion in the cable and a slow trickle of rust down the precious stone. By February's end, after a deluge of rain, the cellar nearly became a pond, yet the source of the leak proved easy to find. Atmospheric moisture would pervade every room, and a single papercut on my finger, caustic with dust, reminded me how painful old buildings can be. A rheumatic gardener, reliant on a cane, would yield his secrets slowly: which soil was fertile, where irrigation would nourish the roses, and how a snail or a spider might lurk under a windowsill. He would eat lunch on the lawn and watch a chick wander by, his eloquence quiet but real. The manor's revenue had fallen badly; a subscription scheme and a modest tariff on tours were our attempt to defray costs without lax accounting. One quirk of the estate was a sealed vault rumored to hold a knight's skull and an excerpt of an old treaty once tainted by treason. We could possibly open it tomorrow, but I felt a nerve of dread. Terrestrial maps, an imaginary line of an Antarctic expedition, an endorsement from a famous historian, all came to fruition as the work advanced. The kernel of the plan, its operative idea, was to outweigh decay with care. Demography studies showed the village was cool to tourism, so we would practise restraint. An impromptu party would terrify no one; a chaste, double-arched hall, a precious eclipse of evening light through the glass, the gentle transport of memory anywhere I walked, all proved that a productivity born of patience could revive even the most bygone of homes. I found an old wallet on a windowsill, a precious resource of faded photographs, and I would suffer no haste in restoring them. Every extra hour would become a gift, and the village would become whole again. The work would not be done in a single month, but it would be done.
translation: 二月里，我的主管交给我一项意外的任务：修复欧洲乡间一座日渐衰败的庄园，这个项目将牵涉数月的细致工作。它的建筑在某些侧翼是现代的，在另一些则带有原住民风格，是一种复杂的混合，任何按字母排列的指南都无法完全转述。人行道旁一块饱经风霜的路标指明了方向，一辆出租车会把访客和他们的行李——每只手提箱都装满希望——拉上长长的车道。主管的声誉建立在审美判断之上；他会赞美好的手艺，并把任何瑕疵，无论多小，都通知业主。每晚我会临时苦读我的笔记，看着褪色的照片，对一个贵族与典礼盛况的逝去时代涌起一阵怀旧。潮湿使电缆腐蚀，让锈迹沿着珍贵的石头缓缓流下。到二月末，一场倾盆大雨之后，地窖几乎成了池塘，但渗漏的源头很容易找到。空气中的湿气会弥漫每一个房间，我手指上一道沾着尘土、刺痛的纸割伤，提醒我老建筑会有多么痛苦。一位患风湿、依赖手杖的园丁会缓缓吐露他的秘密：哪块土壤肥沃，哪里的灌溉能滋养玫瑰，以及一只蜗牛或一只蜘蛛会如何潜伏在窗台之下。他会在草坪上吃午饭，看着一只小鸡踱步而过，他的口才安静却真实。庄园的收入下滑得很厉害；一项订阅方案和一笔对参观团征收的适度关税，是我们在不放松账目的前提下分摊成本的尝试。庄园的一个怪处是一间封闭的地下室，传闻藏着一位骑士的头骨和一份曾被叛国玷污的旧条约的摘录。我们或许明天就能打开它，但我感到一阵恐惧的神经悸动。陆地地图、一条南极探险的假想路线、一位著名历史学家的背书，都随着工作的推进开花结果。计划的核心，它起作用的理念，是用悉心照料去抵过腐朽。人口学研究表明这个村庄对旅游业态度冷淡，所以我们会克制行事。一场即兴的聚会不会吓到任何人；一座纯净的双拱大厅、傍晚光线透过玻璃那珍贵的食般遮蔽、记忆在我走到任何地方时的温柔传送，都证明了一种由耐心而生的生产力，能让即便最久远的家园也焕发生机。我在一扇窗台上发现了一只旧钱包，里面是一份珍贵的、装着褪色照片的资源，我修复它们时绝不容许有半点仓促。每一个额外的小时都会成为一份礼物，而这座村庄也将重新变得完整。这工作无法在一个月内完成，但它终将完成。

text: In a busy seaport in China, the morning began with the steady knock of a piston deep inside an old crane. The dock master, a tall man of nearly seventy, would care for every machine as if it were family. He insisted that each part be adequately oiled, for a single sprain in the gears could halt the whole harbor. A caged canary once hung by the office window, a quaint diversion from the dirt and noise, and the workers would chant a rough tune as they worked abreast along the quay. The crude oil tankers, too heavy and often overweight, moved slowly onward toward the pier. To expedite the unloading, the crew would improvise, adding a lateral brace here, a prefabricate panel there, in a fusion of old craft and modern science. The supervisor, no ferocious dictator but a patient master, would never demand outright submission; instead he would alleviate tension with a kind word. He believed the human spirit, not rigidity, was the true catalyst that let the port flourish. When a recession struck, the mood grew wretched. Cargo handling fell by a tenth, then beyond, and a horrible unrest spread among the men. One worker, soaked by a sudden rainy squall that seemed to drench the whole quay, would stammer his fear of layoffs; another, reluctant and full of reluctance, sat in a battered armchair and stared at the garbage piling up. It was an utterly incongruous sight, and the thought of failure weighed on every mind. The accountant spoke of a delcredere clause and an off-grade shipment that had become immaterial, supposedly useless, its value down to a single cent. In the meantime, a satellite dish on the roof would flutter its old cloth cover in the wind, and a telescope on the watchtower swept the gray sea. The port could not retreat. With utmost effort it had to flourish again. The obligatory inspection, a kind of postmortem on the failed quarter, revealed a deformation in one trolley rail and a crude variation in the loading flora of cranes. The kidnaper of profit, the crisis itself, was no divine punishment, only a problem to solve. They would permit a fresh plan, distribute new tasks, and happily push onward. A graduate engineer, his eyelid twitching from long nights, traced the flaw with a vivid, shiny pointer. He favorably approved each piece. He tightened his pants at the waist, gave a quiet hurrah, and as the French would say of any single thing, le moment had come. A new candidate for foreman, fresh from sightseeing leave with his diploma in hand, would graze his knuckles on the steel, punch the air, and add one more original idea. The jellyfish drifting near the hull, the canary's song, the harbor lights: all proved the port could thrive once more.
translation: 在中国一个繁忙的海港，清晨从一台老旧起重机内部活塞稳定的敲击声中开始。码头主管是个将近七十岁的高个子，他照料每一台机器，仿佛它们都是家人。他坚持每个部件都要充分上油，因为齿轮里哪怕一处扭伤，都可能让整个港口停摆。办公室窗边曾挂着一只笼中金丝雀，在尘土与噪声中是一种别致的消遣，工人们并排沿码头干活时会哼一支粗犷的小调。原油油轮太重，常常超载，缓缓向码头驶来。为了加快卸货，船员们会即兴发挥，在这里加一道横向支撑，在那里加一块预制板，把旧手艺与现代科学融为一体。主管不是凶残的独裁者，而是耐心的能手，他从不要求彻底的屈服；相反，他会用一句善言来缓解紧张。他相信，让港口繁荣的真正催化剂是人的精神，而非僵化。当经济衰退来袭，气氛变得凄惨。货物吞吐量先降了十分之一，继而更多，一种可怕的动荡在人群中蔓延。一名工人被突如其来的雨阵淋透，结结巴巴地说着对裁员的恐惧；另一名满心不情愿的人坐在破旧的扶手椅里，盯着越堆越高的垃圾。会计提到一项保付代理条款，以及一批已变得无关紧要、据说毫无用处、价值跌到一分钱的次级货物。与此同时，屋顶上一只卫星天线那旧布罩在风中翻动，望塔上的一架望远镜扫视着灰色的海面。港口不能退缩。它必须竭尽全力重新繁荣。强制性的检查，像是对那个失败季度的一次事后剖析，查出一条手推车轨道的变形，以及起重机群落里一处粗糙的偏差。利润的绑架者，也就是危机本身，并非神明的惩罚，只是一个有待解决的问题。他们会批准一项新计划，分配新任务，然后高高兴兴地继续前进。一位毕业的工程师，因长夜熬战而眼睑直跳，用一支鲜艳发亮的指示棒追查那处缺陷。他逐一作出有利的认可。他在腰间系紧裤子，悄声欢呼了一句，正如法国人谈起任何单独一件事时所说，le 时刻到了。一位新的工头候选人，刚结束观光假期、手里拿着文凭，会在钢铁上擦破指关节，对空挥拳，再添上一个原创想法。漂在船体附近的水母、金丝雀的歌声、港口的灯火：这一切都证明，这座港口能够再度兴旺。

text: On a golden morning in the United States, a creative team set out to build a floating school on an old oil rig, a project that seemed endless at first. The preliminary plan, drawn from a worn textbook, set each parameter with precise care. We would not construe the river as a barrier but as a friendly road; a ferryboat would carry every passenger across the distance, and a graceful colonnade of bamboo would shade the entry. Our savings were modest, so we had to conserve every bit, and a formal promissory note would empower the work. Regularly, a referee-like inspector would visit for a checkup, give his verdict, and instruct us on rectification wherever a flaw might jeopardise safety. We did not presume the task easy. A pessimistic critic, an outcast from the local council, would commonly call the idea worthless and malicious, but we would not let his criticism shatter our resolve. With fidelity to the children, we would strive to win, conscious that a single careless switch could trap a curious child. To justify the cost, we kept a variable budget and a precise discussion log, and we would not yield to vice or wicked shortcuts. A grateful adoptive family, who had once felt lonely and treated like a nomad, donated a brown medal of honor to hang above the door. We would migrate desks by ladder, bridle our impatience, and meditate in quiet leisure when the strain grew chronic. A baby-sitter taught a tutorial on first aid; a professional from the post-office helped us mail forms; and new legislation, after a close election, eased the way. The agricultural village nearby gave us each a pea-green lunch box, and the children, attentively, would echo every lesson. We had to invent a new desk that would fold flat, and we would wrestle with each design until it worked. The questions of the young could elicit fresh ideas from us all. We gave the school a simple title, painted in white. We added zinc panels to the roof to relieve the heat. One could presume that an elephant of doubt would block us, but we pressed on. A small starfish, a habit of meditation, the prevalent hope of the people: all helped us reach the summit. The intensity of our effort, in fair proportion to the dream, would not let us fly from duty. No invasion of doubt, no snore of laziness, could stop us. Marxism, the brave above-mentioned ideals, the further reach of justice, all met in one wish: to win for the children. The wind would echo, the river would hit the hull, and still we built, brave and free.
translation: 在美国一个金色的早晨，一支富有创意的团队着手在一座旧石油钻井平台上建造一所漂浮的学校，这个项目起初似乎没有尽头。初步方案取自一本破旧的教科书，以精确的细心设定了每一个参数。我们不会把河流理解为一道屏障，而是一条友善的道路；一艘渡轮会载着每位乘客跨越那段距离，一排优雅的竹制柱廊会为入口遮荫。我们的积蓄不多，所以必须节省每一点，一张正式的本票将为这项工作提供资金保障。一位裁判般的检查员会定期前来体检，做出他的裁定，并在任何可能危及安全的缺陷处指导我们整改。我们并不臆断这件事容易。一位悲观的批评者，本地议会的一个弃儿，常常把这个想法说成毫无价值且居心不良，但我们不会让他的批评粉碎我们的决心。出于对孩子们的忠诚，我们会奋力争取胜利，意识到一个粗心的开关就可能困住一个好奇的孩子。为了证明开支合理，我们保留了一份可变动的预算和一份精确的讨论记录，我们绝不会屈从于恶习或邪恶的捷径。一户心怀感激的收养家庭，曾经感到孤独、像游牧者一样被对待，捐出一枚棕色的荣誉勋章挂在门上。我们会用梯子搬运课桌，克制我们的急躁，并在劳累变得长期化时于宁静的闲暇中冥想。一位保姆讲授了一堂急救辅导课；邮局的一位专业人员帮我们邮寄表格；在一场势均力敌的选举之后，新的立法也为我们铺平了道路。附近的农业村庄给我们每人一个豆绿色的饭盒，孩子们会专注地回响每一节课。我们得发明一种能折平的新课桌，并会反复琢磨每一个设计，直到它行得通。孩子们的提问能从我们所有人身上引出新鲜的想法。我们给这所学校起了一个简单的名字，用白漆写上。我们在屋顶加上锌板以缓解暑热。人们或许会臆测，一头疑虑的大象会挡住我们，但我们继续前进。一只小海星、一种冥想的习惯、人们普遍的希望：这一切都帮助我们抵达顶峰。我们努力的强度，与梦想相称地成比例，不会让我们逃离职责。没有任何疑虑的入侵，没有任何懒惰的鼾声，能够阻止我们。马克思主义、上述那些勇敢的理想、正义更深远的伸展，全都汇聚于一个愿望：为孩子们赢得胜利。风会回响，河水会拍打船体，而我们依然建造着，勇敢而自由。

theme: The Architect and the Old Theatre
text: Last summer, a humble architect named Vera took on a heroic task: to restore an established old theatre whose ceiling had begun to mould and whose walls showed deep erosion. She was no pompous figure, no vulgar self-promoter; she would profess only a quiet faith, almost a religion, in honest work. Her staff, each a student of design who would dedicate long hours to the work, would peer at every crack and communicate findings without frustration. A heated debate over the most advanced restoration method filled the office, but it never turned bitter. In comparison to a flashy modern enterprise, with its sudden flash of fame, her firm seemed small, yet its competitiveness lay in care, not in pompous talk. A faint gleam of morning light would intersperse the dusty corridor, and a leafy vine outside the window swayed in the spring breeze. The significance of the project was widely felt; the local consul, a leading patron, gave personal advice, and an old mariner who lived nearby offered a sorrowful moan about how the place had decayed. Vera would fasten loose beams, tighten each bolt, and paste fresh plaster where the wall had worn. She would not surrender to the headstrong owner who demanded a careless rush. With deft hands she would condense a complex plan into a clear photo and a composite drawing, attach a postscript of notes, and present it on a single chair at the center of the stage. The advent of new tools made some old methods unusable, but she kept what had merit. A compulsory examination by the city office was prescribed, and the feedback was integral to her next step. She would not let an impost or a sudden devaluation of the building fund baffle her. To internationalize the theatre's appeal, she planned to feature acrobatics, an essayist's lecture, and a convenient open house. The brilliance of the restored hall, an architectural phenomenon, would obviously draw crowds. A goose wandered awkwardly across the lawn; a deer grazed at the leafy edge; and a child ate a raw vegetable from a paper bag. The local fauna seemed at peace, though the whereabouts of the old caretaker remained a mystery. Briefly, the project felt impossible. The importation of rare marble, the convention of formal permits, the bonded warehouse fees, and the lung-tiring dust all weighed on her. Yet she would not utter a tired platitude. Each beam was bonded and transferable to a new use; the embryo of an idea grew. After a quick haircut and a lighter mood, she returned to focus. The match between old grace and new function was nearly perfect, and as the diverge of opinions finally converged, the theatre, like a phoenix, was ready to finish its long sleep and wake to applause.
translation: 去年夏天，一位名叫薇拉的谦逊建筑师接下了一项英勇的任务：修复一座声名确立的老剧院，它的天花板已开始发霉，墙壁也显出严重的侵蚀。她不是个自负的人物，也不是粗俗的自我吹嘘者；她只信奉一种安静的信念，几乎像一种宗教，那就是诚实地工作。她的员工，每一位都是会为这项工作投入长时间的设计学子，会仔细端详每一道裂缝，并毫无沮丧地交流发现。办公室里曾就最先进的修复方法展开过一场激烈的争论，但争论从未变得尖刻。与一家浮华、骤享一时名声的现代企业相比，她的事务所显得很小，但它的竞争力在于用心，而非自负的空谈。清晨微弱的光会洒落在布满灰尘的走廊里，窗外一株多叶的藤蔓在春风中摇曳。这个项目的意义被广泛地感受到；当地的领事是一位带头的赞助人，给出了个人的建议，住在附近的一位老水手则为这地方的破败发出哀伤的叹息。薇拉会固定松动的横梁，拧紧每一颗螺栓，并在墙体磨损处抹上新的灰泥。她不会向那位任性的、要求草率赶工的业主屈服。她以灵巧的双手把一套复杂的方案浓缩成一张清晰的照片和一幅合成图，附上一段附言注释，并在舞台中央的一把椅子上呈现出来。新工具的出现让一些旧方法变得无法使用，但她保留了有价值的部分。市政办公室规定了一次强制性检查，其反馈对她的下一步是不可或缺的。她不会让一项征税或建筑资金的突然贬值难住自己。为了让剧院的吸引力国际化，她计划安排杂技表演、一位散文家的讲座，以及一次方便的开放日。修复后大厅的辉煌，作为一种建筑奇观，显然会吸引人群。一只鹅笨拙地走过草坪；一只鹿在多叶的边缘吃草；一个孩子从纸袋里拿出一根生蔬菜吃。当地的动物群似乎一片安宁，尽管那位老看守人的下落仍是个谜。短暂地说，这个项目曾让人觉得不可能。稀有大理石的进口、正式许可的惯例、保税仓库的费用，以及那令肺部疲惫的灰尘，都压在她身上。然而她不愿说出一句疲惫的陈词滥调。每根横梁都被黏合并可转用于新用途；一个想法的胚胎在生长。剪了个利落的头发、心情轻快之后，她重新投入专注。旧日优雅与新功能之间的匹配近乎完美，随着分歧的意见最终汇合，这座剧院如同一只凤凰，准备结束它漫长的沉睡，醒来迎接掌声。

text: On a hot afternoon, a young astronomer named Mara came as a stranger to the wide prairie to establish a small observatory. These open lands, she said, were a kind of heaven, fairly untouched, where one could idealize the night and study the propulsion of distant stars. She would not strictly regulate her wonder; the craze for discovery, which would embed itself like a seed and had embedded itself in her since she was a novice, drove her on. Her modish gadgets and her old habits would not break apart. An old rancher, appreciative but sceptical, would exclaim in astonishment at her glassware and her queer instruments. "Whether you find anything or not," he would say with a solemn nod, "these stars belong to no one." Mara had won a scholarship associated with a famous palace of science, and she did not intend to misuse it. She knew the risk: a single bout of bad weather could ravage a season of work, and excessive ambition could prove detrimental. Yet she would gather data over numerous consecutive nights, shift her telescope by a centimeter, and watch the dome oscillate gently in the wind. Around her, the prairie would inhabit a thousand small lives. A crooked snake would hop, no, slither, past a bean field; a bird with a sharp beak would poke at porridge crumbs; a propeller plane would drone overhead. She would gulp cold coffee, tuck a hairpin into her hair, and feel an exuberant joy. The terminology of her field, full of prehistoric mythology and the names of a cavalry of constellations, could distress a beginner, but to her it was a tender, indelible language. She would not idealize the past, nor mourn its emptiness. A traitor to old dogma, she trusted what she could measure. There was a stipulation in her grant, a formality of procurement and authorization, that every result thereof must be shared. She did not mind. The state had funded her, and a fit of generosity was no obnoxious thing. Late one night, a strange intimation came: a faint, indelible signal that she could not deflect from her mind. Was it noxious interference, a misuse of the equipment, or something true? She would not idealize it; she would test it. To ignite the data run, she fed an aminoacid-stained sample into the analyzer, sipped a sweet cocktail of relief, and waited. As the prairie began to waken to dawn, primarily she felt gratitude. The growth of knowledge, like a vine, would inherit the future, superior to any warfare of egos. She thought of her son back home, and of how the exportation of ideas, not goods, would build a better world. No Turk of doubt, no satire of cynics, no generality of fear could enforce silence on her. She wiped a rag across the lens, smoothed a crease near her cervix-stiff neck, and, no addict to fame, simply smiled. The drawing of the constellation, sketched in a small section of her notebook, would outlast her. Up on the hill, the propulsion of the heavens carried on, and she, a humble watcher, carried on with it.
translation: 在一个炎热的下午，一位名叫玛拉的年轻天文学家作为陌生人来到辽阔的草原，要建立一座小型天文台。她说，这些开阔的土地是一种天堂，相当未经触碰，人可以在这里把夜晚理想化，研究遥远星辰的推进。她不会严格地约束自己的惊奇；对发现的狂热会像种子一样把自己嵌入，自她还是新手起便已深植于她，驱使着她前行。她那时髦的小器械与她旧有的习惯并不会割裂分开。一位心怀感激却又持怀疑态度的老牧场主，会对她的玻璃器皿和古怪仪器惊叹出声。“无论你是否有所发现，”他会庄严地点头说，“这些星星不属于任何人。”玛拉赢得了一项与一座著名科学殿堂相关联的奖学金，她无意滥用它。她知道风险：一阵坏天气就可能毁掉一季的工作，而过度的野心可能是有害的。然而她会连续无数个夜晚收集数据，把望远镜挪动一厘米，看着圆顶在风中轻轻摆动。在她周围，草原栖息着千百个细小的生命。一条弯曲的蛇会蹦跳——不，是滑行——经过一片豆田；一只长着尖喙的鸟会啄食粥的碎屑；一架螺旋桨飞机会在头顶嗡嗡作响。她会大口喝下冷咖啡，把一枚发夹别进头发，感到一种兴奋的喜悦。她那领域的术语，满是史前神话和一队队星座的名字，会让初学者苦恼，但对她而言那是一种温柔而不可磨灭的语言。她不会美化过去，也不会哀悼它的空虚。作为旧教条的叛徒，她只信任她能测量的东西。她的资助里有一条规定，一种采购与授权的手续，要求由此得出的每一项成果都必须共享。她并不介意。是国家资助了她，一阵慷慨并非什么可憎之事。一个深夜，一个奇怪的暗示传来：一个微弱而不可磨灭的信号，她无法将它从脑海中偏移开。那是有害的干扰、设备的误用，还是某种真实的东西？她不会把它理想化；她要去检验它。为了点燃这次数据运行，她把一份沾有氨基酸的样本送进分析仪，啜饮一杯甜美的、令人宽慰的鸡尾酒，然后等待。当草原苏醒迎接黎明，她首先感到的是感激。知识的增长如同一株藤蔓，将会继承未来，胜过任何自我之间的争斗。她想起家中的儿子，想到思想的输出——而非货物——才会建成一个更美好的世界。没有任何疑虑的土耳其人、没有任何愤世者的讽刺、没有任何恐惧的笼统说法，能够强迫她沉默。她用一块抹布擦过镜头，抚平靠近她那僵硬如颈椎处脖颈的一道褶皱，作为一个并不沉迷名声的人，只是微微一笑。那幅星座的素描，画在她笔记本的一小节里，将比她活得更久。在山丘之上，诸天的推进仍在继续，而她，一个谦卑的观望者，也随之继续前行。

theme: Globalization Seen from a Small Town
text:
At the outset, no one in our outlying town truly grasped what globalization would signify. Decades ago, industrialization arrived like a dragon, and the textile mills that once defined the entirety of our economy began, repeatedly and simultaneously, to push their goods across every border. The data we collected seemed alarming: a huge share, some ninety percent, of local cloth now competed with imports, and the old norm of self-sufficiency had become elusive.

My elder cousin, a competent trainee who later became an underwriter for an insurance alliance, used to say that change was a natural and inherent function of trade. He would economize on words, yet his clarity of thought was a kind of masterpiece. We would queue outside the spacious restaurant on the outskirts, and there, over a plate of cucumber dressed in tangy sauce, he framed his hypothesis: that no town can isolate itself, that to prevent decline a community must widen its access to the world rather than cloak itself in fear.

I was young and adventurous then, and most afternoons I went skiing in the woods beyond the reservoir, or watched a fireman wax the engine of his truck until the glass gleamed. The mill's old paraphernalia, spindles and dyeing vats of every size, still stood in the yard like the composition of a forgotten painting. The atmosphere of that microcosm felt safe, almost a celebration of ordinary life. After a rainbow appeared over the skyscraper they were building downstairs by the river, I would spin a coin and await my cousin's verdict on whether our family business could stabilize.

Sadly, not every story is happy. A sudden rebellion among the workers, who felt their gender and seniority were overlooked, severely threatened the firm. One thoughtless and insolent manager, cowardly and rather sad, had treated them as if they were an enemy rather than partners. The dispute, heavy as a thousand-kilogram weight, dragged the company into a mire from which clean recovery seemed unlikely. To quench the anger, the elders agreed to undertake reform; they would furnish new tools, improve communication, and perform their promises in their entirety.

It is desirable, a realist would say, to correlate ambition with patience. The recruitment of fresh minds, the steady supply of phosphorus-rich fertiliser to the surrounding farms, even the discovery of penicillin in a distant lab, all reminded us that progress, however unusual, rarely comes alone. A bulge of new orders arrived; my cousin tightened her scarf, smiled, and noted that the sheer scale of demand, hosting buyers by the metre of shelf space, had surprised us all. To my surprise, the great host of foreign buyers did not overlook our little frame of a workshop after all.
translation:
起初，我们这个偏远小镇上没有人真正明白全球化意味着什么。几十年前，工业化像一条巨龙般降临，曾经构成我们经济全部的纺织厂开始反复地、同时地把货物推向每一个边境。我们收集到的数据令人警觉：当地布料中有很大一部分，约百分之九十，如今要与进口货竞争，而自给自足这一旧规范已变得难以捉摸。

我的表兄年长些，曾是一名能干的受训者，后来成了一家保险联盟的承保人。他常说变化是贸易自然而固有的功能。他说话节省，但思路的清晰却是一种杰作。我们会在郊区那家宽敞的餐馆外排队，在那里就着一盘浇了酸味酱汁的黄瓜，他提出了自己的假说：没有哪个小镇能孤立自己，要防止衰落，一个社区必须拓宽通向世界的渠道，而不是用恐惧把自己掩盖起来。

那时我年轻又爱冒险，多数下午我都去水库那边的树林里滑雪，或者看一名消防队员给卡车的引擎打蜡，直到玻璃发亮。工厂里旧的随身器具——纺锤和各种尺寸的染缸——仍立在院子里，像一幅被遗忘画作的构图。那个微观世界的气氛让人感到安全，几乎是对平凡生活的一种庆祝。当一道彩虹出现在他们正沿河在楼下建造的摩天大楼上方时，我会抛一枚硬币转动，等待表兄对我们家族生意能否稳定下来的判断。

可惜，并非每个故事都圆满。工人中突然爆发的反抗——他们觉得自己的性别和资历被忽视了——严重威胁着公司。一个欠考虑又傲慢无礼的经理，既怯懦又颇为悲哀，把他们当作敌人而非伙伴。这场争端重如千千克的砝码，把公司拖入泥潭，干净的复苏似乎无望。为了平息怒火，长辈们同意着手改革；他们要置办新工具，改善沟通，并完整地履行承诺。

一个现实主义者会说，把雄心与耐心关联起来是可取的。招募新人、向周边农场稳定供应富含磷的肥料，乃至在遥远实验室里发现青霉素，这一切都提醒我们：进步无论多么不同寻常，都很少独自到来。一批鼓胀的新订单到来了；表兄系紧她的围巾，微笑着说，需求规模之大——按货架的米数来招待买家——彻底让我们所有人都吃了一惊。 让我惊讶的是，那一大群外国买家终究没有忽视我们这间小小框架般的作坊。

theme: An Anecdote from the Antiques Market
text:
Perhaps no place reveals a person's character like a Sunday market, where the surroundings shift from stall to stall and a single anecdote can sum up a whole life. Last sunny day, in bright sunshine, I joined the throng on the left side of the square, content to wander with no fixed agenda. A junior trader, lazy in manner yet ingenious in talk, was trying to advertise an assorted box of magnetic toys and a compact chess set he hoped would render him rich.

Nearby, a patriot in faded jeans had laid out an ornament shaped like a planet, a postcard collection, and the trunk of an old automobile radio. He spoke with such fortitude about the revival of local craft that a small crowd began to conceive real interest. "In actuality," he said, scraping mud from a coin, "these things carry more security than any star on a screen. Heed the past, and it will not annoy you."

The scene felt almost classical. A female violinist played while a duke's portrait, its frame topmost on the wall, looked down with an air both urgent and serene. Someone had begun to embroider a flag; another chewed slowly on a strip of dried meat, perhaps an eggplant relish on the side. The whole panorama was a study in human variety, comprehensive and warm, though now and then an unwelcome jerk of the elbow reminded you to mind your seat at the crowded counter.

Meanwhile, a notary in a grey coat moved through the rows to nullify a forged document and dismiss a dubious claim. He explained, with a perspective sharpened by years of dealing, that to deny fraud is not enough; one must directly oppose it. "It would be thoughtless to incur a debt you cannot repay," he told a nervous young person, "and reckless to ignore the peril of haphazard spending. Such a habit can degrade a family as surely as slavery once crushed the free."

I bought a small print, a finding I had not anticipated, and a scarred wooden box whose lid no longer closed. The seller, prestigious among collectors, was inclined to donate a second piece, an antique mixer for the kitchen, just to oblige me. As the day cooled, the climactic moment came when an old man, his face marked by a scar, began to play a haunting tune. The preferential treatment of beauty over price, the uniformly gentle mood, the amusement of bargaining: all of it made the market a place I would dispatch my friends to without hesitation, certain they too would leave content. Before the excursion ended, a guard checked our identification at the peripheral gate, his manner the hallmark of an invincible discipline. The conversation never grew stagnant. One unpaid trainee, intentional in every gesture, would not tire; he gave clear instruction and crisp dictation to a junior, who copied it in symmetric handwriting. We watched a man chew betel as another swept excrement and stray scraps away to extinguish the smell. A reformed convict explained the microeconomics of his trade: each acquisition, each direct sale, was a gamble against the odds, as unpredictable as a strike in a game of bowling. My daughter-in-law, who studies how leaves release oxygen, read the manual aloud, and no thief would dare rob a place that might, as they say, scrape the very soul.
translation:
也许没有哪个地方像周日集市这样能揭示一个人的性格，那里的环境随着一个个摊位而变化，一则轶事便能概括一生。上一个晴天，在明媚的阳光下，我加入了广场左侧的人群，乐于漫无目的地闲逛，没有固定的议程。一个年轻的小贩，举止懒散却谈吐机灵，正想为一盒各式各样的磁性玩具和一副他希望能让自己发财的紧凑棋具做广告。

附近，一位穿着褪色牛仔裤的爱国者摆出一件形状像行星的装饰物、一套明信片，还有一台旧汽车收音机的外壳。他谈起本地手艺的复兴时充满坚毅，以至于一小群人开始真正产生兴趣。"实际上，"他一边刮去硬币上的泥说，"这些东西比屏幕上任何明星都更有保障。留意过去，它就不会烦扰你。"

这场景几乎是古典的。一位女小提琴手在演奏，墙上最高处挂着一幅公爵的画像，神情既急切又安详。有人开始绣一面旗帜；另一个人慢慢咀嚼一条肉干，旁边或许配着茄子腌菜。整幅全景是一项关于人类多样性的研究，全面而温暖，尽管时不时一个不受欢迎的胳膊肘猛拉会提醒你，在拥挤的柜台前看好自己的座位。

与此同时，一位穿灰外套的公证人在一排排摊位间穿行，宣告一份伪造文件无效，并驳回一项可疑的索赔。他以多年生意磨砺出的眼光解释说，仅仅否认欺诈是不够的，人必须直接反对它。"欠下你还不起的债是欠考虑的，"他对一个紧张的年轻人说，"无视杂乱无章花钱的危险则是鲁莽的。这种习惯能让一个家庭堕落，就像奴隶制曾经碾碎自由人一样确定无疑。"

我买了一幅小版画——一个我未曾预料的发现——还有一个盖子再也合不上的带伤痕的木盒。卖主在收藏家中颇有声望，他乐意再赠送一件，一台厨房用的古董搅拌器，只为帮我个忙。随着白天转凉，高潮时刻到来了：一位脸上有疤痕的老人开始演奏一支萦绕心头的曲子。把美置于价格之上的优待、一贯温和的氛围、讨价还价的乐趣，这一切都让这个集市成为一个我会毫不犹豫地派我的朋友们前往的地方，确信他们离开时同样心满意足。 远足结束前，一名警卫在外围的门口查验我们的身份证件，他的举止是一种不可战胜的纪律的标志。谈话从未变得停滞。一位不拿薪水的受训者，每个动作都饱含用心，毫不疲倦；他向一位新人清晰地讲解、口述，新人则以匀称的字迹抄录。我们看着一个人嚼槟榔，另一个人则把排泄物和零碎残渣扫走以消除气味。一位改过自新的罪犯讲解着他这行的微观经济学：每一次取得、每一次直接的买卖，都是与几率的赌博，像保龄球运动里的全中一样难以预料。我那研究叶片如何释放氧气的儿媳大声念着手册，没有小偷敢去抢劫这样一个据说能刮净灵魂的地方。

theme: The Realm of Poetry and Discovery
text:
Imagine a winter evening when a silent room fills with the loud crackle of a fire, and you decide to meet an old book again. Poetry, more than any other realm of writing, can recover what daily life misses; it lets us extract meaning from the ordinary and anticipate the joy of understanding. A valuable verse is real in a way that gold is not, for its worth does not depend on currency or financial markets.

When I was barely sixteen, in my teens, a strict teacher who would preside over our class with marked dignity asked us to mend our lazy habits and read conscientiously. He spoke toward us as a specialist might address a junior colleague. "Several of you," he said, "treat learning as rubbish to be tossed aside, yet the synthesis of fact and feeling is what gives a mind its height." His words, given freely, achieved more than any threat. Immediately I felt a vigorous hunger to know more.

That hunger took me far. I read of the universe and the galaxy, of radioactivity and the slow propagation of light across space; of how a flask in a lab might hold a combustible gas, of how scientists measure the tonnage of a ship or chart the song of whales. I learned that to amend a theory is no shame, that to abridge a long work can clarify it, and that a dubious claim should never be embraced without proof. A good reader, like a good farmer who tends his wheat and fowl, must not forsake patience.

There is a kind of admiration that borders on reverence for those who, against starvation and penury, still create. A warrior may win glory in battle, a giant of industry may amass wealth, but the poet who writes through bitterness leaves something that will not blur. History gives us the madman and the realist alike; it shows the explosive force of rebellion and the slow extraction of justice from injustice. To preside over one's own thoughts, to pertain to no faction blindly, to perceive clearly: this is the manifestation of a free mind.

Now, recovered from a long illness, I sit by the dike near my home and watch the water disperse the evening light. My cheek feels the cold; an ear catches a distant infection of laughter from children at play. I think of an antique flask of perfume my mother kept, of a sausage supper after a hurried day, of the juicy pear I shared with a friend. Marketing and expenditure will always demand their share of our hours, and a dispute over money can sour any week. But scarcely a day passes that I do not, clockwise around the room, let my eyes fall on the books and feel, despite every hardship, indefinite and quiet gratitude. My perception of these matters did not come in a hurry. As a folk saying goes, you cannot legislate taste, nor disburse wisdom like coins. When a postponement notice from the printer arrived, I felt a jolt run from hip to bowel, for the despatch of my favourite volume had been delayed. To achieve equality of access, the library waived the tuition of its renovation classes, and an obese cat would smack its lips and climb onto the old chair we had begun to upholster anew. I would not miss those evenings: the radioactive glow of the lamp, a pronoun underlined in red, the worn hull of a paperback, a soft nightgown, and the smack of rain on the glass.
translation:
想象一个冬夜，寂静的房间里充满了炉火响亮的噼啪声，而你决定再次与一本旧书相会。诗歌，比任何其他写作领域都更能找回日常生活所遗漏的东西；它让我们从平凡中提取意义，并预期理解的喜悦。一首有价值的诗以黄金所不具备的方式真实存在，因为它的价值不取决于货币或金融市场。

当我刚满十六岁、还在青少年时期时，一位会以鲜明的尊严主持我们班级的严格老师，要求我们改正懒散的习惯、认真地读书。他朝我们说话的样子，就像一位专家对一位较年轻的同事讲话。"你们中有几个人，"他说，"把学习当作可以丢弃的垃圾，然而事实与情感的综合才是赋予头脑高度的东西。"他慷慨给予的话语，比任何威胁都更有效。我立刻感到一种强烈的求知渴望。

那种渴望带我走得很远。我读到宇宙和星系，读到放射性和光在空间中缓慢的传播；读到实验室里的烧瓶可能装着一种易燃气体，读到科学家如何测量一艘船的吨位或绘制鲸的歌声。我懂得了修正一个理论并不可耻，删节一部长篇作品能使它更清晰，而一个可疑的论断绝不应在没有证据的情况下被接受。一个好读者，就像一个照料小麦和家禽的好农夫，不能放弃耐心。

有一种近乎崇敬的钦佩，献给那些在饥饿与贫困中仍然创作的人。战士可以在战场上赢得荣耀，工业巨头可以积累财富，但那个在辛酸中写作的诗人留下的东西不会模糊。历史既给我们疯子也给我们现实主义者；它展示反抗的爆炸性力量，以及从不公中缓慢提取正义的过程。主宰自己的思想，不盲目附属于任何派别，清晰地感知：这就是自由心灵的表现。

如今，从一场久病中痊愈，我坐在家附近的堤旁，看水面把傍晚的光线驱散开。我的脸颊感到寒冷；一只耳朵捕捉到远处孩子们玩耍时传染般的笑声。我想起母亲保存的一瓶古董香水，想起匆忙一天后的香肠晚餐，想起我和朋友分享的多汁的梨。营销和开支永远会索取我们时间的份额，一场关于金钱的争执能让任何一周变得糟糕。但几乎没有哪一天，我不会顺时针环视房间，让目光落在书上，并感到——尽管有种种艰辛——一种不确定而宁静的感激。 我对这些事的领悟并非匆忙得来。正如一句民间谚语所说，你无法用立法规定品味，也无法像分发硬币那样支付智慧。当印刷厂的延期通知送达时，我感到一阵震颤从髋部直贯肠腹，因为我最爱的那卷书的发送被推迟了。为实现取阅的平等，图书馆免除了其翻新课程的学费，一只肥胖的猫咂着嘴，爬上我们重新开始装饰的旧椅子。我不愿错过那些夜晚：灯下放射性般的光晕、用红线划出的一个代词、平装书磨损的外壳、一件柔软的睡衣，以及雨点打在玻璃上的啪嗒声。

theme: The Sculptor in the Remote District
text:
In a remote district on a windswept peninsular, there lived an obscure sculptor whose true identity remained as elusive as an obscure rumour. His apartment, adjacent to a damp cavern near the coast, was a strange institution unto itself. Behind a waterproof curtain he kept the basis of his craft: a spectrum of stones, a pitcher of water, a rack of tools, and a wide pink membrane of wax he would melt and sprinkle over each frame.

He did not belong to any collective, nor did he beg the mayor or the senate for favours. Customary opinion held that he would degrade his gift if he let a financier appraise it, peg a price to it, or strip it of meaning by selling it alongside cheap goods. Yet his sculptures could exemplify an entire sequence of human feeling, from reverence to bitterness, with perceptible clarity. People said a single piece could obliterate doubt and link the viewer to something eternal.

The story goes that the ruling pope once contemplated a vote in the local senate to glorify the man, and a Russian collector offered to pay a surcharge so steep it could remedy the debt of a small town. But the sculptor would not be a pawn. He refused obedience to fashion. 'Will you eject your own conscience for gold?' he asked, and the financier could only gape, his thigh trembling, before he beg pardon and left.

Frequently, as the hours would elapse, the artist sat behind his bench and let his hand sew the final seam of a clay cloak, or sift fine dust through a sieve. A small burrow of mice lived in the wall, and a holocaust of moths once swarmed his lamp. None of this would obscure his focus. For the sake of the work, he would emit no complaint, blot out no error, and overlook no defect.

One customary morning a great airliner roared overhead, its multiplication of engines loud enough to pounce on the silence. The vibration shook a thing from the shelf. The handling of grief, he believed, was the true construction of art. He had no horsepower, no leadership title, no schooling beyond what the woods taught him; even so, an applicant for apprenticeship arrived weekly. Whichever pupil proved competent, he would teach the alphabet of stone. Interference from the outside world, the customary holocaust of noise and rumour, could not strip away the quiet reverence in which he worked, even as the years melted by. He once gave me a pill for my cough and spoke in a single grave utterance about the macroeconomics of art, how a liberal patron might export beauty abroad. I postulate, he said, that fame is insubstantial; therewith he would countermand any order for the delivery of his work to a gallery of mere conquest. The mucous damp of the cave clung to my tibia as I left, while the ejection of hot wax along a circuit of moulds was his only farewell.
translation:
在一个饱受风吹的半岛上的偏远地区，住着一位默默无闻的雕塑家，他真实的身份像一则晦涩的传闻一样难以捉摸。他的公寓紧邻海边一处潮湿的洞穴，本身就是一所奇异的机构。在一道防水的帘子后面，他保存着手艺的根基：各色石料、一只水罐、一架工具，以及一大片宽阔的粉色蜡膜——他会把蜡熔化，喷洒在每一具骨架上。

他不属于任何集体，也不向市长或参议院乞求恩惠。按照惯常的看法，倘若他让金融家来评估作品、给它标个价，或把它与廉价货摆在一起出售而剥夺其意义，他便糟蹋了自己的天赋。然而他的雕塑能以可感的清晰，举例呈现人类情感的一整个系列，从崇敬到辛酸。人们说，单单一件作品就能抹去疑虑，把观者与某种永恒之物连接起来。

据说当政的教皇曾考虑在地方参议院投票来颂扬此人，一位俄罗斯收藏家也愿意支付一笔高昂的额外费用，足以弥补一座小镇的债务。但这位雕塑家不愿做棋子。他拒绝向时尚屈服。「你愿为黄金喷出自己的良心吗？」他问道，金融家只能张口结舌，大腿发颤，随后求得原谅便离开了。

常常，随着时间流逝，这位艺术家坐在工作台后，让手缝合一件黏土斗篷的最后一道接缝，或用筛子筛过细尘。墙里住着一窝老鼠的地洞，曾有大群飞蛾扑向他的灯。这些都无法使他分心。为了作品，他不发一句怨言，不抹去一处败笔，也不忽略任何瑕疵。

一个寻常的清晨，一架巨大的客机从头顶轰鸣而过，多重引擎的声音大得足以扑灭寂静。震动把一样东西从架上摇落。他相信，对悲伤的处理才是艺术真正的构造。他没有马力，没有领导头衔，除了树林教给他的之外没有受过学校教育；即便如此，每周都有人前来申请当学徒。无论哪个学生证明自己能胜任，他都会教他石头的字母表。来自外界的干扰，那惯常的噪音与传闻的大肆侵扰，都无法剥夺他工作时那份静默的崇敬，哪怕岁月正在融化般流逝。 他曾给我一粒药丸治咳嗽，并以一句庄重的话谈起艺术的宏观经济学，谈起一位慷慨的赞助人如何可能把美输出到国外。他说，我假定名声是虚无缥缈的；据此他会撤回任何把他作品送往一座只为征服而设的画廊的订单。当我离开时，洞穴里黏液般的潮气贴着我的胫骨，而热蜡沿着一圈模具的喷射，便是他唯一的告别。

theme: A Speedy Recovery on the Tropic River
text:
The illness struck without warning. What began as a vague ache in the belly and a dull pain in my neck soon revealed itself, after a fierce week, to be dysentery. The principal doctor at the clinic, a handsome but laborious man with a haggard face, prescribed a course of pills and ordered a brief quarantine to confine the infection. He warned that the bearing of my mood would matter as much as any granule of medicine.

The clinic stood near a wide reservoir in the tropic lowlands, everywhere fringed by tall fir and fragrant pear trees. Immigration officials had stamped my license at the border, and the long check-out queue at the dock had left me nervous. Now, naked of all my plans, I lay still. A touchy old beggar in the next bed, seldom polite, would dispute every remark, while a kindly Dutch shopkeeper across the room told stories with wisdom and gentle humour, never lukewarm in his welcome.

To recapture my strength I had to abandon my old habit of haste. The doctor explained, in elaborate detail, that recovery was not a hurdle to leap but a curve to follow. He did not glorify quick fixes; he warned that an intractable case could undermine the body for a generation if a patient sought only a speedy result. A turbulent fever, he said, must not be allowed to rouse panic. Commitment to rest, he insisted, was no unnecessary luxury but the correct path.

As the days passed I began to gain ground. A postgraduate nurse, cheeky and warm, would bring food on a marble tray: a slice of pear, a little saline broth, sometimes a pimple of honey on bread fresh from the oven. She would scare off my gloom, deny my woe a foothold, and ascribe my progress to plain participation in the daily routine. I no longer differ with her on that. Her commonplace kindness, exclusively given without thought of reward, became the holy centre of those long hours.

On the day of my release I walked, weak but upright, down to the water where a canoe waited. A competitor in some local race paddled past, fist raised in triumph. The collision of joy and relief nearly made me weep. I thought of all I had nearly lost: the festivity of ordinary life, the dignity of standing on my own. A mystic might call it grace; a lawyer might call it luck. The president of the clinic, presiding at the gate, shook my hand. The ax of fear had been laid down; the denial was over. With a synonym for gratitude on my lips, I stepped into the midst of the world again, my hardware of habits rebuilt, ready to slay no enemy but my own despair. At the start of the tour, the doctor would prescribe a triple dose and a careful depiction of every symptom, dealing gently so as not to cosset the patient nor hinder recovery, and he always left the door ajar. In a developing nation moving toward socialism, the marketplace must optimize its scale; the erasure of old debts was indicative of reform, though stacks of lumber and red tape still slowed the work.
translation:
病来得毫无预兆。起初只是腹部一阵模糊的隐痛和脖子上一阵钝痛，经过凶猛的一周后，很快显现为痢疾。诊所的主任医生，一个英俊却辛劳、面容憔悴的人，开了一个疗程的药丸，并下令短期隔离以限制感染。他警告说，我情绪的举止与任何一粒药一样重要。

诊所坐落在回归线附近的低地，挨着一座宽阔的水库，四处都被高大的冷杉和芬芳的梨树环绕。移民官在边境为我的执照盖了章，码头上漫长的离站排队让我紧张不安。如今，剥去了一切计划，我静静躺着。隔壁床一个暴躁的老乞丐，很少有礼貌，对每句话都要争辩；而房间对面一位和善的荷兰店主则带着智慧和温和的幽默讲故事，他的欢迎从不冷淡。

要夺回我的体力，我必须放弃急躁的旧习惯。医生详尽地解释说，康复不是一道要跃过的栏，而是一条要顺着走的曲线。他不美化速效良方；他警告说，一个难处理的病例若患者只求快速结果，可能会削弱身体长达一代人之久。他说，汹涌的高烧绝不能被允许去唤起恐慌。他坚持认为，对休息的承诺并非多余的奢侈，而是正确的道路。

随着日子过去，我开始有了起色。一位研究生护士，俏皮又温暖，会用大理石托盘端来食物：一片梨、一点盐水汤，有时是刚出炉的面包上一小疙瘩蜂蜜。她会吓跑我的阴郁，不让我的悲苦立足，并把我的好转归因于对日常作息单纯的参与。在这一点上我不再与她有异议。她那平凡的善意，专一地给予而不求回报，成了那些漫长时光神圣的中心。

出院那天，我虚弱却挺直地走到水边，一只独木舟在那里等着。一名本地比赛的竞争者划桨经过，握拳作出胜利的姿势。喜悦与宽慰的碰撞几乎让我落泪。我想到自己险些失去的一切：平凡生活的欢庆，独自站立的尊严。神秘主义者会称之为恩典；律师会称之为运气。诊所的院长在门口主持，与我握手。恐惧之斧已经放下；否认已经结束。带着一个表示感激的同义词在唇边，我重新踏入世界的中心，习惯的硬件已重建，准备去屠戮的不是别的敌人，而是我自己的绝望。 在巡诊开始时，医生会开出三倍的剂量，并仔细描述每一个症状，处理时十分温和，既不溺爱病人，也不妨碍其康复，而且他总是让门半开着。在一个走向社会主义的发展中国家，市场必须优化其规模；旧债的勾销是改革的标志，尽管成堆的木料和繁文缛节仍拖慢着工作。

theme: The Brain, Resilience, and a British Farm
text:
On a grand British farm among the rolling meadow country, my father kept a stout shepherd's cottage that he loved to renovate. It was here, surely, that I first learned about resilience. The continual rhythm of the seasons taught a patience no television or best-seller could enhance, and the loneliness of winter could be problematic for a darling child who craved company.

My recollection of those years is vivid. Each morning my father, a worker of real expertise, would specify the chores: replenish the trough, certify the count of the cattle, hoist the hay onto the rod-and-rack frame, and protect the organic crops from gale and frost. The rotation of tasks was a kind of computation, roughly fixed yet flexible, and an observer might call it monotonous. To me it was rewarding. Even the laundry on the line, swirling in the wind, seemed to wiggle with a life of its own.

The farm was no commonplace operation. We grew turnip and flower beds alike, and a small brewery at the rear produced a stout so dark it looked like ash. My father would browse a worn handbook for advice, muscular arms folded, and say that good husbandry was advisable, that incorrect methods would only breed discomfort. He distrusted any hasty or outlandish scheme; a sheepish neighbour once tried a compatible new fertiliser without verification and watched his quantity of barley collapse into paralysis.

There was, of course, hardship. My brother caught a fever that left him incapable of work, his tooth aching, his chin pale; the brain, the doctor said, needed rest more than any pill. The uncertainty was a heavy suspension over the house. Yet my father would summon a hilarious story, accidentally spill the necklace of beads my mother kept by the sink, and turn provocation into laughter. He refused to let depreciate the heritage of good cheer that was our true wealth.

That heritage, multiple and rich, still shapes me. I think of the meadow at dawn, the cat asleep on the price-list pinned to the door, the arrow of geese overhead. I think of the crevice in the old wall where I would bury small treasures, of the bath by the fire, of the adhesive sense of belonging that no sanction or distance could pierce. Among all the things I own, that excellent and continual belief in patience, in protection, in the slow solidify of love, is the one I would never let a competitor purchase. It made me, and represents in its quiet representation everything I hope to pass on. Our friendship, visible to all, did not obligate either of us to anything. At the station we would practice the pronunciation of foreign words, fill a paper with notes, and watch the furniture of the old hall swirl with dust. As a season ski-pass holder, he would transship his gear north each winter, press the accelerator of his battered car, pour a glass of scotch, and speak warmly of the charities that fight aids.
translation:
在一座宏大的英国农场上，置身于连绵起伏的草地之间，我父亲守着一间结实的牧羊人小屋，他喜欢翻修它。无疑，正是在这里，我第一次懂得了什么是顺应力。四季不断的节律教会了一种耐心，那是任何电视或畅销书都无法增进的；而冬天的孤独，对一个渴望陪伴、惹人疼爱的孩子来说，可能是个难题。

我对那些年的回忆很鲜明。每天清晨，我父亲——一个真正有专业技能的劳动者——会详细指定家务活：把饮水槽补满，核实牛的数目，把干草吊到杆架结构的支架上，并保护有机作物不受大风和霜冻侵害。任务的轮换是一种计算，大致固定却又灵活，旁观者也许会觉得单调。对我而言它却很有收获。就连晾衣绳上的衣物，在风中打着旋，似乎也扭动着自己的生命。

这农场可不是寻常的经营。我们既种萝卜也种花圃，后院一家小酿酒厂酿出的烈性黑啤暗得像灰烬。父亲会翻阅一本破旧的手册寻求建议，肌肉发达的胳膊抱在胸前，说良好的农事管理是明智可取的，不正确的方法只会滋生不适。他不信任任何草率或古怪的方案；一个胆怯的邻居曾未经检验就试用一种据说兼容的新化肥，眼看着自己大麦的产量瘫痪般地崩溃。

当然也有艰难。我弟弟得了一场热病，使他无力干活，牙齿作痛，下巴苍白；医生说，大脑比任何药丸都更需要休息。那种不确定像沉重的悬置笼罩着整个家。然而父亲会讲出一个滑稽的故事，不经意打翻母亲放在水槽边的珠串项链，把刺激变成笑声。他拒绝让我们真正的财富——那份乐观的传统——贬值。

那份传统，多重而丰厚，至今仍塑造着我。我想起黎明的草地，想起睡在钉于门上的价格表上的猫，想起头顶上排成箭形的雁群。我想起老墙上那道我用来埋藏小宝贝的裂缝，想起炉火旁的洗澡，想起那种黏着般的归属感——没有任何制裁或距离能将它刺穿。在我拥有的一切之中，那份卓越而不断的信念——对耐心、对保护、对爱缓慢凝固的信念——是我绝不会让任何竞争者买走的。它造就了我，并在它静默的呈现中，代表着我希望传承下去的一切。 我们的友谊众人皆见，却不强求彼此承担什么义务。在车站，我们会练习外语单词的发音，在纸上写满笔记，看着旧厅里的家具与尘埃一同打旋。作为一名季票滑雪卡的持有者，他每年冬天都把装备转运北上，踩下那辆破车的油门，倒上一杯苏格兰威士忌，并热切地谈起那些与艾滋病作斗争的慈善事业。

theme: A Coastal Survey of Species and Terrain
text:
When I was first hired to survey the eastern delta, I did not find the work expensive or boring; initially it was the most rewarding job I could imagine. My task was to map the boundary of a fragile ecosystem along a coral reef, to contemplate its myriad species, and to finalize a report on how congestion from new construction near the motorway might harm the territory.

The handbook the institute gave me was handy and economical with words. It urged me to visualize each habitat as a single subject, to deduce patterns from genetic data, and to broaden my view from one lane of evidence to the whole interior of the problem. I would unlock my field card at dawn, drag my rack of gear past the lighthouse, and wait for the boiling sun to clear the morning hum of insects. A turtle might cross the wet sand; a shark's fin might cut the surface beyond the reef; once a curious bee followed me the whole starting mile.

The terrain was a versatile teacher. Each cycle of the tide exposed a new seam in the rock, an incipient crack that would, given irregularity of weather, widen into a cave. I learned to read the omen of a snowstorn far inland and to intensify my pace against any deadline I might exceed. A gracious old landlady near the customs house rented me a bedroom with cotton sheets, served strong tea and soup, and told me anecdotes about smugglers who once demanded a ransom from passing ships, or a butcher who would extort and entangle honest traders in petty schemes.

Not everything was fair. A by-product of tourism was a flood of cheap commodity stalls; an alloy of greed and haste, you might say. Some vendors, lavish in promises, were less than legitimate, and their etiquette toward the species we studied was appalling. They would pluck cosmetic shells, drag nets across the reef, and leave the outland fringes of the marsh in ruins. Considerably more troubling, a garrison of developers contemplated a synthetic harbour that would obscure the wild shore forever.

My certificate of completion came with a designation I was proud of, and freely I admit the triumph felt sweet. The survey would, I hoped, broaden public belief in conservation. As I packed my jacket, rolled down my sleeve, knelt on one knee to seal the last crate, and signed against the flag of the field office, I felt the independence of useful labour. The reactor of my own ambition had cooled into something serviceable: a calm wish to absorb what the world teaches and, in turn, to defend it. In his autobiography he made a brief appearance as a vegetarian cook who could envision an entire menu from the marrow of a single idea. During the snowstorm we stayed indoor, sipped beer and chewed candy, and watched the slow upcreep of prices on the board. The greenhouse owner offered me a fair remuneration to hire a guide, and with a polite bow I accepted.
translation:
当我第一次被雇来勘测东部三角洲时，我并不觉得这份工作昂贵或无聊；起初它是我所能想象到的最有收获的工作。我的任务是沿着一片脆弱的珊瑚礁绘制这一脆弱生态系统的边界，仔细考察其无数物种，并就高速公路附近新建筑带来的拥堵可能如何危害这片区域，定下一份报告。

研究所给我的手册很方便，文字也很省。它敦促我把每一处栖息地都想象成一个独立的主题，从遗传数据中推断规律，并把视野从单一的一条证据拓宽到整个问题的内部。每天黎明我会解锁我的野外记录卡，把一架装备拖过灯塔，等待沸腾的太阳驱散清晨虫鸣的嗡嗡声。一只海龟也许会穿过湿润的沙滩；一条鲨鱼的鳍也许会划破礁外的水面；有一次，一只好奇的蜜蜂跟着我走完了开头整整一英里。

这片地形是个多才多艺的老师。每一次潮汐的循环都会暴露出岩石里的一条新缝，一道初生的裂口——倘若天气反复无常，它会扩大成一个洞穴。我学会了解读远在内陆的暴风雪之兆，并在我可能超出的任何期限前加快步伐。海关旁一位和善的老房东把一间铺着棉布床单的卧室租给我，端来浓茶和热汤，给我讲述走私者的趣闻——他们曾向过往船只勒索赎金；还讲到一个屠夫，会敲诈并把诚实的商人卷入鸡毛蒜皮的勾当。

并非一切都公平。旅游业的一个副产品是廉价商品摊位的泛滥；你可以说那是贪婪与匆忙的合金。一些摊贩在承诺上慷慨大方，却谈不上正当合法，他们对我们所研究物种的礼数令人发指。他们会采摘用作化妆装饰的贝壳，把渔网拖过珊瑚礁，把沼泽偏僻的边缘弄得一片狼藉。更令人不安得多的是，一队开发商正打算建一座人工合成的港口，那将永远遮蔽这片野性的海岸。

我的结业证书附带一个我引以为豪的头衔，我坦率承认那份成功的滋味很甜。我希望这次勘测能拓宽公众对保护的信念。当我收拾夹克、卷下袖子、单膝跪地封好最后一只板条箱、对着野外办公室的旗帜签字时，我感到了有用劳动带来的独立。我自身野心的反应堆已冷却成某种耐用的东西：一个平静的愿望——去吸收世界所教导的一切，再反过来去守护它。 在他的自传里，他作为一名素食厨师短暂登场，能够从一个念头的精髓中构想出整份菜单。暴风雪期间我们待在室内，啜着啤酒、嚼着糖果，看着告示牌上价格缓缓上涨。温室的主人开出一笔公道的酬劳雇我做向导，我礼貌地鞠了一躬便答应了。

theme: A Centenary at the Harbour Museum
text:
The notice on the bulletin board was patently momentous: our harbour town would mark its centenary with a grand festival, and the new museum by the quay would open its doors. So many people had worked to bring this day about that the mere idea of failure now seemed outdated. A provisional committee, active since spring, had drawn up an agenda, and a cheerful counsellor with a healthy complexion was certain everything would be o.k.

I walked along the gulf at departure of light, past a tombstone-grey wall and a row of croissant shops, to analyze the panorama for the souvenir postcard I planned to sell. From the ballroom window one could perceive the whole sector of coast: the fishery boats laden with the night's catch, the old tunnel cut through the headland, a brook that fed a pond fringed with vegetation. A pedestrian in a wet coat paused to applaud a street musician whose musical talent was impressive, while children chased a butterfly past a hanger of drying nets.

Inside, the curator showed admission to a sufficient crowd. He had a hint of the showman about him. In A.D. ninety-two, he began, a baron had built the first warehouse here. He spoke of the harsh era of imperialism, of a riot and the democrat who quelled it, of a wreck whose ash and broken mold still lay in the deep. A glass case held a coin, a clasp, a tube of antique toothpaste, and an eyeball-shaped lamp once used to certify cargo. The continuity of these objects, he said, was an acknowledgment of every worker who had shaped the place.

Not all the exhibits were peaceful. One alcove was particularly grim: it depicted a raid in which an opponent fleet had stormed the bay. A faded omen, painted on a shilling-sized medal, hinted at the diarrhea and dysentery that swept the garrison afterward. The herbivorous deer carved into a groove of stone seemed to swing between sorrow and hope, a tinge of grief in their eyes.

By dusk the vogue of the day had not faded. A centrifugal energy pulled the crowd toward the square, where a coach unloaded baggage for visitors travelling westward. Someone shouted a cheerful exhortation; another would exhort the band to play on. I normalize my breathing, pluck up courage, and stepped onto the platform. To my surprise the speech went well, identical in spirit to the one I had rehearsed, buoyant and gratifying. An applicable lesson, I think, for anyone who fears a crowd: the relief of finishing is its own reward, and the avenge of nerves is simply to begin. Far from boring, the institute planned to upgrade its stock of exhibits before the crowds depart. A positive sum of donations, free of any opaque capitalization scheme, would shelter the synthetic models from the remoteness of decay. In neat notation a comrade catalogued each cricket-shaped brooch in careful alignment, and the notional value, an exporter once joked, lay beyond the cave of any vault.
translation:
公告栏上的通知显然意义重大：我们这座海港小镇将以百年庆典纪念它的一百周年，码头旁的新博物馆也将开门迎客。为促成这一天，那么多人付出了努力，以至于失败的念头如今都显得过时。一个自春天起就活跃的临时委员会拟好了议程，一位面色红润、性情开朗的顾问确信一切都会没问题。

天光初褪时，我沿着海湾散步，经过一道墓碑般灰色的墙和一排新月形面包店，去分析全景，好为我打算出售的纪念明信片取材。从舞厅的窗口可以看到整段海岸：满载着夜捕的渔船、穿过海角凿出的旧隧道、一条注入被植被环绕的池塘的小溪。一位穿湿外套的行人停下来为街头乐手鼓掌，那人的音乐才华令人印象深刻；孩子们追着一只蝴蝶跑过一架晾网的衣架。

馆内，馆长向足够多的人群展示了入场。他身上有几分演艺人的味道。「公元九十二年，」他开口道，「一位男爵在这里建起了第一座仓库。」他讲到帝国主义的严酷年代，讲到一场暴乱以及平息它的那位民主党人，讲到一艘沉船——它的灰烬和破碎的铸模至今仍躺在深处。一只玻璃柜里放着一枚硬币、一个扣子、一管古董牙膏，还有一盏眼球形状、曾用来检验货物的灯。他说，这些物件的连续性，是对每一位塑造了这地方的工人的鸣谢。

并非所有展品都安宁。有一处壁龛格外阴森：它描绘了一场突袭，一支敌方舰队曾猛攻这座海湾。一枚先令大小的徽章上画着褪色的预兆，暗示了此后席卷驻军的腹泻与痢疾。刻在石槽里的食草鹿，似乎在悲伤与希望之间摇摆，眼中带着一丝哀愁。

到黄昏时，这一天的热潮仍未消退。一股离心般的能量把人群拉向广场，那里一辆长途客车正为西行的旅客卸下行李。有人喊出一句愉快的劝勉；另一人则要鼓动乐队继续演奏。我让呼吸恢复正常，鼓起勇气，踏上讲台。出乎我意料，演讲很顺利，在精神上与我排练过的那篇一模一样，轻快而令人欣慰。我想，这对任何害怕人群的人都是一条适用的经验：完成之后的宽慰本身就是回报，而战胜紧张的报复方式，不过就是开始。 这家机构毫不乏味，计划在人群离去之前升级其藏品库存。一笔可观的捐款总额，不涉及任何不透明的资本化方案，将使这些合成模型免于在偏远中朽坏。一位同志以工整的符号为每一枚蟋蟀形胸针编目，整齐排列，而一位出口商曾打趣说，那名义上的价值，超出了任何金库的洞穴。

theme: The Diligent Lexicographer in the Alpine Town
text:
It was a frightful winter when I first arrived in the alpine town, and indeed the cold seemed to gratify nothing but my growing loneliness. I had come, increasingly certain of my plan, to compile a best-selling dictionary, for lexicography had been my passion since my school class days. The feasibility of the project once seemed infinite; now, motionless at my desk, I would stare at a blank page and feel like a victim of my own ambition.

My landlord, a cordial old resident with a witty tongue, did much to refresh my spirits. He would devise small comforts: a banquet of mince pies, a drum of refreshment by the fire, honey for my tea. "A pessimist sees only shadow," he said, "but a diligent man sees variety." He had previously worked at the shipyard, and the consistency of his good cheer, in defiance of his dental aches and frequent headache, was a marvel to me. He kept a parakeet in the attic and a salmon smoking in the shed.

Each morning I would hike past the gymnasium and the memorial in the precinct square, then walk along a path of flagstone toward the lake, where the water was navigable only in summer. I learned to bake bread, to mince herbs, to coordinate my hours so that no fortuity could derail the work. A previously published bulletin had testified that the region's economics depended on its ecosystem, and I would marvel at how a whole business could grow from such a fragile relation between people and land.

The town itself was a kind of memorial to endurance. An old uprising was commemorated by a plaster relief on the town hall; a coin minted for that occasion still passed from hand to hand. The distributor of the local paper, a manly figure with the decency of a saint, told me that the prevailing mood after the revolt had been one of solidarity. A chairwoman of the council, qualified and gracious, had backdated certain pardons so that no rebel's remains went unhonoured.

My own learning deepened. I came to differentiate the permissible from the merely fashionable, and to confront each doubtful entry with patience and accuracy. When at last the manuscript arrived at completion, I felt its meteoric sweetness, a pearl of effort polished over many months. To celebrate, I would baby-sit my neighbour's child, pitch a tent by the brook, and invert my old habits of worry. The documentation alone filled a tent's worth of boxes; an enclosure of notes, an alarm clock that I no longer needed, and a sieve of discarded ideas. Strange as it sounds, the work that had once seemed a sclerosis of the mind became, in the end, the purest enjoyment I had ever known. To good effect I would lounge amidst my luggage in the flat above the lounge and spell out new entries. I reckon a prerequisite of the craft is patience, for the damp would necessitate scraping mildew from the walls. I can testify that even a single kilowatt of heat helped. A painting of an ape hung crooked; whenever I would arrive each morning I made the same substitution of words and would backdate my notes, and the school's move to coeducation, I reckoned, had a fine effect on the town.
translation:
我初到那座高山小镇时，正值一个可怕的冬天，确实，那寒冷除了助长我日益加深的孤独，似乎什么也满足不了。我来此，是越来越确信自己的计划——编一部畅销的词典，因为自学生时代以来，词典编纂一直是我的热情所在。这个项目的可行性一度看似无穷无尽；如今，我一动不动地坐在书桌前，盯着空白的纸页，觉得自己成了自己雄心的牺牲品。

我的房东是位热诚的老居民，言谈风趣，为我重振精神出了不少力。他会想出种种小小的慰藉：一桌肉馅饼，炉边一桶饮料，给我的茶里加蜂蜜。“悲观者只看到阴影，”他说，“勤奋的人却看到多样。”他先前在船坞工作，尽管牙痛、常常头痛，他始终如一的好心情对我而言是个奇迹。他在阁楼里养着一只长尾鹦鹉，棚里熏着一条大马哈鱼。

每天早晨，我会徒步经过体育馆和广场上选区里的纪念碑，然后沿着一条石板路朝湖边走去——那湖水只有夏天才可通航。我学会了烤面包、切碎香草、合理安排时间，好让任何偶然事件都无法打乱工作。先前出版的一份公报曾证明，这个地区的经济情况依赖于它的生态系统，我会惊叹于：一整桩生意竟能从人与土地之间如此脆弱的关系中成长起来。

小镇本身就是一座耐力的纪念碑。市政厅上一幅灰泥浮雕纪念着一场古老的起义；为那次事件铸造的一枚硬币至今仍在人们手中传递。当地报纸的分销商是个有男子气概的人物，具有圣徒般的体面，他告诉我，那场暴动之后占上风的情绪是一种团结一致。议会的一位女主席既有资格又和蔼，曾把某些赦免回溯生效，使没有一个反叛者的遗骸得不到尊崇。

我自己的学问也加深了。我学会区分什么是可允许的、什么只是时髦的，并以耐心和准确去面对每一个存疑的词条。当手稿终于完成时，我感到了它流星般的甜美，那是历经数月打磨的一颗努力的珍珠。为庆祝，我会替邻居看孩子，在小溪边支起帐篷，颠倒我从前忧虑的种种习惯。光是那些文件就装满了够一顶帐篷大小的箱子；一沓附件笔记、一只我再也不需要的闹钟，还有一筛被丢弃的念头。说来奇怪，那项曾让我头脑仿佛硬化的工作，最终却成了我所体验过的最纯粹的乐趣。 颇见成效地，我会在休息室楼上那套公寓里、在行李堆中懒洋洋地坐着，逐字拼出新词条。我认为这门手艺的一个先决条件就是耐心，因为潮湿迫使我刮去墙上的霉斑。我可以作证，哪怕一千瓦的热量也有帮助。一幅画着猿猴的画歪挂着；每天早晨我一到，便做同样的词语替换，并把笔记回溯标注日期，而我寻思，学校改为男女同校，对这座小镇产生了不错的影响。

theme: The Punctual Receptionist and the International Match
text:
Today, everyone at the international sports club knew that the chess final was imminent. I am the receptionist there, a punctual and qualified worker, and basically my job is to count guests, sign forms, and see that no one feels like an illiterate outsider. There is always a chance that some workaholic designer or a conservative baron will arrive late, but I dare not let the schedule deteriorate.

The champion's opponent this year was a first-rate proletarian player from a distant commonwealth, a man of dogged, magnanimous spirit. He had survived bronchitis as a child, broken his femur in a fall, and once seemed a victim of every misfortune. People used to suspect his nerve would collapse, that his form was rotten and his confidence insoluble. Yet he refused to dodge the contention; with quiet solidarity among his fans, he turned each match into a marvel.

Before the storm of the final, the hall was tense. I deduct nothing from the drama when I say the air felt drowsy yet electric, as if some omen hung above the platform. A series of officials, diplomatic and needful of order, tried to clarify the rules and modulate the rising uproar. One absent-minded count kept toying with a walnut, while an aunt of the champion peeled a butterfly-shaped pastry and offered it around. A premium had been paid for the freight that brought the engraved trophy, and rejection of any late entry was final; the committee would shelve no complaint.

The game itself was an infinite pleasure to watch. Each player would stretch his thinking, tense as a spring, and the stimulus of the clock made every move ragged with risk. Protein bars and luxury chocolates lay untouched. When the challenger forced a merge of his rooks and a sudden sign of weakness appeared in the champion's defence, a ridiculous, joyful uproar broke out. It was pure fun, intelligible even to a kid who had wandered in from the zoo trip next door.

The pertinence of the result was clear to all. The flight home would carry a new hero. As the players signed the scoresheet, I felt the enjoyment of an ordinary day made extraordinary. A dependant of the old champion, drowsy in his seat, woke to applaud. Some said the dynasty had ended; others insisted it had merely undergone a consolidation. There was no conspiracy, no profane trick, no parody of sport, only a clean victory. The motorway outside filled with cars, and as the pulp of the day's newspapers spun off the presses with my photograph in the corner, I let myself, for once, simply marvel and seclude my doubts. Because the existing engine could hardly start, we left it under a blanket in the barn. I would peel an orange and write a letter in a calm voice, careful not to harass anyone. A scientific spirit of stewardship should permeate every page rather than leave it blank, yet a child's toy lay forgotten where the cold would permeate the boards.
translation:
今天，国际体育俱乐部里人人都知道，国际象棋决赛即将到来。我是那里的接待员，一个守时又称职的工作人员，基本上我的工作就是清点客人、签署表格，并确保没有人觉得自己像个被排斥的文盲。总有可能某个工作狂设计师或某位保守的男爵迟到，但我不敢让日程恶化。

今年冠军的对手是一位来自遥远联邦的一流无产阶级棋手，一个顽强而宽宏大量的人。他小时候得过支气管炎，摔断过大腿骨，一度看似遭受种种不幸的牺牲品。人们曾怀疑他会神经崩溃，状态糟糕，信心无法挽回。然而他拒绝逃避这场较量；在球迷们默默的团结一致之下，他把每一场比赛都变成了奇迹。

决赛的风暴来临之前，大厅气氛紧张。我毫不夸张地说，空气既令人昏昏欲睡又仿佛带电，好像有某种预兆悬在台上。一连串官员，既讲究外交手腕又需要秩序，努力厘清规则、调节渐起的喧嚣。一位心不在焉的伯爵不停地摆弄一颗核桃，而冠军的一位姨母剥开一块蝴蝶形的点心分给众人。运送那座刻字奖杯的货运付了额外费用，任何迟到报名一律拒绝，绝无更改；委员会不会搁置任何申诉。

比赛本身看起来是无穷的乐趣。每位棋手都会拉伸思维，紧绷得像一根弹簧，时钟的刺激让每一步都因风险而显得粗糙。蛋白棒和奢侈的巧克力无人去碰。当挑战者迫使他的车合并、冠军防线上突然出现一丝破绽时，一阵荒谬而欢乐的喧嚣爆发了。那是纯粹的乐趣，连一个从隔壁动物园之行溜进来的小孩都看得懂。
