My guide, who would sometimes harangue tourists about foreign looters, grew almost hysteric with delight. "Do not let this gem rot in obscurity," he said, his voice rising in an upward sweep. "To dignify the dead is no mere imposition; it is a sacred standard."

Inside, the air smelled of old fodder and damp cartilage left from some animal that had died here. I felt a vague unease, the kind that can derive from any ruin where men once chose suicide over surrender. A single dart of light pierced a crack and seemed to envelop a heap of rusted armor. I did not complain; such moments expend the soul yet feed it.

We worked to rehabilitate the site, not to exploit it. Every gram and every ton of debris was logged. To agonize over each fragment would be unworkable, so I set a simple criterion and let the team supervise themselves while I worship, in my own quiet way, the craft that originated here. A foreign foundation had agreed to expose the find to the public through a single broadcast, a true milestone, and to underwrite the application for a grant whose value, the curator joked, was inestimable.

By dusk a small crowd had gathered. I heard one boy whistle and overhear another claim, with comic superiority, that the fort had been built to lubricate trade between two valleys. It was, of course, an exception to no rule of history; an ailment of pride had brought it down, simultaneous with famine. We loaded the last crate, dodged a passing flock of poultry, and drove home through the suburban lanes, the engine's reverse gear whining, our minds set with full concentration on the ancient lives we would, sometime soon, retroactive justice fulfilled, bring back into the light. Back at my desk, the receiver of my old phone buzzed as the museum confirmed the find; I felt a supreme calm, free at last of the low-grade neurosis that any long process can originate in a tired mind. There was no space now for doubt. With sincere sympathy for the curators who feared the project might fail, I promised to undo every careless note and rewrite the report from the start.
translation:
作为一名传记作者，也是一名瑞典纪录片制作人，在我能够探访那座被毁的要塞之前——那位著名的德国将军曾在那里苦撑过一场漫长的围城——我必须仔细研读大量满是灰尘的文件。耐心是我的长处，而这项工作需要它发挥到极致。当地向导以烤面包为生，性情上是个业余历史爱好者，他在老驿站的信箱旁与我会合，递给我一杯草药酒。

我们沿着那道棱角分明的山脊往更远处开去，经过一道深坑和一条停用的天然气管道，田野终于在我们面前展开。黎明的凉爽让我缓缓吐气。那里，半埋在细如粉末的尘土中，躺着一具石头的「尸体」：一座倒下的雕像，它那简洁的铭文，由于原来石匠并非微不足道的疏忽，刻错了国王的名字。但凭良心说，前辈学者的判断错误是无心的。我取出数码相机和勘测工具，开始记录。

该拍什么从来都不简单。我怀着深深的鉴赏之情取景那座菱形的庭院，随后忽然瞥见一枚残破印章上雕着的海豚。我的向导有时会就外国掠夺者向游客高声训话，此刻他高兴得近乎亢奋。「别让这块瑰宝在默默无闻中腐烂，」他说，声音一路上扬。「让逝者得到尊荣绝非强加之举；这是一条神圣的准则。」

里面的空气闻起来有陈旧饲料的味道，还混着某只死在此处的动物留下的潮湿软骨气息。我感到一种模糊的不安，那种不安可能源自任何一处废墟——人们曾在此选择自杀而非投降。一束光如标镖般刺过一道裂缝，仿佛包裹住一堆生锈的盔甲。我没有抱怨；这样的时刻耗费灵魂，却也滋养灵魂。

我们努力修复这处遗址，而非利用它。每一克、每一吨碎石都被登记在册。为每一块碎片苦恼是行不通的，于是我定下一个简单的标准，让团队自行监督，而我则以自己安静的方式，崇敬这里所发源的技艺。一家外国基金会同意通过一次广播把这一发现公之于众，这是个真正的里程碑，并愿意资助那笔补助金的申请——馆长打趣说，它的价值无法估量。

黄昏时，一小群人聚拢过来。我听见一个男孩吹口哨，又无意中听到另一个带着滑稽优越感的男孩宣称，这座堡垒是为润滑两座山谷之间的贸易而建的。这当然不违背任何历史规律；是傲慢这种顽疾让它倒下，与饥荒同时发生。我们装上最后一只木箱，躲开一群路过的家禽，沿着郊区的小路开车回家，引擎的倒挡发出呜呜声，我们全神贯注，心思都放在那些古人的生命上——不久之后的某一天，迟来的公正得以实现，我们会把他们重新带回光亮之中。回到书桌前，当博物馆确认这一发现时，我那部旧电话的听筒嗡嗡作响；我感到一种至高的平静，终于摆脱了任何漫长过程都可能在疲惫头脑中引发的轻度神经衰弱。此刻已没有怀疑的空间。我真诚地同情那些担心项目会失败的馆员，并承诺要撤销每一条草率的笔记，从头重写报告。

text:
Ms. Adair, a venerable Scotsman by descent and a learner all her life, ran a vocational program that taught young people to chisel stone. Her own maternal grandmother had been an elite craftswoman, and Ms. Adair was loth to let that backbone of skill fade. "Compare any chemical shortcut to honest labor," she would say, "and you will return to the chisel." Her exterior was stern, yet her cognitive frankness made students less envious than grateful.

The systematic curriculum had a predictable, seasonal pattern. In the warm months students worked outdoors until sunburn forced them under the bungalow roof beside the plantation that bordered the school. Each pupil learned to subtract waste from a block, to pave a path, and to react calmly when a piece cracked. The endurance this demanded was no small extent of their training; an unfortunate slip could ruin a week's effort, and the disillusion that followed tested even the most prepared.

One immediate problem that protracted into the eighth and seventh week was a dearth of good mineral stone. The consultant they hired, a frank elderly builder, plugged a lamp into a wall socket near the temple-shaped studio, unrolled a sketch, and spoke with exalted certainty. "This region was once a hinterland of quarries," he said. "The industry here could pave the way for a true revival. Let imagination, not mere want, guide you."

He was a namesake of a long-dead sculptor, a coincidence that delighted the dormitory at night. The students would compare their work, mourn a failed bud of an idea, and feel a strange sensation of awe at how a plump lump of clay could, once shaped, redound to their pride. To avail themselves of every chance, they categorized each tool and stowed it with care, lest a careless mishap miscarry the whole project.

Winter brought ultraviolet warnings and thinning ozone in the news, plus a tax debate that reached even this quiet northern town. Ms. Adair remained obedient to her own rule: beauty first, profit later. She would not let the dead weight of difficult bookkeeping crush the feeling of joy. When a wealthy patron offered to reimburse the cost of marble shipped from Oceania, she accepted, then used part of the cannon-shaped fountain commission to buy cosmetics and small wares as prizes.

The final show was magnificent. An orchestra played while a balloon drifted over the courtyard and a single liquid line of light traced each finished statue. Critics from as far as the upper coast came; some, plainly envious, called the work a prophecy of a new school, comparing it to the calcium-white classical busts of old. Ms. Adair only smiled. Her plasma of ambition had never been about fame. The fascination she felt, watching a once-obedient student grow bold, was reward enough. She picked up a fresh ware, examined a sneeze-inducing cloud of dust in the ambient light, and, ineffective tools set aside, named her favorite piece after the nostril-flaring lad who had carved it, sealing his adoption into the long, proud line of her craft. History, she liked to say, would vindicate the slow trades: while empires plunder and armies jam the roads in panic, while waves of emigration empty whole villages, a center of patient skill endures. She paid the marble bill in a single instalment, refused to categorize art by price, and taught each pupil to stow a tool and to name a stone with equal respect, knowing how far one lesson could ramify through a life.
translation:
阿黛尔女士，祖籍是位令人尊敬的苏格兰人，一生都是个学习者，她开办了一个职业培训项目，教年轻人雕凿石头。她自己的外祖母曾是一位精英级的女工匠，而阿黛尔女士不愿让那一脊梁般的技艺消逝。「把任何化学捷径同诚实的劳作相比，」她常说，「你最终都会回到凿子这边来。」她外表严厉，但她在认知上的坦率，让学生们与其说嫉妒，不如说感激。

这套系统化的课程有着可预测的、随季节而变的规律。温暖的月份里，学生们在户外劳作，直到晒伤迫使他们躲进与学校相邻的种植园旁那栋平房的屋顶下。每个学生都要学会从石块上削去多余的部分，铺一条路，并在作品开裂时冷静应对。这所需要的耐力，是他们训练中不小的一部分；一次不幸的失手可能毁掉一周的努力，而随之而来的幻灭，连最有准备的人也会受到考验。

一个拖延到第八周和第七周的紧迫问题，是优质矿石的匮乏。他们聘请的顾问是位坦率的年长建筑工人，他把一盏灯插进神殿状工作室旁墙上的插座，摊开一张草图，带着得意的笃定说道。「这一带从前是采石场的腹地，」他说。「这里的产业能为一场真正的复兴铺平道路。让想象力——而非单纯的匮乏——来引导你们。」

他与一位早已去世的雕刻家同名，这个巧合在夜里的宿舍里令大家欣喜。学生们会比较彼此的作品，为一个夭折的念头之芽哀叹，并对一团丰满的黏土一经塑形竟能增添他们的自豪而感到一种奇异的敬畏。为了不放过任何机会，他们给每件工具分类，小心收存，以免一次粗心的意外把整个项目搞砸、运不到目的地。

冬天带来了新闻里的紫外线警告和臭氧层变薄的消息，还有一场连这个安静北方小镇都波及到的税收辩论。阿黛尔女士仍然服从她自己的规则：美在先，利在后。她不会让繁难记账的沉重负担压垮那份喜悦的感觉。当一位富有的赞助人提出报销从大洋洲运来的大理石费用时，她接受了，随后用那笔大炮形喷泉委托项目的一部分款项买了化妆品和小物件作为奖品。

最后的展览十分壮观。一支管弦乐队演奏着，一只气球飘过庭院，一道液体般的光线勾勒出每一尊完成的雕像。远至上游海岸的评论家都来了；有些人显然心怀嫉妒，称这些作品是一个新流派的预言，把它们比作旧时钙白色的古典半身像。阿黛尔女士只是微笑。她那如血浆般的雄心从来都不是为了名声。看着一个曾经顺从的学生变得大胆，她所感到的入迷，已是足够的回报。她拿起一件新出炉的作品，在环境光中端详那团令人打喷嚏的尘埃，把低效的工具搁在一旁，用那个鼻翼张大的小伙子——正是他雕出了这件作品——的名字为自己最喜爱的作品命名，将他的「收养」封进她那条悠长而骄傲的技艺传承之中。她爱说，历史终将为这些缓慢的行当正名：当帝国掠夺、军队惊慌中堵塞道路，当一波波向外移民掏空整座村庄时，一个耐心技艺的中心却能长存。她一次付清了大理石的账单，拒绝按价格给艺术分类，并教每个学生以同样的敬意去收存一件工具、去为一块石头命名，因为她深知一堂课能在一生中分叉延展到多远。

theme: A Layman's Guide to Modern Medicine
text:
Before you assume that medicine is only for the genius or the autonomous specialist, hear a layman explain it in general terms, preferably with a daily, optimistic attitude. A famous statesman once said that to know your own body is a religious duty; that proposition, though archaic in wording, still applies. This chapter, free of the artificial gloss of a glossy commercial, will equip you with a working vocabulary.

Start with the basics. The body has a remarkable elasticity, and doctors classify symptoms much as you would sort miscellaneous items into a basket. Doctors assess each case, often unconditional in their care, and rarely fastidious to the point of cruelty. When you apply for a hospital membership or seek a loan to cover surgery, an arbitrator or auditor may review your file; an audit is routine, not a sign of guilty wrongdoing.

Consider the flu. It seems trivial, yet for a minority of patients it is grave. A neurotic or hysteric reaction helps no one; calm politeness toward the nurse in her crisp uniform does. If a downpour soaks you on the pavement and you feel sour and desolate, that misgiving is natural, but it is no excuse to wander off your itinerary of care. The gradual return of health is the true excellence.

Modern surgery can transplant organs, amputate a ruined limb, or—through vaccination—prevent disease before it can saturate the blood. A surgeon may apply hypnosis to soothe a patient, equip a ward with an amber night-light, or scatter staff across a busy floor like seeds across a field. Even the indigenous herbal lore of remote villages, once thought illusive, now informs commercial research; the gush of new data is hard to classify.

Think of the lining of a coat, or the watertight seam of a gown: each layer has a purpose. So too the body. A drunkard who poisons his liver, a widower who lets grief gush unchecked, a girl who ignores a sore throat—each underestimates the cost. The independent statesman, the religious gentleman, the Catholic nun, the granddaughter learning English from a dictionary: none is exempt. Unless we modernize our habits, we pay later.

My random advice, drawn from a magnificent body of study, is simple. Equip yourself with knowledge. Treat the prosecutor of bad health—neglect—as your enemy. When a distributorship of clinics or a wholesale supplier markets a product, read the asterisk and the fine print. A proposition that sounds too magnificent is often a preposition away from a trap. Stay sober in judgment, keep your image of health clear, and let no embankment of fear, no solvency worry, no reappraisal of your worth keep you from the dining-room of good life. Equip, assess, and act. Hospitals now employ physicists who study how a single neutron behaves in scanning equipment, and good clinics earmark funds for prevention rather than cure. Health is no narrow sexual or private secret; it is an outdoor matter of clean air and sunlight, of a quiet hour on a balcony, of returning now and then to your own birthplace to walk familiar paths. Turn your attention inwards, plant the seed of one good habit, and let it grow.
translation:
在你认定医学只属于天才或自主行事的专科医生之前，先听一位外行用通俗的话来解释它，最好带着每日的、乐观的态度。一位著名的政治家曾说，了解自己的身体是一种近乎宗教的责任；这个命题尽管措辞古老，却仍然适用。本章不带光鲜广告那种矫揉造作的浮饰，将为你配备一套实用的词汇。

从基础开始。身体具有惊人的弹性，医生给症状分类，就像你把各色各样的杂物归入一个篮子。医生评估每一个病例，常常在照护上不附带任何条件，也很少挑剔到残忍的地步。当你申请一家医院的会员资格，或寻求贷款来支付手术时，仲裁员或审计员可能会审查你的档案；审计是例行公事，并不意味着你有罪。

想想流感。它看似无足轻重，但对少数患者却很严重。神经质或歇斯底里的反应于事无补；对那位身着挺括制服的护士保持冷静的礼貌才有用。如果一场倾盆大雨在人行道上把你淋透，你感到酸楚而孤寂，那种疑虑是自然的，但这并不能成为你偏离护理日程的借口。健康的逐渐恢复才是真正的卓越。

现代外科能移植器官、截去坏死的肢体，或者——通过接种——在疾病浸透血液之前就加以预防。外科医生可以用催眠来安抚病人，给病房配一盏琥珀色的夜灯，或像把种子撒在田里那样把员工分散到繁忙的楼层。就连偏远村庄那些本土的、曾被认为虚幻的草药学问，如今也为商业研究提供信息；新数据的涌流很难归类。

想想一件外套的衬里，或一件长袍防水的接缝：每一层都有其用途。身体也是如此。一个毒害自己肝脏的酒鬼，一个任由悲伤无节制涌出的鳏夫，一个忽视喉咙痛的女孩——每个人都低估了代价。独立的政治家、虔诚的绅士、天主教修女、跟着字典学英语的孙女：无人能够例外。除非我们使自己的习惯现代化，否则日后总要付出代价。

我那随意的建议，取自一大批宏大的研究，其实很简单。给自己配备知识。把损害健康的检察官——疏忽——当作你的敌人。当一家诊所的分销机构或一个批发供应商推销产品时，要读那个星号和小字。一个听起来过于宏大的主张，往往只差一个介词就成了陷阱。在判断上保持清醒，让你健康的形象清晰，别让任何恐惧的堤坝、任何对偿付能力的担忧、任何对自身价值的重新评估，把你挡在美好生活的餐厅之外。装备、评估、行动。如今医院聘用物理学家研究单个中子在扫描设备中的行为，好的诊所会划拨专款用于预防而非治疗。健康并非狭隘的性方面或私人的秘密；它是关乎清新空气与阳光的户外之事，关乎在阳台上安静的一小时，关乎时不时回到自己的出生地，走一走熟悉的小路。把注意力转向内心，种下一个好习惯的种子，让它生长。

theme: The Carpenter Who Rebuilt the Harbor Town
text:
In July, a gorgeous southern light fell over the seashore town, and a lad named Tomas stood in the yard behind his cellar workshop. He was a carpenter by trade, habitual in his work, and the message that reached him that morning would change everything. The local economy had suffered a great loss; the old superstructure of the wharf had begun to creep and crumble, and the bottom-line of the council's budget looked bleak. The success of any repair was uncertain, but Tomas was not indifferent.

He loaded a barge with timber, a carton of nails, and a soda for the road. A migrant crew, lawful and hardworking, helped him stow the cargo. The shipping company had sent finished beams and planks fresh from a processing yard; the manufacturer's catalog, quoted at a cif price, promised they would not flatten or warp. Tomas would measure each one, for accurate measurement was, in his view, a requisite of any honest craft. He refused to portray cheap work as good; he had nothing but contempt for advertising that hid the exact truth, and he would enumerate every fault aloud rather than stay silent. A former apprentice, now a meat-shop owner, lent him an old mincer to grind paint pigment and a sack of crushed ore for ballast.

As they worked, a puppy crept near, and an old hostess from the inn brought clothes and a pan of food. The men did not grumble; even when a sudden clash of opinion threatened to creep into a quarrel, prudence and the foreman's authentic kindness restrained it. One worker tried to sneer at the slow pace, but Tomas would not let impatience curb the perfection he sought. "We must not impair the frame," he said, "nor inflict a hidden flaw that doom will later expose."

The scene was picturesque, almost like a mirage: vines on the southern wall, a robot crane lent by a modern firm, a wedge of light over the water. Tomas would wrap a splint of oak around a cracked post, compress the joint, and nail a carton-thin board to hide the seam. He would eliminate every weak point, encircle each pillar with iron, and reduce the risk of collapse. He would not be the cause of the harbor's downfall.

By nine that evening the men were awake but weary. A surgeon from the next avenue, summoned for a minor cut, praised their measurement and their nerve. The poison of doubt had no hold here. Tomas thought of the mercantile glory the town once knew, of the gaudy festivals, the wedding processions down the main avenue, the trail of lanterns along the pavement. He would liberate the town from decay. He picked up his nozzle and hose to clear the drain, flicked a flap of canvas over the fresh wood, and felt, despite the shameful neglect of years past, a clear and gorgeous hope. The language of good work, he knew, needs no translation; it speaks in every yard of solid timber. The robot crane hummed; the lad smiled; the harbor would live.

Later, in the town stadium, the mayor gave a ponderous monologue that some called epoch-making, though a blunt fisherman muttered, "Oh, fuck the speeches." Tomas, a former apprentice now a master, was decidedly calm. He would not be the judge of politics. He let his plans materialize on paper, enumerate each cost on a line marked cif for the imported steel and iron ore, and restrain his impatient hopes. The automatic saw whirred near a vine-covered fence. A neighbor used a mincer to prepare stuffing for a roast, and Tomas would cook a simple supper at home. He clutched a worn garment to his bosom against the night chill. This was no fiction, no idle processing of dreams; it was real work, and it was good.
translation:
七月，一片绚丽的南方光线洒在这座海滨小镇上，一个名叫托马斯的小伙子站在他地窖作坊后面的院子里。他以木匠为业，做事一向有条不紊，而那天早上传到他这里的消息将改变一切。当地经济遭受了巨大的损失；码头那座古老的上层建筑开始一点点下陷崩塌，议会预算的结余看上去一片黯淡。任何修缮能否成功都没有把握，但托马斯并不漠然。

他往一艘驳船上装了木料、一纸箱钉子，还有路上喝的一瓶汽水。一支合法又勤劳的外来工队帮他把货物装好。航运公司送来了成品横梁和加工好的木板；制造商的目录承诺它们不会被压平或翘曲。托马斯会一一测量，因为在他看来，精确的测量是任何诚实手艺的必备条件。他拒绝把劣质活儿粉饰成好活儿；对于那种隐瞒确切真相的广告，他只有轻蔑。

他们干活时，一只小狗悄悄凑近，客栈里一位上了年纪的女主人送来衣服和一锅食物。工人们没有抱怨；即便突然爆发的意见冲突险些演变成争吵，谨慎和工头那真诚的善意也将它克制住了。一个工人想要嘲笑这缓慢的进度，但托马斯不让急躁束缚他所追求的完美。"我们绝不能损害骨架，"他说，"也不能埋下某个迟早会被厄运揭露的隐患。"

这景象如画，几乎像一场海市蜃楼：南墙上的葡萄藤，一家现代公司借来的机器人吊车，水面上一楔形的光。托马斯用一片橡木夹板箍住一根开裂的柱子，压紧接口，又钉上一块纸盒般薄的木板来遮住接缝。他要消除每一处薄弱点，用铁把每根支柱环绕加固，降低坍塌的风险。他不愿成为这港口衰败的原因。

到那晚九点，工人们虽然醒着却已疲惫。隔壁大街上请来处理小伤口的一位外科医生，称赞了他们的测量和胆识。怀疑的毒药在这里没有立足之地。托马斯想起小镇昔日的商业辉煌，想起那些华丽的节庆、沿主街而行的婚礼队伍、人行道上一串串灯笼的痕迹。他要把小镇从腐朽中解放出来。他拿起喷嘴和水管去清理排水沟，往新木料上掀过一片帆布，尽管为过去多年那可耻的疏忽而感慨，却感到一种清晰而绚丽的希望。他知道，好手艺的语言无需翻译；它在每一码结实的木料中诉说着。机器人吊车嗡嗡作响；小伙子微笑了；港口将会活下去。

后来，在镇上的露天运动场里，市长发表了一通冗长的独白，有人称之为划时代之作，尽管一个直率的渔夫嘟囔道：「哦，去他的演讲吧。」托马斯——一个昔日的学徒，如今已是师傅——显得格外平静。他不愿做政治的评判者。他让自己的计划在纸上成形，在标着「到岸价」的一行里逐项列出进口钢材和铁矿石的成本，并克制住自己急躁的期望。一台自动锯在爬满葡萄藤的篱笆旁嗡嗡作响。一位邻居用一台绞肉机准备烤肉的填料，托马斯则要回家做一顿简单的晚饭。他把一件破旧的衣服紧紧搂在胸前，抵御夜里的寒意。这不是虚构，不是对梦想的空洞加工；这是实实在在的活儿，而且是好活儿。

theme: A Hot-Air Balloon Festival in April
text:
Certainly the most romantic event of our year is the ballooning festival held in April, nearly at the foot of the mountain. To be there at sunrise, when the first balloon lifts and the music of a brass band drifts over the field, is to feel a zest for life that no trivial worry can deprive. I will reply at once to anyone who calls it a freak hobby: they simply have not tried it.

My uncle, a grown-up who never lost his boyish wonder, taught me the trade. He would shave at dawn, light his pipe of tobacco, and forecast the weather with an accuracy the local viewer of the weekly broadcast could not match. "The wind is erratic today," he might gasp, half out of breath, "so we adjust the diet of fuel and watch the gauge." His anatomy of the sport was thorough, almost clinical, and his yearly notes filled a thick file.

Conversely, my aunt was inefficient with the ropes but a genius with people. She would console a nervous first-timer, hush a crying child, and offer popcorn and a glass of champagne to anyone who looked pale. Her zest for hospitality gave the festival its prominence. She disliked any deception; a salesman who once tried a sly back-call to upsell cheap gear got a mute, distinctly cold reply, and she sent him off with a limp wave and a yawn.

The science is real, though laymen find it enlightening. Hot air, being neutral and light, expands and the craft rises; cool it, and you descend. A periodic check of the network of valves is mandatory. One must never underestimate the strain on the fabric, nor the threat of a sudden gust. A humanitarian group even uses balloons to extend aid to flooded country districts, dropping a supplementary parcel of food where a bomber could never safely fly.

This year a contemporary artist, a luxurious dreamer with a fortune to spend, painted his balloon to imply the migration of the locust and the grasshopper across the plain, a strange but striking image. A clinical psychologist gave a talk on the temptation to overwork and the suffering it breeds; she urged us, economically and otherwise, to rest. Even the Egyptian consul, a descendant of an ancient line, came to acclaim the show, and an old Swede in a tan jacket wept with quiet pity at its beauty.

As the climax neared, fifty balloons rose at once. A volley of cheers went up. The performance was flawless. I felt my own anguish of the past year drain away. To imply that such joy is mere escape would be to misread it; it is, rather, a phase of healing. Whatever doom the headlines forecast, here, for a few apiece-bought hours, we were free. The literature of flight, I thought, is written not in books but in the slow, ginger ascent of a single bright sphere into the morning sky, and I would borrow that feeling for as long as I could, fully and without apology, before the limp ropes called me back to earth and sleep.

If I must give a brief synopsis of why this endeavor matters, it is this. The festival's success rate is no accident; we accommodate every type of visitor, from the child who points at a platypus drawn on a banner to the retiree living on a modest annuity. Volunteers work overtime without complaint, and any variance in the schedule is posted early so no one need disagree at the gate. We expand the program each year, replacing every dead bulb in the night display and refusing to let litter contaminate the field. The crew that runs the propane will not trigger a single careless spark; safety is the prime ingredient of the whole affair. No one is dragged off to jail for a mistake, but no one cuts a corner either.
translation:
我们这一年里最浪漫的活动，无疑是四月间几乎在山脚下举行的热气球节。在日出时分到场，当第一只气球升起、铜管乐队的音乐飘过原野时，你会感到一种对生活的热情，是任何琐碎烦恼都无法夺走的。对任何把它称作古怪嗜好的人，我会立刻回应：他们只是没尝试过罢了。

我叔叔是个从未失去孩童般好奇心的成年人，是他把这门手艺教给我的。他会在黎明刮胡子，点上一斗烟草，预报天气的准确度连每周收看广播的本地观众都比不上。"今天的风变化无常，"他会气喘吁吁地说，"所以我们要调整燃料的'食量'，盯着仪表看。"他对这项运动的剖析十分透彻，几乎像临床分析，他每年的笔记装满了厚厚一摞文件。

相反，我婶婶摆弄绳索很笨拙，可与人打交道却是个天才。她会安慰紧张的新手，哄住啼哭的孩子，给任何脸色发白的人递上爆米花和一杯香槟。她待客的热情让这个节庆声名远扬。她讨厌任何欺骗；曾有个推销员想用狡猾的二次回访来推销廉价装备，得到的是一个无声、明显冷淡的答复，她对他懒懒地挥挥手、打个呵欠，就把他打发走了。

其中的科学是真实的，尽管外行人觉得颇受启发。热空气呈中性且轻盈，受热膨胀，飞行器便上升；让它冷却，你就下降。对那一整套阀门网络进行周期性检查是强制规定。人绝不能低估织物所承受的拉力，也不能低估突发阵风的威胁。一个人道主义团体甚至用气球把援助延伸到被洪水淹没的乡村地区，把额外的一包食物投放到轰炸机永远无法安全飞抵的地方。

今年，一位当代艺术家——一个挥金如土、爱好奢华的梦想家——把他的气球画成暗示蝗虫和蚂蚱跨越平原迁徙的图案，怪异却引人注目。一位临床心理学家做了演讲，谈到过度工作的诱惑及其滋生的苦难；她敦促我们无论从节俭还是其他角度都要休息。连那位古老家族的后裔、埃及领事也来为演出喝彩，一位穿棕褐色夹克的老瑞典人则带着安静的怜惜为它的美而落泪。

临近高潮时，五十只气球同时升空。一阵欢呼齐声爆发。表演完美无缺。我感到过去一年自身的痛苦渐渐流走。若把这种喜悦仅仅说成逃避，那是误读了它；它毋宁是疗愈的一个阶段。无论报纸头条预报了怎样的厄运，在这里，在花钱买来的、每人各享的几个钟头里，我们是自由的。我想，飞行的文学并不写在书本里，而写在一只明亮的球体缓慢、谨慎地升入晨空的过程中，我会尽可能久地借用那种感觉，毫无保留，毫不道歉，直到松垂的绳索把我唤回大地与睡眠。

如果我必须简要概述这项事业为何重要，那就是这样。节庆的成功率绝非偶然；我们容纳每一类访客，从指着横幅上画的鸭嘴兽的孩子，到靠微薄年金生活的退休者。志愿者们毫无怨言地加班，日程上任何变动都会提早公布，免得有人在入口处不一致而起争执。我们每年扩展项目，更换夜间灯光秀里每一只坏掉的灯泡，绝不让垃圾污染场地。负责丙烷的工作组不会触发任何一星半点粗心的火花；安全是整个活动最首要的成分。没人会因一个失误被拖进监狱，但也没人偷工减料。

theme: The Painter and the Physicist Next Door
text:
The inn at the edge of the wharf was a busy place, though its grey walls and the stain on the canvas awning made it look secondhand and dismal. Inside, a thin painter sat at a table set with a menu, a jar of olive paste, and a bowl of fresh carrot scraps the cook had spared. He had come to rest, yet his mind was an ongoing racket of ideas. He pinned a sketch to the wall with a single thumbtack, his old passion for science still burning like a fire. He would not divorce his art from science, he said, and that very claim made his work fresh. Painting, for him, was no juvenile pastime nor mere moonlighting; it was a manufactured world standing on the surface of real laws.

Next door, behind a rug that hung as a curtain, lived a quiet professor, a mathematician and sometime biologist who lectured on the curriculum of optics at the college to the south. His tongue could explain the theory of relativity as easily as a child describes snow. He spoke of the acceleration of light, the breadth of a sphere of stars, and the impact of one idea upon another, all as themselves a kind of mathematics. The painter would lick the tip of his brush, pinch a fresh tube of grey, and listen.

"Evidence," the professor said, "is the prime burden of any honest mind. A terrorist or a gangster hides behind secrecy; a scientist must seek publicity for the truth." He held that a society too permissive of deception would breed its own deadly decline. He would rejoice at a clean proof and grumble at a sloppy one. Computerization, he admitted, had changed his field; an ultrasonic scan or an X-ray now gave data he once had to fumble for by hand.

The painter, for his part, worked in a different setting. He would twist a coil of wire into a small sculpture, nestle it on a perch of driftwood, and call it done. A vender at the next stall, eager to repute himself a critic, would sneer, but the painter was willing to ignore him. Context, he believed, was everything; a scrap of color in the right place had more competence to move the heart than a whole museum.

One grey morning the two men shared a check-up of the season's plans. The professor proposed a referendum among their friends: should they introduce a joint show, mixing canvas and equation? The painter, twisting a grin, agreed at once. "A minimum of fuss," he said, "and a maximum of wonder." They would not conceal their aim. To honor an old appointment, they invited the grandparent of a neighbor, a retired commune organizer, to open it. As the snow fell outside in stillness, they drew up the plan, signed it, asked a witness to countersign, and felt, for the first time in a year, that their separate spheres had at last begun to touch. The next phase, they agreed, would prove harder, but neither doubted it was worth the burden, nor that the glamour of the idea would, in time, repay every hour of their labor.

There was, of course, no doubt left in their minds. They would seek no asylum in vanity and take no bribe of easy praise. The professor, who had once worked in banking before fleeing a capitalist scheme he found offensive, spoke of an intermediate plan: a slow removal of every weak piece, then a fierce, tigress-like defense of the strong. As a former defendant in a petty dispute, he knew how the law could repeal goodwill overnight; he would not contend with rivals of high rank, nor try to repatriate old grudges. He swatted a mosquito, smiled at the odd mixture of paint and chalk dust on his sleeve, and let the matter rest.
translation:
码头边的那家小客栈是个热闹的地方，尽管它灰色的墙壁和帆布雨篷上的污渍让它看上去像二手的，显得阴郁。屋内，一个瘦削的画家坐在一张桌前，桌上摆着菜单、一罐橄榄酱，还有一碗厨师匀出来的新鲜胡萝卜碎。他是来休息的，可他的脑子却是一连串源源不断的吵闹念头。他说他不会把自己的艺术与科学分离，正是这一主张让他的作品显得新鲜。

隔壁，在一张当帘子挂着的地毯后面，住着一位安静的教授，他是数学家，偶尔也搞生物学，在南边的学院里讲授光学课程。他的口才能像孩子描述雪一样轻松地解释相对论。他谈到光的加速度、群星这一球面的广度，以及一个想法对另一个想法的冲击，把这一切都当作某种数学。画家会舔舔画笔尖，捏一捏一管新鲜的灰色颜料，听他讲。

"证据，"教授说，"是任何诚实头脑的首要负担。恐怖分子或歹徒躲在秘密之后；科学家则必须为真理寻求公开。"他认为，一个对欺骗过分纵容的社会会孕育出自身致命的衰落。遇到一个干净的证明他会欣喜，遇到一个草率的便会发牢骚。他承认，计算机化改变了他的领域；如今一次超声扫描或一张X光片就能给出他过去得靠手摸索才能得到的数据。

画家这边则在另一种环境里工作。他会把一卷电线拧成一件小雕塑，安放在一根浮木的栖木上，就算完工。隔壁摊位上一个小贩，急于把自己说成评论家，会冷笑，但画家乐意不去理他。他相信，背景就是一切；一抹放对地方的颜色，比整座博物馆更有打动人心的能力。

一个灰色的清晨，两人一起核对这一季的计划。教授提议在朋友间搞一次公民投票：他们该不该推出一个把油画与方程混在一起的联展？画家咧嘴一笑，立刻同意。"麻烦最少，"他说，"惊奇最多。"他们不会隐瞒自己的目的。为了履行一个旧约，他们邀请一位邻居的祖辈——一位退休的公社组织者——来为展览揭幕。当外面的雪在寂静中飘落时，他们拟好计划，签了字，请一位见证人会签，并第一次在这一年里感到，他们各自分隔的领域终于开始触碰。他们一致认为，下一阶段会更难，但谁也不怀疑这份负担是值得的，也不怀疑这个想法的魅力终将报偿他们劳作的每一个小时。

当然，他们心中已不再有任何怀疑。他们不会在虚荣中寻求庇护，也不会接受廉价赞美的贿赂。教授曾在银行业工作过，后来逃离了一项他认为令人反感的资本主义计划，他谈起一个中间方案：先慢慢拆除每一处薄弱构件，再像母虎一样凶猛地守护牢固的部分。作为一桩小纠纷里曾经的被告，他深知法律能在一夜之间撤销人们的善意；他不会与高位的对手争斗，也不会试图把旧日的怨恨遣返回来。他拍死一只蚊子，对袖子上颜料与粉笔灰那奇特的混合物笑了笑，便把这件事搁下了。

theme: The Museum of an Ancient Trade
text:
On a small Pacific island once governed from far-off Ireland, an academic named Dr. Pace set out to update an old museum and popularize the craftsmanship of an ancient trade. The building was situated near the water, where the salt would seep into the foundations and the monsoon each year tested its defence against the storms. Currently the place was dismal, and visitors were few; he wanted to reconcile its dusty past with a complete, living future.

He began at the entrance aisle, where a thermostat now kept the rooms at an even temperature. On the wall, a fresh rubric printed in bold gave each exhibit a heading, and a glossy booklet served as a handout for every receiver. With a screwdriver and a steady forefinger he fixed a loose label himself; he would not mishandle a single relic, nor conceal a flaw, for he was a truthful and loyal man whose heart lay in honest work. He took care not to violate the spirit of the original collection, regardless of pressure from the new publisher who funded the show.

The museum told the historical story of the island's peasantry. There were tools worn smooth by use: an opener of carved bone, a jar that once held shrimp paste, a saucer of fired clay, and a cylinder press a long-dead inventor had built. A soldier's costume hung beside a robber's mask used in an old pageant. Glass cases displayed how the people would breed cattle, drive a single loyal cow to market, and hatch fish in shallow furrows of seawater. Nearby, a faded magazine described the whaling crews who once braved the deep, hunting prey that could, like a brute, smash a boat to foam.

Not everyone approved. One entrenched landowner, indignant and pathetic in his anger, claimed the new tenancy of the building was disadvantageous to him and contractual in name only. He sent an uncalled-for letter, almost a threat, hinting he might alienate the island's council. Dr. Pace refused to be intimidated. He would not let such a brow-beating rebel spoil the work. "I can compute the cost," he said calmly in the auditorium, before a united assembly of townsfolk and one visiting pedlar, "and I can vouch that the benefit is greater. This is no pretense."

When the doors finally opened, the response was dazzling. A batch of schoolchildren filed past the blackboard where a guide chalked diagrams; their applause, free of any conventional restraint, filled the hall. A delectable smell of grilled hamburger and pineapple drifted from a stall outside, near the toilet block and the rows of lockers. The influence of the day was plain: people lingered, took notes in pencil, and left with a sense that the development of their own heritage was, at last, an entity worth protecting. By the eleventh hour, as a soft mist rose over the dorsal ridge of the hills, Dr. Pace stood alone, secure in the secrecy of his own quiet pride, certain the museum's slippery decline had been halted and that no ruinous neglect, no susceptible weakness, no death of memory would claim it again. The campaign for publicity had not been ruthless; he had simply let people pass through and see for themselves. A retired farmer, his face lined like a furrow, said the museum had restored his failing eyesight for the past. A college choir sang beside an old stereo, and a reformed gangster, now a generous giver to charity, donated a relic from another continent, its outward shine masking a deeper worth. He offered no apology for caring so much, and added the day's small disbursement to the ledger with a deposit of hope. The inference, written therein, was simple: a trade preserved is a people preserved, and the strategy had worked.
translation:
在一座曾由遥远的爱尔兰管辖的太平洋小岛上，一位名叫佩斯博士的学者着手翻新一座旧博物馆，并推广一门古老行业的手艺。这座建筑坐落在水边，盐分会渗进地基，每年的季风都考验着它抵御风暴的防御能力。眼下这地方很阴郁，参观者寥寥；他想让它尘封的过去与一个完整而鲜活的未来相调和。

他从入口的通道着手，那里如今装了一台自动调温器，使各个房间保持均匀的温度。墙上，一行粗体印刷的新题目给每件展品都加了标题，一本光亮的小册子作为发给每位参观者的资料。他用一把螺丝刀和稳稳的食指亲手修好了一块松动的标签牌；他不会把任何一件文物装卸不慎，也不会掩盖瑕疵，因为他是个诚实而忠诚的人，他的心系于诚实的工作。无论出资办展的新出版商如何施压，他都注意不去违背原始藏品的精神。

博物馆讲述了岛上农民的历史故事。这里有被使用磨得光滑的工具：一把骨雕的起子、一只曾盛过虾酱的罐子、一个陶土碟子，还有一台早已作古的发明者造的圆筒压机。一套士兵的服装挂在一副旧庆典上用过的强盗面具旁。玻璃柜里展示着人们如何养牛、把唯一一头忠实的奶牛赶去集市，以及在浅浅的海水犁沟里孵鱼。附近一本褪色的杂志描述了当年勇闯深海的捕鲸队，他们追猎的猎物能像野兽一样把船撞成泡沫。

并非人人赞同。一位根深蒂固的地主，愤怒得既义愤又可怜，声称这座建筑新的租赁对他不利、徒有合同之名。他寄来一封多余的信，几乎是威胁，暗示他可能离间岛上议会。佩斯博士拒绝被恐吓。他不会让这样一个咄咄逼人的反叛者搅黄这项工作。"我能算出成本，"他在礼堂里平静地说，面对镇民们团结一致的集会和一名路过的小贩，"我也能担保收益更大。这不是装样子。"

当大门终于打开时，反响令人目眩。一批小学生鱼贯走过黑板，导览员在上面用粉笔画着图；他们的掌声毫无惯常的拘束，充满了大厅。外面一个摊位飘来烤汉堡和菠萝的诱人香味，就在厕所那栋楼和一排储物柜附近。这一天的影响显而易见：人们流连忘返，用铅笔记笔记，离开时都觉得自己这份遗产的发展终于成了一个值得保护的实体。到了第十一个小时，当一层薄雾在山丘的脊背上升起时，佩斯博士独自站着，安然守着自己那份静默骄傲的秘密，确信博物馆那滑坡式的衰退已被遏止，再没有毁灭性的疏忽、没有易受影响的弱点、没有记忆的死亡能够把它夺走。这场争取宣传的行动并不残酷无情；他只是让人们走进来，自己去看。一位退休农夫，脸上的皱纹深如犁沟，说博物馆为他修复了对往昔那衰退的「视力」。一支大学合唱团在一台旧立体声音响旁歌唱，一名改邪归正、如今对慈善慷慨解囊的前歹徒，捐出了一件来自另一个大洲的遗物，它外表的光泽掩盖着更深的价值。他不为自己如此用心而道歉，并把这一天小小的支出连同一份希望的存款记入账册。其中写下的推论很简单：一门行业被保存，便是一个民族被保存——这套策略奏效了。

theme: The Queen's Charity and the Village Choir
text:
It is imperative, the local nun told the children, that we aid one another, for assistance freely given is always preferable to charity offered as a mere pretense. She ran a small parish in the countryside, and her every speech, however short, seemed worthy of a hymn. Her nephew, a quiet boy with bright eyes, would imitate her grammar and her gentle tone, and the likeness made the whole village smile.

The parish was situated where a green hill adjoins a winding river, and a single subway line, recently extended, now linked it to the town. The local landlord, a firm but fair man whose family firm supplied grain to half the region, had agreed to cater the harvest feast. He would not intimidate his tenants; rather, he sought to animate the place, to lift its spirits and lessen its old burdens. He could vouch, he said, that the coming year held plenty.

This year the queen herself, regardless of the long road, planned to visit. The news did not awaken envy so much as joy. The children rehearsed a dialog and a cartoon-bright pageant; the choir practiced until each voice could equal the next. A supplier of costumes sent a label-stamped crate of robes; a subsidiary of the town's largest firm donated a film crew to record the day. Deliberately and with care, the committee drew up a heading for every part of the program and asked the bishop to verify the order of events.

Science, too, had its place. A visiting teacher gave a talk on the molecular life of the soil, on the coefficient of growth in a healthy field, and on how a single seed, properly tended, could split, sprout, and never degenerate into waste. He showed an X-ray of a leaf and a generalization drawn from years of study, urging the children to envisage a future they could build. "Do not imitate the past blindly," he said. "Replace what fails; encourage what works."

The day arrived under a thin mist that soon lifted. A bead of dew still clung to every blade as the queen stepped from her car, her face calm, her hair tied back with a simple band. She walked the avenue between two rows of villagers, paused before the firm's new opener-and-canning works, and admired the craftsmanship of the local carvers. A drunk reveler at the rear was gently steered away, and a robber who had once plagued the lanes was, the constable noted with herein-recorded relief, long since gone.

Her speech was outstanding, an everyday warmth raised to something fine. She spoke of equal worth, of the interaction between a people and their land, of a life lived in service. She did not animate the crowd with grand pretense but with truth. When she finished, the choir sang their hymn; the sound seemed to constrict every throat with feeling, then release it in applause. Even the oldest husband and wife, side by side near the locker of old tools, wept. The whaling songs of the coast, the booklet of local lore, the romanticism of the poets—all of it, for one bright afternoon, seemed to hold together, and no ruinous doubt could touch it. In the days that followed, a quiet change settled over the parish. Each parent helped to manufacture the small adornment that would complement the festival hall, whose lower wing would adjoin a new pioneering workshop. A printed description, no longer than a ticket stub, hung by the door, and the nun would impress upon every child that they shall keep the place clean. They learned to separate the good seed from the empty shell, to read a thermometer and note the temperature, and to eke out a living without bitterness. No coffin of old grief, no memory of past persecution, could lower their hope. Within the small arena of the courtyard, a boy with an artistic bent dropped a dime into the box for the eleventh time that week, a modest disbursement toward a brighter year, and the nun, her duty done, gave silent thanks for a day so plainly worthy of memory.
translation:
当地的修女对孩子们说，我们彼此相助是绝对必要的，因为自愿给予的援助，永远胜过仅仅作为借口而施舍的慈善。她在乡下管理着一个小教区，她的每一次讲话，无论多短，似乎都配得上一首赞美诗。她的侄子是个安静、眼睛明亮的男孩，会模仿她的语法和温和的语气，那相似的样子让整个村子都笑了。

这个教区坐落在一座青山与一条蜿蜒河流相接的地方，一条最近延长的地铁线如今把它与镇子连了起来。当地的地主是个既坚定又公道的人，他家的公司向半个地区供应粮食，他答应承办这场丰收宴。他不会恐吓佃户；相反，他想给这地方注入活力，提振它的精神，减轻它旧日的负担。他说，他可以担保来年会有丰足的收成。

今年女王本人不顾路途遥远，计划前来访问。这消息唤起的与其说是嫉妒，不如说是喜悦。孩子们排练了一段对话和一场卡通般明快的庆典游行；唱诗班练习到每个声部都能与另一个相称。一家服装供应商送来一只贴着标签的长袍木箱；镇上最大公司的一家子公司捐出一支摄制组来记录这一天。委员会审慎而用心地为节目的每一部分拟定标题，并请主教核实活动的顺序。

科学也有它的位置。一位客座教师做了演讲，讲土壤的分子生命，讲健康田地里的生长系数，以及一粒种子若悉心照料，如何能裂开、发芽，永不退化为废物。他展示了一片树叶的X光片和一条由多年研究得出的概括，敦促孩子们去构想一个他们能够建造的未来。"不要盲目模仿过去，"他说。"替换掉失败的，鼓励奏效的。"

这一天到来时天上覆着一层薄雾，很快便消散了。女王下车时，每一片草叶上仍挂着一颗露珠，她神情平静，头发用一条简单的发带束在脑后。她走在两排村民之间的大道上，在公司新建的开罐与罐装工坊前停下，赞赏当地雕刻匠人的手艺。后排一个喝醉的狂欢者被轻轻引开，而一个曾在巷子里为患的强盗，警员在此记下并松了口气，早已不知去向。

她的讲话十分出色，把日常的温暖提升为某种美好的东西。她谈到平等的价值，谈到一个民族与他们土地之间的相互作用，谈到一生奉献于服务。她不是用浮夸的伪装来鼓动人群，而是用真理。当她讲完时，唱诗班唱起赞美诗；那声音仿佛让每一个喉咙都因情感而收紧，随后又在掌声中将它释放。连那对最年长的夫妇，并肩站在装旧工具的储物柜旁，也落了泪。海岸的捕鲸歌谣、当地传说的小册子、诗人们的浪漫主义——所有这一切，在这个明亮的下午，似乎都融为一体，没有任何毁灭性的怀疑能够触碰它。在随后的日子里，一种宁静的变化笼罩了整个教区。每位家长都帮着制作那些小装饰，用以衬托节庆礼堂，礼堂较低的侧翼将与一间新建的开创性工坊相邻。门旁挂着一份不超过一张票根长的印刷说明，修女会让每个孩子铭记：他们应当保持这里整洁。他们学会把好的种子与空壳分开，学会读温度计、记录温度，学会不带怨恨地勉力谋生。任何旧日悲伤的棺材、任何过去迫害的记忆，都无法压低他们的希望。在院子这小小的竞技场里，一个有艺术天赋的男孩这一周第十一次把一枚一角硬币投进箱子，这是为更光明的一年所做的一笔微薄支出。修女在尽完本分之后，为这样一个分明值得铭记的日子默默道谢。

text:
As a seasoned traveler keeping a concise journal, I learned that the best stories refuse to stay dormant. Last weekday I would skip the noisy main road and resort to a quiet harbour cafe near my neighborhood, a public spot open to all, where I could chat over a pint of creamy coffee whilst watching a pigeon and a stray duck nibble crumbs by the water. The place was never pretentious, and no rival cafe could match it; its diverse, multifarious crowd would mingle freely — a poet scribbling in ink, an off-duty meteorologist reading a weather catalogue, and a retired stevedore who once loaded a railroad car with platinum and pyrite ore.

The owner, a sentimental woman with great tact, kept attendance low so guests could concentrate. She told a tale about an ancient treaty signed under moonlight, when a marshal had to salute a pharaoh-like ruler and a mercenary army was ordered, by sudden directive, to disobey and lay down arms. To suppress a coming war, both sides reached a concerted agreement; the threat of mutual extinction was suddenly null.

I am no spy, just a self-taught listener with a quiet internal compass. Over the years a cumulative habit of note-taking gave me real leeway to wander. I have seen a helicopter descend onto land caked with mud, watched a comet streak across the cosmos, and studied plankton through an audio-visual lab on a university campus. None of it felt stupid or pretentious; every tangible detail mattered, and curiosity was the deciding factor.

That morning the harbour traffic sat stationary, a market slump having idled the docks. A trumpet rang out as a sports rally passed; runners completed one more lap, their steady rhythm broken only by one athlete's minor injury from a deflection off a metal bar. A spike in the crowd surged forward like a turning wheel, then settled behind a low hedge.

The handicapped veteran beside me, a man whose liver troubles made him avoid anything alcoholic, raised his cup as if to shield a memory. He had begun to wear the same coat for years. The separation of past and present, he said, is the only real disadvantage of growing old. There was no absurdity in that; it was the plain truth, hard-won on an arduous road. He would never hitchhike, never scheme for profit, and refused to let nostalgia dominate his days. Each cog of his routine turned with purpose, and that, more than money, was what spread warmth through the room.
translation:
作为一名经验丰富、记着简明日记的旅行者，我懂得最好的故事拒绝沉睡。上一个工作日，我会避开喧闹的主干道，转而去我家附近一间安静的海港咖啡馆——一个向所有人开放的公共场所，在那里我可以一边喝着一品脱奶油咖啡闲聊，一边看着一只鸽子和一只野鸭在水边轻啻面包屑。这地方从不自命不凡，没有哪家竞争对手的咖啡馆能与之婲美；形形色色、各式各样的人群在此自由地交融——一位用墨水涂写的诗人、一位下班后翻看天气目录的气象学者，还有一位曾把白金和黄铁矿装上铁路车厢的退休码头工人。

店主是位多愁善感、极有分寸的女子，她让出席人数保持很少，好让客人能专心。她讲了一个故事，关于一份在月光下签署的古老条约：当时一位元帅不得不向一位法老般的统治者致敬，而一支唯利是图的雇佣军接到突然的命令，要他们违抗军令、放下武器。为了镇压一场即将到来的战争，双方达成了一致商定的协议；相互灭绝的威胁顿时归于无效。

我不是间谍，只是一个自学成才的倾听者，怀着一只安静的内在指南针。多年来，做笔记的累积习惯给了我真正自由活动的余地去四处漫游。我见过直升机降落在沾满泥浆的陆地上，看过彗星划过宇宙，还在大学校园的视听实验室里研究过浮游生物。这一切都不显得愚蠢或做作；每一个切实的细节都很重要，而好奇心正是决定性的因素。

那天早晨，海港的交通停滞不动，一场市场不景气让码头闲置下来。一阵喇叭声响起，一支体育集会队伍经过；跑者们又跑完一圈，他们平稳的节奏只被一名运动员的小伤打断——那伤来自一根金属横杠的偏斜反弹。人群中突然涌起一股尖峰，像转动的车轮般向前冲，随后又退到一道低矮的树篩后面。

坐在我旁边那位有残疑的老兵，肝脏不好，因而避开任何含酒精的东西，他举起杯子，仿佛要守护一段记忆。他已经把同一件外套穿了好多年。他说，过去与现在的分离，是变老唯一真正的不利之处。这话里没有荒唐；它是朴实的真理，在一条艰苦的路上来之不易。他从不免费搭便车，从不为牟利而算计，也拒绝让怀旧主宰他的日子。他日常生活的每一个齿轮都带着目的转动，而这一点，比金钱更能把温暖散布到整个房间。

text:
My visit to the western coast began the day a shipowner agreed to sue a rival over a counter-offer he called dreadful. The dispute was largely about an oyster bed, a sizable quarry of shells that, since birth, had been the root of his livelihood. To examine the case, a judge had to mediate, and I was asked to learn the facts and dig into the records, however unbearable the informal paperwork seemed. Each tertiary clause, each segment of every proceeding, demanded a thorough reading.

The shipowner was a sturdy, capable man, righteous yet fickle, and his enthusiasm could strike you as both lasting and dreadful at once. He would catch the morning tide, drink a mug of strong tea, eat a slab of pudding, and clock in before the sunset of the previous day had even faded. A handful of workers, mostly young, would slack off whenever he turned his back, an idleness he could not suppress. To thrash out a routine, he set a strict mandate that would prohibit lateness; worse, he said, than a slave's chains is a blunder born of plain stupidity.

He explained, with vividness, how a hydraulic pulley driven by a small propellent pump in good tune could raise oysters from the muscle-aching deep. The requirement was teamwork; collaboration, he insisted, was elemental, and to retain a crew you must reward them, and vice versa. Cheap pay breeds envy and a consequent sense of inferiority, whereas a fair, sectional bonus can inspire loyalty. People misconceive the work, giving the wrong attribution to luck; in truth diligence helps a crew earn and even transcend old limits. He refused the abortion of a plan once begun, and would not displace a loyal hand for an obsolete machine.

Yesterday I learned how the trade had changed over a millennium. Methods once thorough are now obsolete; analogous to how an apple cart yields to a truck, the old ways lapse and a new governor of commerce takes the helm. Exposure to harsh weather can quiz even the bravest, like an infernal assault from the sea, and any lapse in care brings ruin. The work is intensive, the abundance never guaranteed, even when Jupiter shines bright as a far beacon and trade with Brazil booms.

As the rosy dawn rose over the gutter of a quiet street, a dancer in a clean collar walked by; a hen pecked at scraps, and a chemist, up early, examined a vial. The shipowner tugged his collar and, with a polite nod to a passing madam, returned to the cover of his shed to wait for the verdict.
translation:
我对西部海岸的那次拜访，始于一位船东决定就一份他称之为糟糕透顶的还价起诉其对手的那一天。争议主要围绕一片牟蛎床——那是一处可观的贝壳采集场，从他出生起就是他生计的根本。为了审查此案，法官得出面调解，而我被要求去了解事实并深挖记录，哪怕那些非正式的文件看似令人难以忍受。每一条第三级条款，每一份诉讼程序的每一段，都需要认真阅读。

这位船东强壮而有能力，正直却反复无常，他的热情可能让你觉得既持久又可怕。他会赶上清晨的潮水，喝一杯浓茶，吃一块布丁，赶在前一天的日落还未退去之前就打卡上工。一小批工人多半年轻，一转身就偷懒，这种懒散他无法压制。为了碎定一套规矩，他定下严格的指令禁止迟到；他说，比奴隶的锁链更糟的，是由赤裸裸的愚蠢造成的失误。

他生动地解释了一台由小型推进泵驱动的液压滑轮，只要调校得当，就能从让肌肉酸痛的深处括起牟蛎。要求是团队协作；他坚持说，协作是最基本的，要留住一支队伍就必须奖励他们，反之亦然。廉价的薪酬会滋生嫩妒与随之而来的自卑感，而公平的分部奖金则能激发忠诚。人们误解这份工作，错误地归因于运气；其实勤奋能让队伍赚钱，甚至超越旧日的极限。他拒绝中途放弃已经动工的计划，也不会为了一台过时的机器而辞退一名忠心的伙计。

昨天我了解了这一行业在一千年里的变迁。曾经周到的方法如今已过时；就像苹果手推车让位于卡车一样，旧方式逐渐消退，一位新的商业主宰接过舷柄。他警告说，暴露于恶劣天气中会考验哪怕是最勇敢的人，如同来自大海的可怖袭击，任何护理上的疏忽都会带来毁灭。这份工作强度大，丰收从不可保证，即使木星像遥远的灯塔一样明亮、与巴西的贸易蓬勃也一样。

当玫瑰色的黎明升起，映在一条宁静街道的阳沟上时，一位身着干净衣领的舞者走过；一只母鸡在启碎屑，一位早起的化学家正在检验一小瓶样本。船东拽了拽衣领，对路过的一位太太礼貌地点头，随后退回他棚屋的遮蔽处，等待裁决。

text:
Only a fractionally trained dean could foresee how a single afternoon would unravel. The plan was to computerize the exam hall, yet a breakdown of the main server displeased everyone. The story is true, though it sounds far-fetched, and properly told it should make you glance twice.

I had come to invigilate, a duty I expect to be dull. On the eve of the test, a rapid remittance from the secretariat had confirmed my pay, so I felt no envy. Students arrived in a long procession and would huddle against a bumpy wind, each carrying an umbrella against the rain, papers ready to sprawl across the top of every desk. One blond boy gripped a pair of scissors he had no legal reason to bring; a fussy girl clutched a tattered biography of a poet whose loyalty and patriotism had won him a strange notoriety. A toad of a man, the proctor, told them to skim no notes and to skip nothing on the page. He would utilize a strict, grammatical script for the rules, biting his lip after each line as if to interview the room with his eyes.

The room was meant to exhilarate the mind, not displease it. On the wall a faded carving and a school motto in disuse hung above the boarding-house list. Somewhere a clock ticked. To conclude the briefing, the dean read out each regulation in a sound, justifiable voice, his aim to guide rather than to scold. He invoked the heroine of an old tale, a near-idol whose courage, like a unicorn's, seemed virtual yet stirring.

Then the tremor came. It was a real, faint quake, felt far underground where alluvial soil and limestone lie. A male student's pen began to roll; another, overcome by fatigue, gave a tired crow of alarm. I had to confirm no one was hurt. Adverse rumor spread of smuggling and of a make-shift bomb, but I would not vex the room with panic. A crow flew past the window; a melon rolled off a desk and would surely fall and split its skin like a torn stitch. The dull routine, a killer of nerves, had turned thrilling. The principal, frantic yet refined, came round on a tram from the far campus to investigate.

No one was liable for the gravitation of events. The civilian crowd outside, fed false counsel by a criminal, nearly knocked down the gate. A herbalist offered ice-cream to calm the children; a nightingale-like singer hummed an overture to soothe them. The cubism on a nearby poster looked grim, its stitched edges torn. When help did come, we found the panic insufficient cause for alarm; the danger, we concluded, stemmed therefrom no real threat. Ultimately, with steady guidance, calm was restored. His report, signed with a careful rivet of ink, called it the ninth quiet ending to a noisy day, and the clan of weary staff went home to a deserved rest, the bathroom queue at last short, while Saturn rose pale over the rooftops.
translation:
只有一个仅受过一点点训练的系主任，才能预见一个下午会如何崩溃。计划是把考场电脑化，但主服务器的故障让所有人都不满。这件事是真的，尽管听起来很离谱，但若讲得得当，会让你多看两眼。

我来监考，这是一项我预料会很乏味的职责。考试前夕，秘书处一笔快捷的汇款确认了我的报酬，所以我毫无嫉妒。学生们排着长队进场，在颠簸的风中挤作一团，人人都拿着一把伞挡雨。一个金发男孩握着一把他根本没有合法理由带来的剪刀；一个挑剔的女孩紧攥着一本破旧的传记，传主是一位因忠诚与爱国而赢得奇怪名声的诗人。一个像蟾蜍般的人——那位监考——告诉他们不要偷看笔记，也不要跳过页上的任何内容。

这间屋子本该令人振奋，而非令人不快。墙上一幅褪色的雕刻和一句早已废弃的校训，挂在寄宿名单上方。某处一只钟在滴答。为了结束说明，系主任用稳健、有理的声音逐一读出每条规定，他的目的是引导而非责骂。他提起一个古老传说中的女主角，一个近乎偶像的人物，她的勇气如独角兽一般，虚幻却振奋人心。

然后震动来了。那是一场真实、微弱的地震，在冲积土与石灰岩深埋的地下被感知到。一个男生的笔开始滚动；另一个因疲惫而发出一声疲倦的惊呼。我得确认没有人受伤。关于走私和一枚临时炸弹的负面谣言四处传播，但我不愿用恐慌去骚扰这间屋子。一只乌鸦飞过窗口；一个甜瓜从桌上滚落。校长狂乱却不失文雅，从遥远的校区乘电车赶来调查。

没有人对这一连串事件的引力负责。外面的平民人群被一个罪犯灌输了错误的劝告，差点撞倒大门。一位草药师给孩子们送来冰淇淋安抚他们；一位夜莺般的歌手哼起一段序曲来抚慰他们。附近海报上的立体派画作看起来阴森，缝合的边缘已经破裂。等到救援真的赶到，我们发现这点恐慌不足以构成警报；我们断定，危险由此并未带来真正的威胁。最终，在稳定的引导下，平静得以恢复。他的报告用墨水仔细铆定签字，把这称作嘈杂一天里第九个安静的结尾，疲惫的一群工作人员各自回家好好休息，卫生间的队伍终于短了，而土星在屋顶上空苍白地升起。

text:
On the inaugural Monday of summer, I took a job at a small hotel in Japan, a marvelous old building with a wide patio and a tiled window that would shine in the morning sun. As an undergraduate with little money, I could not afford to starve, so I jumped at the vacancy the moment an agent confirmed it. The pay was equitable, the contract was incorporated into a tidy document, and a kindly sponsorship from the owner made the deal exceedingly fair.

My duties seemed minor but never let up. I had to itemize the goods in the pantry, mix batter for cake, fry an egg or boil a chicken, and insert fresh flowers into every vase. I learned to shun shortcuts and to resist the urge to skip a task, since any backlog would only sicken the schedule and trouble the staff. A restrictive house rule warned that no one should be oblivious to a guest's need, and I tried to honor it with a calm countenance and never a fiery temper.

The other recruit was a naughty boy whose timidity hid behind loud jokes. He would shamble across the lobby pushing a baby's stroller for a young mother, then snap a photo with a grin. Once he dropped a pebble into the gas burner as a prank and seemed seemingly proud of it, an idiot move that earned him a light slap of warning from the cook. I had to remember that he was the real culprit, yet I gave him the consolation of a second chance.

Guests arrived from everywhere. A deaf artist sketched the arctic light; a scholar of Islam discussed freedom and faith in the vernacular over tea; a singer with the voice of a nightingale practiced each syllable beside the organ in the hall. The amplification of her loudness filled the patio, and people would nod and confer in low tones, oblivious to time. We would still confer warrant for late check-ins, a reciprocal courtesy.

One afternoon a pipe burst, and the result was instant chaos. Molten residue from an old axle had clogged a cavity in the boiler, and a worker had to excavate the filthy clay around it. Water rose heavily, so I had to scoop it out and instantly divide the wet towels into a neat package for the laundry. An off-duty plumber, watching a hind leg of his dog twitch in sleep, refused to let the leak bewilder him. He said a steady crew should embody calm, never panic. We used a small calculator to itemize the cost of an amendment to the lease, then ran a shuttle to import spare parts. The franchise office bitterly disputed the bill, but the manager set a preset budget, fed the chickens by the hive, and ate from his appetite of patience. By the end my whole personality felt remade, and I would not slap that summer from memory; it had become, on a humble mat, a true import to my growing self.
translation:
夏天第一个星期一，我在日本一家小旅馆找了份工作，那是一栋很棒的老建筑，有宽阔的天井和一扇瓷砖窗，清晨阳光下闪闪发亮。作为一个没什么钱的本科生，我可不能挨饿，所以一位中介刚一确认有空缺，我就立刻抓住了机会。报酬公道，合同被并入一份整齐的文件，房东的一笔好心赞助让这桩交易格外公平。

我的职责看似不起眼，却从不停歇。我要把储藏室的物品逐项列出，调好做蛋糕的面糊，煎个蛋或煮只鸡，还要把鲜花插进每个花瓶。我学会了避开捷径、克制跳过任务的冲动，因为任何积压都只会让日程变糟、让员工烦恼。一条严格的旅馆规定警告说，谁都不该对客人的需求视而不见，我努力以平静的神情去恪守它，绝不动怒。

另一个新人是个调皮的男孩，他的胆怯藏在大声的玩笑后面。他会推着婴儿车蹒跚地穿过大堂帮一位年轻母亲，然后咧嘴一笑拍张照。有一次他把一颗小石子丢进煤气灶搞恶作剧，似乎还颇为得意，这种白痴举动招来厨师一记轻轻的警告耳光。我得记住他才是真正的罪魁祸首，但我还是给了他第二次机会作为安慰。

客人来自各地。一位失聪的画家画着北极的光线；一位研究伊斯兰教的学者用方言喝着茶谈论自由与信仰；一位嗓音如夜莺的歌手在大厅的管风琴旁练习每一个音节。她的洪亮经过放大充满了天井，人们会点头并低声商谈，浑然不觉时间流逝。我们仍会为迟到入住的客人开具许可，这是一种互惠的礼节。

一天下午，一根管子爆裂了。一根旧车轴上的熔渣堵住了锅炉的一个空腔，一名工人不得不挖开周围肮脏的黏土。我们用一个小计算器逐项算出修改租约的成本，然后开了一趟班车去进口备件。加盟总部对账单极为不满，但经理拒绝被弄糊涂；他设定了一份预设预算，在蜂箱旁喂了鸡，并以耐心的胃口面对一切。到夏末时，我整个人的性格仿佛被重塑，我不会把那个夏天从记忆中抹去；在那张朴素的草垫上，它已成为我成长中自我的真正馈赠。

text:
The deposition of fresh frost on the broad valley was reminiscent of my childhood on a native ranch. Last autumn I drove out to a get-together hosted by an elderly acquaintance, a distinguished and decent rancher whose generosity was as sincere as it was famous. He had sent a proper invitation, signed with a flowing signature, and I could predict the day would be a happy one as I took the approach road past the dock and the old rail line.

The ranch was a hive of honest work. At dawn the livestock would stir, and a hired retailer of feed arrived to mount a delivery, his truck rough on the uneven, bumpy track. I watched a worm wriggle in the ripe soil and a green sprout pry its way up through a chunk of softened earth. The rancher would pore over a seed catalogue, deciding which plant to grow, and he kept an old gun by the door against coyotes. He liked detailed statistics; he would argue, with a sensible humor, that the salability of his beef was a relatively steady, if middling, business, dependent on the duration of good weather and on a fair rebate from the market. To possess such land, he said, demanded that one check every figure and beware of every faulty calculation.

Inside, the kitchen smelled of soap and fresh bread. A ceramic bowl held a soluble powder for the meringue his niece was beating into a happy froth. We ate a hotdog each, then went to wash up. An Irish guest in a thick sweater told us, with rough good cheer, of an American pilgrim whose pry into family records had uncovered an heiress and a long-lost portrait. An onlooker, a quiet intellect, gave an interesting recital of a poem whose intonation and inclination toward melancholy made us all shrug and smile.

Not everything was easy. A nasty bug had bitten the herd, and the gene pool, the rancher feared, had grown too thin; the radium-bright sun of the equator seemed to mock his cumbersome, demanding routine. He had a scheme to disguise the loss and to redistribute the strong calves, but he would forbid any plan that proved immoral. Regretfully, a constraint on water put real privacy and rest out of reach.

We knocked off at dusk, and a sudden knock at the door announced a late guest. My playmate from years past, now a selfish but charming man, liked to shuffle a deck of cards by the tile stove. A thinner, aching cold crept in, and I felt a pang of carelessness for having stayed so long. Yet the social warmth, granted unconditionally, was a fine hobby of the heart, and I would approve of such a gathering again, without trial or doubt.
translation:
新霜沉积在宽阔的山谷上，令我回想起在故乡牧场度过的童年。去年秋天，我开车去参加一位年长熟人举办的聚会，他是一位德高望重、正派的牧场主，慷慨之心与其名声一样真诚。他寄来了一份正式的请柬，签着流畅的签名，当我沿着小路驶过码头和旧铁路线时，便能预见这将是愉快的一天。

这座牧场是诚实劳作的繁忙之地。黎明时分，牲畜开始骚动，一位受雇的饲料零售商赶来卸货，他的卡车在坑洼不平的小路上颠簸。我看着一条虫子在成熟的土壤里蠕动，一株嫩芽从一块松软的泥土中艰难钻出。牧场主喜欢详尽的统计数据；他会带着明智的幽默争辩说，他牛肉的销路取决于好天气的持续时间，以及市场上公道的回扣。他说，要拥有这样的土地，就必须提防每一次错误的计算。

屋里飘着肥皂和新鲜面包的香味。一个陶瓷碗里盛着可溶的粉末，是他侄女打成蛋白霜的原料。我们各吃了一个热狗，然后洗了碗。一位穿着厚毛衣的爱尔兰客人，带着粗犷的好心情，给我们讲了一个美国朝圣者的故事，他对家族记录的探究揭出了一位女继承人和一幅久已失落的肖像。一位旁观者，一个安静的才智之士，朗诵了一首诗，其语调和忧郁的倾向让我们都耸耸肩，会心微笑。

并非事事顺利。一种讨厌的虫子叮咬了牛群，牧场主担心基因库已变得太单薄；赤道般镭光刺眼的太阳似乎在嘲笑他繁重而苛刻的日常。他有一个计划，想掩盖损失并重新分配强壮的小牛，但他禁止任何被证明不道德的方案。遗憾的是，水源的限制让真正的私密与休息变得遥不可及。

黄昏时我们收了工。我多年前的玩伴，如今是个自私却迷人的男人，在瓷砖火炉旁洗着一副扑克牌。一股更瘦削、酸痛的寒意悄然袭来，我为自己逗留如此之久而生出一丝懊悔。然而那无条件给予的社交温暖，是心灵的一种美好嗜好，我愿意再次赞同这样的聚会，毫无疑虑或迟疑。

text:
Every year during the long winding road home for Thanksgiving Day, our homely Canadian family would gather in a rented farmhouse to celebrate a festival of food and memory. The rental was plain but warm, and the key to the front door hung from a faded ribbon like a small private treasure. I cannot bear to miss it; the anticipation alone could enchant me for weeks.

My grandmother, a proud lady whose marriage had lasted fifty years, ruled the kitchen with quiet gravity. She would govern the stove, harden the toffee, and puff flour across a tray as she rolled the filling for the pies. Namely, she made three: pumpkin, apple, and a Danish pastry she had learned to bake abroad. She liked to cite an old written recipe, an heirloom whose ink had begun to fade, and she would not modify it for anything, not even to cut a single calorie. To loosen the dough she added cream, and the rich, fabulous smell could fascinate any child in the house.

My uncle, a reticent man, worked in telecommunication and could explain any system of automation, from a barrel-shaped engine to the machinery that ran the local ministry of transport. He spoke of progress with pride, citing protectionism and indemnity clauses as if they were poetry. He could even explain each abbreviation on a circuit board entirely from memory, pausing only to play a slow tune on the upright piano. He once showed me an odometer he had taken from a deceased neighbor's car, hoping to reclaim and restore it, and a coupon book, a monthly rebate scheme, that he kept like a fossil of thriftier days. He never let his curiosity falter.

After dinner we played games. A comic cousin would mimic a panther creeping, then a frog leaping, while the youngest cradled an ivory chess piece like a sled-bound toy. We held a contest of riddles; the prize was a globe and a worn map of Rome. I cannot disentangle one memory from another, for they all puff together like smoke.

Not every year was easy. One winter influenza, a sharp respiratory illness, left two of us sick and mournful, and a continental cold snap froze the tar on the road. An ominous telephone call once warned of a smuggler caught at the border, an immoral man whose retrial in court was front-page news; the case, undeniable in its detail, had to be settled by a fair measure of law. We would mail a card to the lonesome, the rose-cheeked nursery children, and to relatives who could not travel.

Finally, plumb tired and happy, we would throw the last logs on the fire with a thick glove to guard our hands. An aquatic calm settled, as if the whole globe had paused in motion. Like a slow, steady puff of breath, the evening closed; it was available to us only once a year, etc., yet it never lost its meaning, and the reproach of distance melted away.
translation:
每年感恩节,我们沿着漫长蜿蜒的回家之路返乡时,这个朴实的加拿大家庭都会聚在一间租来的农舍里,庆祝一场关于美食与回忆的节日。这租来的房子简朴却温暖,前门钥匙系在一条褪色的丝带上,像一件小小的私人珍宝。我无法忍受错过它;光是这份期待就能让我陶醉好几个星期。

我的祖母是一位骄傲的女士,她的婚姻已经维系了五十年,她以一种安静的庄重统治着厨房。她会掌管炉灶,把太妃糖熬硬,一边把馅料擀进馅饼一边让面粉喷洒在烤盘上。也就是说,她做三种:南瓜派、苹果派,还有一种她在国外学会烤的丹麦酥皮点心。她喜欢引用一份古老的手写食谱,那是一件墨迹开始褪色的传家宝,无论如何她都不会修改它,哪怕只是少放一卡路里。为了让面团松软,她会加奶油,那浓郁奇妙的香味能让屋里任何一个孩子着迷。

我的叔叔是个沉默寡言的人,从事电信工作,能解释任何自动化系统,从桶形发动机到运营当地交通部的机械。他谈起进步时满怀自豪,引用贸易保护主义和赔偿条款仿佛它们是诗。他曾给我看一只从已故邻居车上拆下来的里程表,还有一本优惠券册,一个按月返利的方案,他把它当作节俭年代的化石珍藏。他的好奇心从不动摇。

晚饭后我们玩游戏。一个滑稽的表亲会模仿黑豹潜行,然后学青蛙跳跃,而最小的孩子像抱着雪橇玩具一样捧着一枚象牙棋子。我们举行猜谜比赛;奖品是一个地球仪和一张破旧的罗马地图。我无法把一段记忆与另一段分开,因为它们都像烟一样喷涌交融。

并非每年都顺利。有一年流感——一种凶猛的呼吸道疾病——让我们两人病倒、心情忧伤,而一场大陆性的寒潮冻住了路面上的柏油。曾有一通不祥的电话警告说边境抓到一名走私者,那个不道德之人的重审成了头条新闻;此案细节确凿无疑,只能由公正的法律尺度来裁决。我们会寄一张卡片给孤独的人,给那些脸蛋红润的托儿所孩子,以及无法远行的亲戚。

最后,累得筋疲力尽却心满意足,我们会把最后几块木柴扔进火里。一种水一般的宁静降临,仿佛整个地球都在运动中停顿了。像一口缓慢平稳的呼吸,夜晚落幕;它每年只对我们开放一次,如此等等,却从未失去它的意义,而距离带来的责备也烟消云散。

text:
I still remember the hubbub of my grandfather's carpentry shop, a place that could muffle the noise of the windy street and replace it with the steady flow of honest work. As a boy of seventeen I would bike there after school, hungry for the warmth and serenity of the four cluttered walls. He kept the place in optimal order, every chisel and saw on par with the next, every accessory in its proper category. An old aphorism hung above the door, wrought in iron: a steadfast hand and a logical mind together denote a true craftsman.

Grandfather was a renowned weaver of wood, though he was color-blind and could not always trust his eyes. He would consult a worn manual, calculate each angle, and adhere to a plan that was always definitive. To prepare a fresh board he would measure twice, his attention never wavering. I watched, in something close to ecstasy, as a plain plank was transformed downwards from rough timber into a thing of beauty, and the joy of it was inextricable from my earliest memory.

He disliked drudgery for its own sake, yet he refused to relinquish patience. 'Luck is unavoidable,' he would say, 'but you cannot bait it; you must earn it.' He had no intention to deceive a customer, and any flimsy or unsuitable job he would tear apart and begin again. He never showed indifference to a flaw. A single misaligned rib of a chair could cause havoc to the whole frame, and he refused to conform to shoddy taste merely to win patronage.

The shop sold many things. A king of customers, a wealthy man, once placed an invitation to build a cabinet adorned with carved leaves, a cardinal piece for his hall. Grandfather wrought it so the joints were perfectly congruent, then sent me on an errand to inform the buyer. The man's delight was a small victory, an advantage of good name that money could not buy.

Over the years a quiet disintegration set in. His old washing-machine broke; a leak in the roof let evaporation rot a beam; the displacement of the trade by automation made the awkward future hard to translate into hope. Yet he remained courageous. He would not swerve from his quest, nor lock the door on his calling. A manifold of small mushroom-like worries could occur, but he met each with an easy grace.

I am forever indebted to him. When a child would toddle in and a dog would bark and paw at a quart pail, he would smile, dose out a sweet, and adorn the moment with a story. His shop was a kind of forum, a place of wholesome talk where every outlook met. He had a clear conception of craft, and he would use no shortcut; a clumsy joint, notably, could deflate his pride faster than a snapped sword. He gave my mental world its shape, and his holding of one hectare of dignity, mechanical and human at once, taught me what it means to be a friend to one's own work.
translation:
我至今仍记得祖父木工坊里的喧闹，那地方能把街上的风声压下去，代之以诚实劳作的稳稳节奏。十七岁那年，我放学后会骑车去那儿，渴望那四壁杂乱却温暖宁静的氛围。他把店铺收拾得井井有条，每把凿子和锯子都摆得整整齐齐，每件配件都归在恰当的类别里。门上方挂着一句铁打的古老格言：稳健的手和缜密的头脑，二者合一，才显出真正的工匠。

祖父是远近闻名的木工巧匠，尽管他色盲，眼睛并不总能信赖。他会查阅一本破旧的手册，计算每一个角度，并坚持一份始终确定无疑的方案。为了备好一块新木板，他会量两遍，注意力从不松懈。我看着一块普通的木板从粗糙的原木被一点点削制成美丽之物，那种近乎狂喜的喜悦，与我最早的记忆密不可分。

他厌恶为苦工而苦工，却从不肯放弃耐心。他常说：'运气无可避免，但你不能去诱它；你得去挣得它。'他无意欺骗顾客，任何粗劣或不合格的活儿，他都会拆掉重做。他对瑕疵从不漠然。一把椅子上一根没对齐的横档，就可能毁掉整个框架，他也绝不为迎合粗劣品味、博取主顾而将就。

店里卖各种东西。一位富有的大主顾曾下了订单，要做一只镌刻着叶纹的橱柜，是他厅堂里最重要的一件。祖父把它做得接榫严丝合缝，然后差我去跑腿通知买主。那人的欢喜是一场小小的胜利，是金钱买不到的好名声带来的优势。

岁月里，一种悄然的衰败开始了。他的旧洗衣机坏了；屋顶漏雨，水汽蒸腾,腐蚀了一根梁；自动化对这门行当的取代，使得别扭的未来难以化作希望。然而他依旧勇敢。他不肯偏离自己的追求，也不肯对自己的天职关上门。无数蘑菇般冒出的小忧虑会接踵而至，但他都以从容的优雅去面对。

我永远感激他。每当一个孩子蹒跚走进来，一条狗吠叫着用爪子去扒一只一夸脱的桶，他便会微笑，分出一颗糖果，再用一个故事点缀那一刻。他的店铺像是一个论坛，是个人人畅谈的健康之地，各种观点都在此相会。他对手艺有清晰的认知，从不走捷径；一处笨拙的接榫，尤其会比折断的剑更快地令他的自尊泄了气。他塑造了我精神世界的模样，而他对一公顷尊严的坚守——既是机械的，又是人性的——教会了我何为对自己的事业忠诚。

text:
It is unlikely that anyone in our provincial town will forget the hard-working boxer who once trained behind the old cathedral. He was born in a solitary brick house near the airport, the son of a gardener, and from an early age he had an absolute, heartfelt motive: to escape the dropout's fate and make something terrific of his life. His physique was ungainly at first, chiefly because a childhood surgery had left one shoulder stiff, but he learned to pronounce each ambition aloud, dwelling on every vowel as if the very sound were a referent for his dream, a quiet creed of humanism that put human effort above all. Years of experience, stacked one upon the next like a tall stack of worn gloves, would standardize his own resolve.

He would bypass the noisy cafeteria and ride his bicycle past the zebra crossing to a humble gym above a kitchen that always smelled of butter and toast. There he would devote whole afternoons to the chase of skill, working a single tactic until it ran smoothly. A bossy old coach, a former commissioner of the regional league, did not spare the rebuke; his harsh words could seize a man's pride, yet they were never pure cruelty. 'Comply with the protocol,' he would say, 'or simply leave.'

The coach loved to elaborate. His elaboration on footwork was a kind of verse, and he could compile a whole canon of drills from memory. He taught the boy to understand each facet of the ring, to contrast a clumsy lunge with a clean one, and to ascertain an opponent's weakness before the first horn. 'Hatred has no place here,' he said. 'Neutrality of mind, and a fruitful patience, will produce the win.' It was a fallacy, he insisted, that rage alone could shoot a man to victory; that was an awful and prohibitive lie.

Life outside the gym was harder. Inflation had made even postage dear, and the cost of clean linen and good fabric for his robe was nearly prohibitive. A provincial reporter, eager for an issue to fill the page, once wrote a doubtful piece that seemed to trespass on his privacy, citing a faked facsimile of a contract. The boy felt mean and blue for a day, then would refresh his focus and let the slight pass like a breeze.

On Boxing-Day, the eleven-year mark of his career and his eleventh fight, he stepped onto the stage. The hall, thick with smog from outside and the heat of the crowd, would hinge on a single round. He would not hurl wild blows; he moved in a calm orbit, a compound of speed and grace, until the executive judges, with absolute fairness, named him the winner. A typhoon of cheers rose. Royalty itself, they joked, would envy such comfort and delight. He sipped a cold beverage, qualify at last for the national stage, and offered a quiet specimen of thanks, a delicacy of feeling that no isolation or fame could ever take from him.
translation:
我们这座外省小镇上，大概没有人会忘记那位曾在老教堂后面训练的勤奋拳手。他出生在机场附近一栋孤零零的砖房里，是一位园丁的儿子，从小他就怀着一个绝对而真挚的动机：摆脱辍学者的命运，让自己的人生有所成就。起初他的体格笨拙，但他学会把每一个志向大声说出来，仿佛要把自己的决心规范化。

他会绕开喧闹的食堂，骑着自行车经过斑马线，来到一间厨房楼上的简陋拳馆，那厨房里总飘着黄油和烤面包的味道。在那里他会把整个下午奉献给对技艺的追逐，反复练一个战术，直到它运转流畅。一位专横的老教练——地区联赛的前任专员——从不吝惜责备；他刻薄的话能攫住一个人的自尊，但那从不是纯粹的残忍。'遵守规程，'他会说，'否则干脆走人。'

教练喜欢详加阐述。他对步法的阐述像是一种韵文，他能凭记忆编纂出一整套训练教规。他教这男孩理解拳台的每一个方面，把笨拙的出击与利落的出击作对比，并在第一声号角响起前就探明对手的弱点。'这里没有仇恨的位置，'他说。'心境的中立，加上富有成效的耐心，才能赢下比赛。'他坚称，以为单凭狂怒就能把一个人射向胜利，那是一种谬误；那是个可怕而要命的谎言。

拳馆之外的生活更艰难。通货膨胀让连邮费都变贵，做拳袍要用的干净亚麻和好布料几乎贵得离谱。一位急于找个话题填版面的外省记者，曾写过一篇可疑的报道，似乎侵犯了他的隐私，还引用了一份伪造的合同传真件。男孩沮丧消沉了一天，随后重整专注，让这点轻慢像微风一样掠过。

在节礼日，他职业生涯的第十一场比赛中，他登上了擂台。大厅里弥漫着外面飘来的烟雾和人群的热气，胜负系于一个回合。他不会胡乱挥拳；他在一个沉稳的轨道里移动，是速度与优雅的结合，直到执行裁判们以绝对的公正宣布他获胜。一阵欢呼如台风般涌起。他们打趣说，连王室都会羡慕这样的舒适与喜悦。他啜了一口冰凉的饮料，终于有资格登上全国舞台，并献上一份安静的谢意样本，一种任何孤独或名声都夺不走的细腻情感。

text:
How a refined governess from Iceland came to hold a copyright on a super wireless generator is a tale worth the daily grind of telling. Her name, hereinafter the Inventor, was known across society for her analytical mind and her steady, almost ethereal calm. She never let a single germ of doubt grind her down. Each daytime she would scramble up a massive hill at a brisk pace, trot home, and abide by a strict budget, principally to fund her secret undertaking.

The core of her work was a translucent filament that, when fed a dual current, could weld itself to a stainless bracket and glow with a light pliable enough to bend. She had to calibrate it with great detail, adapt the design again and again, and counterbalance the inefficiency of the early models. A frantic period followed; she would grunt over the equations, paint diagrams across the wall, and treat each failure as a lesson rather than a defeat. Her deep understanding of physics made safety her first priority.

Her sister, a fisherman's wife, and her young niece helped without complaint. They would canvass the village for parts, declare every honest cost, and abide by a managerial discipline that no thief or careless hand could spoil. The Inventor took the initiative to elect a small board, responsible and virtuous, to guard against piracy of her idea. A brutal controversy soon arose: a rival in America, negligent of all ethics, tried to redistribute her plans under a false name. The undermentioned clause of her contract, however, gave her the affirmative right to fight back.

The project would ultimately culminate on a quiet evening when Venus hung bright in the sky. With a single clap she switched on the generator; a hose of coolant hissed, and the device hummed toward infinity. Her champion supporters cheered. The light, steadily brightening, seemed to coincide with the dawn of something new. It was not a missile of war but an inducement to peace, a thing of permanence rather than spectacle.

That night she ate a simple meal of beef and a vitamin tonic, then arranged a bouquet on the windowsill in remembrance of her late mother. A doctor friend rode in on the last tram to say hello and to tackle a small repair. She would not strew her success across the headlines; instead she set a modest target, accept the praise eastward toward the harbour, and let a rhyme of gratitude settle in her heart. The dye of her ambition had set; her livelihood, built on the firm constitution of patience, was finally her own.
translation:
一位来自冰岛、举止文雅的女家庭教师，是如何拥有一台超级无线发电机的版权的，这是个值得费力讲述的故事。她的名字——下文称为发明家——以其善于分析的头脑和稳定得近乎飘逸的沉静而闻名于社会。她从不让一丝怀疑的萌芽把自己磨垮。每个白天她都会快步爬上一座巨大的山丘，再小跑回家，并恪守严格的预算，主要是为了资助她那秘密的事业。

她工作的核心是一根半透明的灯丝，当通入双向电流时，它能把自己焊接到一个不锈钢托架上，并发出柔韧到可以弯曲的光。她必须极其细致地校准它，一次次调整设计，并抵消早期型号的低效。一段疯狂的时期随之而来；她会对着方程嘟囔，把图表画满墙壁，把每一次失败都当作教训而非挫败。

她的姐姐——一位渔夫的妻子——和她年幼的侄女毫无怨言地帮忙。她们会到村里四处搜集零件，如实申报每一笔诚实的成本，并遵守一种任何窃贼或粗心之手都无法破坏的管理纪律。发明家主动提议选出一个小小的董事会，既负责又正直，以防她的创意被剽窃。一场残酷的争议很快爆发：美国的一个竞争者，毫无道德顾忌，企图用假名重新散布她的方案。然而，她合同中下述的那一条款，赋予了她反击的正当权利。

这个项目最终在一个金星高悬、明亮的安静夜晚迎来高潮。她一拍手就开启了发电机；一根冷却液软管发出嘶嘶声，装置朝着无限嗡鸣运转。她的拥护者们欢呼。那道光稳稳地变亮，仿佛与某种崭新事物的黎明相吻合。它不是战争的导弹，而是和平的诱因，是一种持久之物，而非一时的奇观。

那夜她吃了一顿简单的牛肉和一剂维生素补品，然后在窗台上摆了一束花，以纪念她已故的母亲。一位医生朋友乘最后一班电车赶来打招呼，并顺手处理一处小小的修理。她不会把自己的成功撒满头条；相反，她设定了一个适度的目标，朝着海港方向向东接受赞誉，让一段感恩的韵律在心中安顿下来。她抱负的染色已经定型；她的生计，建立在耐心这坚实的体质之上，终于属于她自己。

text:
The annual charity bazaar in our oriental seaside town was a genial, energetic affair, and this year it carried a special weight of commemoration. We held it in remembrance of a beloved missionary who had spent her life helping the homeless, and the preparation alone took months. Without her, the work would have been infinitely harder, and her introduction of small kindnesses had, over the years, helped to fortify the whole community.

The square was a paradise of color. Stalls lined every wall, each one suitable for a different trade. One booth sold tulip bulbs and seeds; a second offered an up-to-date pictorial catalogue printed by a duty-free importer. An elderly tailor had hauled out his old sewing-machine and a battered typewriter, and beside him a clerk made photostatic copies for a fractional fee. A vendor of cameras let children peer through one camera's clean lens, while a fishmonger laid out a fresh halibut on ice. I bought a plain shirt and an innocuous little ashtray as a gift.

The day was not without its dilemma. A frequent visitor, a man once nearly bankrupt, arrived homesick and weary; his confidence had grown inferior to his old vigor, and he seemed ready to doze in a corner rather than join the fun. We urged him, urgently but gently, to cope and to receive the warmth of the crowd. Slowly his grit returned. He helped irrigate the flower beds, carried a child piggyback through the laughter, and would clench his fists in a gesture of renewed greatness.

There were games of every figure. A naval veteran ran a ring-toss; a strong-arm competition tested who could combat fatigue the longest, the championship decided by a single concurrent throw. Energy seemed to swell and reel through the square. Now and then a weird hush would halt the noise as a melody drifted from a fan-cooled bandstand, only for the din to activate again like a capillary catching the tide.

Profits would fund a clinic. A doctor explained, with quiet piety, how a scarce medication, mixed with glycerin, could keep a wound from turning septic, and how iron in the blood could oxidize and darken if a patient went untreated. He cited one instance of a sailor whose arm, crushed by a falling shaft, had healed only after a long confinement in bed, the daily irritation of the dressings a small price for survival. An injure to the body, he said, must be met second by second, irrespective of cost, lest a small hurt swell into a devastating recurrence that could devastate a family. The effectiveness of early care was, he insisted, appreciably greater.

By night, the lamps glowed at their peak over the edge of the harbour. A child fell asleep on a bookcase bench near a gear-driven music box; a peak of joy settled over us all. There had been no burial of hope here, only its revival. The product of our labor was nought less than belonging, and as people drifted home around the quiet streets, no one needed to apologize for a single tear of gratitude.
translation:
我们这座东方海滨小镇一年一度的慈善义卖会是个亲切而充满活力的盛事，今年它还承载着特殊的纪念意义。我们举办它是为了缅怀一位深受爱戴的传教士，她一生致力于帮助无家可归者，光是筹备就花了好几个月。没有她，这项工作会艰难无数倍，而多年来她带来的种种小小善举，已让整个社区变得更加坚强。

广场是色彩的天堂。每面墙边都排着摊位，每个摊位都适合一种不同的行当。一个摊位卖郁金香球茎和种子；第二个摊位提供一份由免税进口商印制的最新图文目录。一位年长的裁缝搬出了他那台旧缝纫机和一台破旧的打字机，他旁边一位职员以零头的费用提供影印复印件。一个卖相机的小贩让孩子们透过干净的镜头张望，而一个鱼贩则把一条新鲜的大比目鱼摆在冰上。我买了一件朴素的衬衫和一个无害的小烟灰缸当礼物。

这一天也并非没有难题。一位常客，一个曾几近破产的男人，带着思乡和疲惫到来；他的自信已不及往日的精力，似乎宁愿在角落里打盹，也不愿加入欢乐。我们急切而温和地劝他振作起来，去接受人群的温暖。渐渐地，他的毅力回来了。他帮忙浇灌花圃，背着一个孩子穿过笑声，攥紧拳头，做出重焕生机的姿态。

各种各样的游戏都有。一位海军老兵主持套圈；一场比力气的比赛考验谁能最久地对抗疲劳，冠军由一次并行的投掷决定。活力似乎在广场上涨潮、回旋。时不时会有一阵诡异的寂静让喧闹停下，因为一段旋律从风扇降温的乐台飘来，随后嘈杂声又像毛细血管接住潮水般重新涌起。

收益将用来资助一家诊所。一位医生带着安静的虔诚解释道，一种稀缺的药物，与甘油混合后，可以防止伤口化脓；血液中的铁若得不到治疗，会氧化并变暗。他说，身体的伤害必须分秒必争地处理，不计代价，免得小伤膨胀成毁灭性的复发，进而毁掉一个家庭。他坚称，及早护理的效果要明显得多。

入夜时分，灯火在海港边缘升至最盛。一个孩子在一台齿轮驱动的音乐盒旁的书架长椅上睡着了；一阵喜悦的高峰笼罩着我们所有人。这里没有希望的葬礼，只有它的复苏。我们劳动的成果不亚于归属感，当人们绕着安静的街道渐渐回家时，没有人需要为一滴感激的泪水道歉。
